topics.title: Introduction to IoT - Physical Design of IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST834 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to IoT - Physical Design of IoT # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition — The Physical Design of IoT The **Physical Design of IoT** refers to the actual hardware architecture, tangible components, and physical arrangement of devices (often called "Things") that collectively enable the Internet of Things to sense, communicate, compute, and actuate within a real-world environment. It is the "body" of the IoT system, in contrast to the logical or software design, which represents the "mind." In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Physical Design of IoT encompasses the specifications, block-level interconnections, power considerations, communication interfaces, and hardware constraints of the four universal building blocks of any IoT deployment: - **Things / Devices (Sensors and Actuators)** - **Communication / Network Infrastructure (Gateways, Routers, Protocols)** - **Edge / Fog / Cloud Computing Hardware (Servers, Storage, Processors)** - **Application / User Interface Hardware (Mobile, Displays, Embedded Boards)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Physical Design is a foundational concept in Module 1. Examiners frequently test the student's ability to *draw and label* the generic IoT block diagram, identify each block's role, and state at least one example of a hardware component for each block. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Imagine IoT as a **human body**: | IoT Block | Human Body Counterpart | Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Sensors (Eyes, Ears, Skin) | Sense organs | Collect raw data from environment | | Actuators (Muscles) | Limbs and muscles | Perform physical actions | | Microcontroller/Processor (Brain) | Brain | Local decision-making | | Communication Network (Nerves) | Nervous system | Transmit signals | | Cloud (Memory) | Long-term memory | Store historical data | | Application (Consciousness) | Conscious mind | Present information to the user | Just as the body cannot function without a heart pumping blood, an IoT system cannot function without a *power source*, a *communication medium*, and *sensors/actuators* — the three pillars of the physical design. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** The "Physical Design of IoT" simply answers the question — *What actual chips, boards, wires, and devices are physically present in an IoT system, and how are they connected?* If you can touch it, plug it in, or hold a chip of it, it is part of the Physical Design. ## 1.3 The Generic Block Architecture of IoT The KTU-endorsed generic architecture represents IoT as **four interconnected physical layers**: 1. **Sensing / Perception Layer** — Hardware sensors and actuators interacting with the physical world. 2. **Network / Communication Layer** — Physical transmission media (wired/wireless), routers, gateways. 3. **Middleware / Processing Layer** — Edge processors, fog nodes, cloud data centers. 4. **Application / Business Layer** — End-user hardware (smartphones, PCs, dashboards) and presentation logic. A single IoT deployment is not bound to use all four layers in a strict order; the layers may be co-located (e.g., a sensor with built-in processing is "sensing + middleware" in one chip). However, the **logical separation** of concerns is the hallmark of the Physical Design. ## 1.4 Standard Hardware Metrics Used in IoT Physical Design When comparing IoT hardware, KTU students must remember the following standard metrics: - **Power Consumption (mW / W)** — Critical for battery-powered nodes. - **Operating Voltage (V)** — Typically **3.3 V** or **5 V** for embedded boards. - **Communication Range (m)** — From centimeters (NFC) to kilometers (LoRa). - **Data Rate (bps / kbps / Mbps)** — Speed of information exchange. - **Latency (ms)** — Delay between sensing and action. - **Form Factor (mm³)** — Physical size of the device. - **Operating Temperature (°C)** — Industrial range: **−40 °C to +85 °C**. > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Whenever you describe a sensor/actuator, always quote its **range, accuracy, and interface protocol** (e.g., I²C, SPI, UART) — examiners reward this discipline with full marks. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of the Four Physical Layers ### Layer 1: Things / Sensing Layer (The Hands and Eyes of IoT) This layer contains the **physical transducers** that interact with the environment. It is sub-divided into two classes: - **Sensors** — Convert a physical parameter into a measurable electrical signal. *Examples:* Temperature (DHT11, LM35), Humidity, Pressure (BMP180), Gas (MQ-2), Light (LDR), Motion (PIR HC-SR501), Proximity (IR), Accelerometer (ADXL345), Gyroscope (MPU6050), GPS (NEO-6M), Camera (OV2640), RFID reader, Biometric sensors. - **Actuators** — Convert an electrical signal into a physical action. *Examples:* DC/Servo/Stepper Motors, Relays, Solenoids, LEDs, Buzzers, Heaters, Pumps, Pneumatic/Hydraulic valves. **Microcontroller Unit (MCU)** is the local "brain" of the sensing node. Popular low-power MCUs in IoT include **ESP32, ESP8266, Arduino UNO (ATmega328P), STM32, MSP430, and Raspberry Pi Pico**. These execute the embedded firmware that reads sensor data and transmits it. ### Layer 2: Network / Communication Layer (The Nervous System) This layer comprises all **physical communication interfaces** that move data between the Things, gateways, and the cloud. It is broadly split into: - **Short-Range Wired Communication:** UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, RS-232, RS-485, Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), USB. - **Short-Range Wireless Communication:** Bluetooth / BLE (IEEE 802.15.1), ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4), Z-Wave, NFC, Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax), RFID. - **Long-Range Wireless Communication:** LoRaWAN, Sigfox, NB-IoT, LTE-M, 5G NR, Satellite IoT. - **Wired Backhaul:** Fiber-optic, DSL, Ethernet, PLC (Power Line Communication). **Gateway Hardware** is the physical bridge between constrained sensor nodes (often using lightweight protocols like ZigBee or BLE) and the IP-based internet (Wi-Fi/Ethernet). Examples include Raspberry Pi-based gateways, industrial IoT gateways (e.g., Cisco IC3000), and edge routers. ### Layer 3: Middleware / Processing Layer (The Memory and Reasoning) This is where **raw data is transformed into meaningful information**. It consists of: - **Edge Computing Devices** — Process data near the source (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson Nano, Google Coral Edge TPU, Intel NUC, Raspberry Pi 4). - **Fog Computing Nodes** — Intermediate layer between edge and cloud (e.g., industrial PCs, micro-data centers). - **Cloud Servers** — Large-scale storage and compute (AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine, private OpenStack clusters). - **Storage Hardware** — SSDs, HDDs, NVMe, in-memory databases (Redis hardware nodes). ### Layer 4: Application Layer (The Face of IoT) The application layer is the **physical interface** through which humans consume IoT services: - **Mobile Devices** (Smartphones, Tablets) - **Desktop Workstations** - **Smart Displays** (Amazon Echo Show, Google Nest Hub) - **Wearable Hardware** (Smartwatches, AR/VR headsets) - **Voice Assistants** (smart speakers) ## 2.2 Generic IoT Equation — Linking Hardware to Data Flow Although IoT is not a closed-form mathematical discipline, a useful engineering relationship that governs the physical design is the **Node-Resource Trade-off Equation**: $$ P_{\text{node}} = V_{\text{supply}} \times I_{\text{active}} \times t_{\text{active}} + V_{\text{supply}} \times I_{\text{sleep}} \times t_{\text{sleep}} $$ Where: - $P_{\text{node}}$ = total average energy consumed by a node (in Joules or Watt-hours). - $V_{\text{supply}}$ = supply voltage (typically **3.3 V** or **5 V**). - $I_{\text{active}}$ = current drawn during active sensing/transmission (mA). - $I_{\text{sleep}}$ = current drawn during sleep/duty-cycled mode (µA). - $t_{\text{active}}$ and $t_{\text{sleep}}$ = respective time durations. The **battery lifetime** of an IoT node is then: $$ T_{\text{lifetime}} = \frac{C_{\text{battery}} \times V_{\text{battery}}}{P_{\text{node}}} $$ Where $C_{\text{battery}}$ is battery capacity in mAh. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Unit | Physical Significance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $V_{\text{supply}}$ | Operating supply voltage of node | V (Volt) | Determines logic level compatibility | | $I_{\text{active}}$ | Active mode current draw | mA | Power when sensor is sampling/transmitting | | $I_{\text{sleep}}$ | Sleep mode current draw | µA | Power when MCU is in low-power mode | | $P_{\text{node}}$ | Total node power consumption | mW | Determines battery sizing | | $T_{\text{lifetime}}$ | Battery / node lifetime | hours, days, years | Critical for remote deployments | | $C_{\text{battery}}$ | Battery capacity | mAh | Total charge stored in cell | | $R_{\text{data}}$ | Data rate of communication | bps / kbps / Mbps | Throughput of link | | $L_{\text{latency}}$ | End-to-end delay | ms | Time from sensor to user | | $f_{\text{clk}}$ | MCU clock frequency | MHz | Dictates processing speed | | $N_{\text{node}}$ | Number of nodes in network | — | Scalability metric | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** A common KTU 14-mark question asks to **calculate the battery lifetime** of an IoT node given supply voltage, sleep/active currents, and duty cycle. Master the equations above. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Physical Design decisions directly influence: - **Smart Agriculture** — Soil moisture sensors with LoRa modules enable multi-km range over a single AA battery lasting **5+ years**. - **Healthcare Wearables** — Low-power BLE + bio-sensors enable 7-day heart-rate monitoring. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — Vibration sensors on motors with edge gateways reduce cloud bandwidth by **80 %**. - **Smart Cities** — Air-quality sensors networked via NB-IoT cover entire metropolitan areas. - **Smart Homes** — Wi-Fi/BLE smart bulbs with built-in MCUs demonstrate co-located sensing + application layers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The choice between *Edge*, *Fog*, and *Cloud* processing in the physical design is driven by the **latency-energy-cost** triangle. A physical design that processes everything on cloud is simple but energy-hungry on the link; a design that processes on edge is complex but energy-efficient and low-latency. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Node Battery Lifetime Formula **Problem Setup (typical KTU numerical):** A wireless IoT sensor node operates at $V_{\text{supply}} = 3.3 \, \text{V}$. The active current is $I_{\text{active}} = 80 \, \text{mA}$ and the sleep current is $I_{\text{sleep}} = 20 \, \mu\text{A}$. The node is active for $t_{\text{active}} = 1\% $ of the time (duty cycle). The battery is rated at $C_{\text{battery}} = 2400 \, \text{mAh}$ at $V_{\text{battery}} = 3.7 \, \text{V}$. Find the **battery lifetime in years**. ### Step 1: Convert the active/sleep time over a one-hour reference window We assume a **1-hour operational window** to simplify. $$ t_{\text{active}} = 0.01 \times 3600 \, \text{s} = 36 \, \text{s} $$ $$ t_{\text{sleep}} = (1 - 0.01) \times 3600 \, \text{s} = 3564 \, \text{s} $$ ### Step 2: Compute energy consumed in active mode $$ E_{\text{active}} = V_{\text{supply}} \times I_{\text{active}} \times t_{\text{active}} $$ Substitute values: $$ E_{\text{active}} = 3.3 \times 80 \times 10^{-3} \times 36 $$ $$ E_{\text{active}} = 3.3 \times 0.080 \times 36 $$ $$ E_{\text{active}} = 0.264 \times 36 = 9.504 \, \text{J (per hour)} $$ ### Step 3: Compute energy consumed in sleep mode $$ E_{\text{sleep}} = V_{\text{supply}} \times I_{\text{sleep}} \times t_{\text{sleep}} $$ Substitute values: $$ E_{\text{sleep}} = 3.3 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 3564 $$ $$ E_{\text{sleep}} = 3.3 \times 7.128 \times 10^{-2} $$ $$ E_{\text{sleep}} = 0.235224 \, \text{J (per hour)} $$ ### Step 4: Total energy consumed per hour $$ E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{active}} + E_{\text{sleep}} $$ $$ E_{\text{total}} = 9.504 + 0.235224 = 9.739224 \, \text{J/hour} $$ ### Step 5: Convert battery capacity to total stored energy $$ E_{\text{stored}} = V_{\text{battery}} \times C_{\text{battery}} \times 3600 $$ Substitute values: $$ E_{\text{stored}} = 3.7 \times 2400 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 $$ $$ E_{\text{stored}} = 3.7 \times 2.4 \times 3600 $$ $$ E_{\text{stored}} = 8.88 \times 3600 = 31968 \, \text{J} $$ ### Step 6: Compute the total operating hours $$ T_{\text{hours}} = \frac{E_{\text{stored}}}{E_{\text{total}}} $$ $$ T_{\text{hours}} = \frac{31968}{9.739224} $$ $$ T_{\text{hours}} \approx 3282.6 \, \text{hours} $$ ### Step 7: Convert hours to years $$ T_{\text{years}} = \frac{T_{\text{hours}}}{24 \times 365} $$ $$ T_{\text{years}} = \frac{3282.6}{8760} $$ $$ T_{\text{years}} \approx 0.375 \, \text{years} \approx 134 \, \text{days} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** With a 1% active duty cycle, the node lasts only about **4.5 months** on a 2400 mAh battery. To extend this, the designer must either (a) reduce $I_{\text{active}}$ by selecting a lower-power radio (e.g., BLE instead of Wi-Fi), (b) shorten the active window, or (c) increase $C_{\text{battery}}$ (e.g., solar harvesting). ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Sensors to Physical Parameters | Physical Parameter | Sensor Example | Output Signal | Typical MCU Interface | Power (mW) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Temperature | LM35, DHT11 | Analog / Digital | ADC, I²C | 5 – 15 | | Humidity | DHT22, SHT31 | Digital | I²C, GPIO | 5 – 10 | | Pressure | BMP180, MPL3115A2 | Digital I²C/SPI | I²C, SPI | 10 – 20 | | Gas (CO, LPG) | MQ-2, MQ-135 | Analog | ADC | 800 – 950 (heater) | | Motion (PIR) | HC-SR501 | Digital pulse | GPIO | 50 – 70 | | Light | LDR, BH1750 | Analog / Digital I²C | ADC, I²C | 5 – 15 | | Proximity | IR (Sharp GP2Y0A21) | Analog | ADC | 30 – 50 | | Acceleration | ADXL345 | Digital I²C/SPI | I²C, SPI | 0.1 – 0.4 | | Gyroscope | MPU6050 | Digital I²C | I²C | 5 – 10 | | Heart Rate / SpO₂ | MAX30102 | Digital I²C | I²C | 10 – 20 | | GPS | NEO-6M | UART | UART | 50 – 100 | | Camera (Vision) | OV2640 | Parallel / MIPI | DCMI / CSI | 60 – 200 | | RFID | RC522 | SPI | SPI | 30 – 70 | ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Communication Protocols to Use-Case | Protocol | Standard | Range | Data Rate | Power | Use-Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | NFC | ISO/IEC 14443 | < 10 cm | 106 – 424 kbps | Very Low | Payments, Tags | | BLE | IEEE 802.15.1 | ~ 10 m | 1 – 2 Mbps | Low | Wearables | | ZigBee | IEEE 802.15.4 | 10 – 100 m | 250 kbps | Low | Home Automation | | Wi-Fi | IEEE 802.11 | 50 – 100 m | 11 – 1200 Mbps | Medium | High-throughput nodes | | LoRaWAN | LoRa Alliance | 2 – 15 km | 0.3 – 50 kbps | Very Low | Smart Agriculture | | NB-IoT | 3GPP Cat-NB1/NB2 | 1 – 10 km | 26 kbps (DL) | Low | Utility metering | | 5G NR | 3GPP Release 15+ | < 1 km (dense) | 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps | Variable | Autonomous vehicles | ## 3.4 Step-by-Step Logic — Designing a Generic IoT Node A KTU-style design question typically asks the student to **justify each hardware choice**. The logic flow is: 1. **Identify the physical phenomenon to be sensed** (e.g., soil moisture). 2. **Select a sensor** with appropriate range and accuracy. 3. **Choose an MCU** with matching interface (ADC/I²C) and low sleep current. 4. **Decide the communication protocol** based on range and bandwidth. 5. **Compute the battery lifetime** using the equations above. 6. **Add a gateway** if the protocol is non-IP (ZigBee/LoRa). 7. **Select cloud/storage** based on data volume. 8. **Validate the application layer hardware** (mobile / dashboard). # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic IoT Physical Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical
Environment] --> B1[Sensor Node A] A --> B2[Sensor Node B] A --> B3[Sensor Node C] B1 --> C[IoT Gateway
Raspberry Pi] B2 --> C B3 --> C C --> D[Communication
Backhaul WiFi LTE 5G] D --> E[Cloud Server
AWS Azure GCP] E --> F[Database and
Storage] E --> G[Analytics and
ML Engine] G --> H[Application Layer
Mobile Web Dashboard] H --> I[End User
Smartphone PC] B1 -. Power .-> P[Battery and
Energy Harvesting] B2 -. Power .-> P B3 -. Power .-> P C -. Power .-> P2[Mains or UPS] E -. Power .-> P3[Data Center PSU] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The leftmost column is the *Sensing Layer*, the gateway and backhaul form the *Network Layer*, the cloud forms the *Middleware/Processing Layer*, and the rightmost column is the *Application Layer*. Power lines are shown as **dotted lines**, indicating that power is a cross-cutting concern that affects every physical block. ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Single IoT Node ```mermaid flowchart TB S1[Temperature
Sensor] --> MCU S2[Humidity
Sensor] --> MCU S3[Motion
Sensor] --> MCU A1[Actuator
Relay Motor] --> MCU MCU[Microcontroller
ESP32] --> RADIO[Wireless Radio
WiFi BLE LoRa] MCU --> MEM[Local Memory
EEPROM Flash] RADIO --> ANT[Antenna
PCB Trace] PWR[Battery 3.7V Li-ion] --> REG[Voltage Regulator
3.3V LDO] REG --> MCU REG --> S1 REG --> S2 REG --> S3 REG --> A1 PWR -. Charge .-> SOLAR[Solar Panel and
Harvesting IC] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Physical Design Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[What must the IoT node do?] --> Q2{Need to sense or actuate?} Q2 -->|Sense| S1[Choose Sensor Type] Q2 -->|Actuate| A1[Choose Actuator Type] S1 --> Q3{Range Required?} A1 --> Q3 Q3 -->|Under 10 m| P1[BLE or NFC] Q3 -->|10 to 100 m| P2[ZigBee or WiFi] Q3 -->|Over 1 km| P3[LoRa or NB-IoT] P1 --> MCU[Select Low Power MCU] P2 --> MCU P3 --> MCU MCU --> BAT[Battery Sizing
Energy Equation] BAT --> END[Final Physical Design] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to "design" an IoT system in the exam, follow this decision-tree structure. The examiner is looking for a logical sequence: *Sense → Communicate → Process → Actuate → Power*. Skipping any step usually costs 2 marks. ## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence — Data Flow in Physical Design ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Sensor participant MCU participant Radio participant Gateway participant Cloud participant User Sensor->>MCU: Analog/Digital Reading MCU->>MCU: ADC and Filtering MCU->>Radio: Encoded Packet Radio->>Gateway: Wireless Transmission Gateway->>Cloud: HTTPS or MQTT over IP Cloud->>Cloud: Storage and Analytics Cloud->>User: Push Notification or Dashboard ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the "Physical Design of IoT" and list its four universal building blocks. **Model Answer:** The Physical Design of IoT refers to the actual hardware components and their physical interconnections that enable an IoT system to sense, communicate, process, and present data. The four universal building blocks are: 1. **Sensors and Actuators (Things)** — Interface with the physical world. 2. **Communication / Network Hardware** — Gateways, routers, wireless modules. 3. **Edge / Fog / Cloud Computing Hardware** — Servers, storage, processors. 4. **Application / User-Interface Hardware** — Smartphones, dashboards, displays. Each block is implemented with tangible components such as ESP32, ZigBee radios, AWS EC2 instances, and mobile devices. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a sensor and an actuator. Give one example of each. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Sensor | Actuator | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Function | Converts physical quantity to electrical signal | Converts electrical signal to physical action | | Direction | Input to the system | Output from the system | | Example | DHT11 temperature sensor, MQ-2 gas sensor | DC motor, relay, solenoid, LED | A temperature sensor (DHT11) measures ambient heat and outputs a digital code; a relay (actuator) closes a high-voltage circuit in response to a low-voltage command from the MCU. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **generic physical architecture of an IoT system**. Describe the role of **each block** in the data flow, citing at least **one example component** for each. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** An IoT soil-moisture sensor node operates at $V = 3.3 \, \text{V}$. Its active current is $50 \, \text{mA}$ and sleep current is $15 \, \mu\text{A}$. The node is active for **2 %** of the time. The battery is rated **3000 mAh at 3.7 V**. Calculate the **battery lifetime in years**. State two design strategies to extend it. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) The generic physical architecture of IoT is a four-layer model: 1. **Sensing / Perception Layer** — Contains the actual sensors (e.g., soil-moisture probe, DHT22 for temperature) and actuators (solenoid valve for irrigation). These are wired to the MCU via ADC, I²C, SPI, or GPIO. They physically interact with the environment. 2. **Network / Communication Layer** — Hardware such as the **ESP32's built-in Wi-Fi/BLE radio**, ZigBee modules (XBee), LoRa transceivers (SX1276), and the **Raspberry Pi gateway** that aggregates data. Protocols include Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), BLE (IEEE 802.15.1), LoRaWAN, and MQTT over TCP/IP. 3. **Middleware / Processing Layer** — Edge processors (e.g., **Jetson Nano**), fog nodes, and cloud servers (**AWS EC2**). Hardware storage includes SSDs and in-memory caches. The middleware handles data aggregation, filtering, machine-learning inference, and persistence. 4. **Application / Business Layer** — End-user hardware (smartphones, dashboards, voice assistants). It presents the data as actionable insights, e.g., a farmer's mobile app showing field moisture map. **Data Flow:** Sensor → MCU → Radio → Gateway → Cloud → Application → User. A neat block diagram (as in Section 4.1) is awarded 3 marks; descriptions of each layer with examples fetch the remaining 4 marks. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1: Time per hour** $$ t_{\text{active}} = 0.02 \times 3600 = 72 \, \text{s} $$ $$ t_{\text{sleep}} = 0.98 \times 3600 = 3528 \, \text{s} $$ **Step 2: Energy per hour in active mode** $$ E_{\text{active}} = 3.3 \times 0.050 \times 72 = 11.88 \, \text{J} $$ **Step 3: Energy per hour in sleep mode** $$ E_{\text{sleep}} = 3.3 \times 15 \times 10^{-6} \times 3528 \approx 0.1746 \, \text{J} $$ **Step 4: Total energy per hour** $$ E_{\text{total}} = 11.88 + 0.1746 = 12.0546 \, \text{J/hour} $$ **Step 5: Total stored energy** $$ E_{\text{stored}} = 3.7 \times 3.0 \times 3600 = 39960 \, \text{J} $$ **Step 6: Lifetime in hours** $$ T_{\text{hours}} = \frac{39960}{12.0546} \approx 3315 \, \text{h} $$ **Step 7: Lifetime in years** $$ T_{\text{years}} = \frac{3315}{8760} \approx 0.379 \, \text{years} \approx 138 \, \text{days} $$ **Design strategies to extend battery life:** 1. **Reduce the active duty cycle** further, or use **event-driven wake-up** (sleep until a threshold is crossed). 2. **Switch to a lower-power radio** like LoRa or BLE, or add **solar energy harvesting** to recharge the battery in situ. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students forget to convert mAh to Joules using the formula $E = V \times C \times 3600$. A common mistake is to divide mAh by mA directly (giving only hours, not years). Always convert to **Joules** for energy-based lifetime calculations. Also, watch unit consistency: $1 \, \text{mA} = 10^{-3} \, \text{A}$ and $1 \, \mu\text{A} = 10^{-6} \, \text{A}$. ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** > **(a)** Explain the **role of sensors and actuators** in the IoT physical design. Classify **five sensors** and **three actuators**, and specify the **interface protocol** each uses to connect to an MCU. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Compare **short-range, medium-range, and long-range communication protocols** used in IoT physical design. Give **two examples** for each category and justify your choice for a **smart agriculture application** that requires monitoring soil moisture over a **5 km** farm. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Role of Sensors:** Sensors are the *input transducers* of IoT. They capture physical phenomena (temperature, humidity, motion, light, gas concentration) and translate them into electrical signals (analog voltage, I²C/SPI digital frames). Without sensors, the IoT system would be "blind" to the physical world. **Role of Actuators:** Actuators are the *output transducers*. They accept electrical commands from the MCU and produce physical effects (rotation, switching, lighting, heating). They are what makes IoT *controlling* rather than just *monitoring*. **Five Sensor Classifications with Interfaces:** | Sensor | Physical Parameter | Interface to MCU | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | LM35 | Temperature | ADC (Analog) | | DHT22 | Temperature + Humidity | Single-wire GPIO | | BMP180 | Pressure | I²C | | ADXL345 | 3-axis acceleration | I²C / SPI | | MQ-2 | Gas (LPG/CO) | ADC (Analog) | **Three Actuator Classifications with Interfaces:** | Actuator | Action | Interface to MCU | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | DC Motor | Rotation | PWM via motor driver (L298N) | | Relay | Switching high-voltage load | GPIO | | Servo Motor | Angular positioning | PWM (50 Hz, duty-cycle coded) | **Conclusion:** The choice of interface (Analog/ADC, I²C, SPI, GPIO, PWM) determines the MCU's pin requirements and the firmware architecture. #### Model Solution — Part (b) | Range | Typical Protocols | Examples | Data Rate | Range | Power | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Short (< 10 m) | BLE, NFC, ZigBee, Wi-Fi | HC-05 BLE, RC522 NFC | 100 kbps – 100 Mbps | 5 – 50 m | Low – Medium | | Medium (10 m – 1 km) | Wi-Fi, ZigBee mesh | ESP32 Wi-Fi, XBee | 250 kbps – 100 Mbps | 50 m – 300 m | Medium | | Long (1 km – 15 km) | LoRaWAN, Sigfox, NB-IoT | SX1276 LoRa, BC95 NB-IoT | 0.3 – 50 kbps | 2 – 15 km | Very Low | **Justification for Smart Agriculture (5 km farm):** For a 5 km farm, the appropriate choice is **LoRaWAN**. Justifications: - **Range:** LoRa covers 2 – 15 km in rural open fields, easily spanning a 5 km farm. - **Power:** LoRa transceivers consume only ~ 10 – 100 mA during transmission, ideal for battery-powered soil-moisture nodes that must last years. - **Data rate:** Soil moisture readings are small (~ a few bytes), so 0.3 – 50 kbps is sufficient. - **Cost & Infrastructure:** A single LoRa gateway (e.g., Dragino LPS8) can serve thousands of nodes, far cheaper than deploying 5G or NB-IoT. Alternative acceptable: **NB-IoT** if cellular coverage is available; **ZigBee mesh** if a chain of 5–10 hops is acceptable over the 5 km span. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *range* with *throughput*. Mark is deducted when students claim "Wi-Fi works for 5 km" — Wi-Fi (802.11) is limited to ~ 100 m in open air. Always state the **standard's typical range** before selecting it. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition First:** Physical Design of IoT = the *hardware reality* of IoT (sensors, actuators, gateways, servers, applications). - **Four Universal Blocks:** Things → Network → Middleware → Application. - **Sensors are INPUTS, Actuators are OUTPUTS.** Never mix them up in MCQs. - **Microcontroller is the local brain** of every sensor node (ESP32, Arduino, STM32). - **Gateway bridges non-IP protocols to IP-based internet** (e.g., ZigBee → Wi-Fi/Ethernet). - **Three pillars of Physical Design:** *Sensors/Actuators*, *Communication*, *Power*. If any one is missing, the system fails. - **Power Equation:** $P = V \times I$, integrated over time gives energy in Joules. - **Battery lifetime formula:** $T = \frac{V_b \times C_b \times 3600}{P_{\text{node}}}$. - **Common interfaces:** ADC (analog), I²C, SPI, UART, GPIO, PWM. Know at least one example device for each. - **Range-vs-Power Trade-off:** BLE < ZigBee < Wi-Fi < LoRa < NB-IoT < 5G, but power consumption rises and falls non-monotonically. - **Industrial temperature range:** **−40 °C to +85 °C** for rugged IoT nodes. - **Edge vs Cloud:** Edge = low latency + low bandwidth; Cloud = high compute + high storage. - **Diagram Memorization:** Memorize the four-layer generic IoT block diagram — this is the single most-asked KTU diagram. - **Sample Sensors to Remember:** DHT11 (temp/humid), LM35 (temp), MQ-2 (gas), PIR (motion), LDR (light), ADXL345 (accel), NEO-6M (GPS). - **Sample Actuators to Remember:** DC motor, servo, stepper, relay, solenoid, LED, buzzer, heater. - **Energy-harvesting options:** Solar, vibration (piezo), thermal (TEG), RF — increasingly important for *deploy-and-forget* IoT nodes. - **Security in Physical Design:** Hardware-level TPM chips, secure boot, encrypted flash, tamper-proof enclosures — always mention security in long-answer questions. - **Standard wireless bodies to remember:** IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee basis), IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi), IEEE 802.15.1 (BLE), 3GPP (NB-IoT, 5G), LoRa Alliance (LoRaWAN).

topics.title: Logical Design of IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST834 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logical Design of IoT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Logical Design of IoT** refers to the abstract, functional representation of an Internet of Things system — describing *what* each component does, *how* data flows between them, and *which* communication patterns are employed — without being tied to any specific vendor, hardware, or physical implementation. It is the **"blueprint of behaviour"** that sits between the high-level use-case (functional view) and the physical deployment (physical view).

The logical design is formally decomposed into **four canonical pillars** as per the KTU 2024 OECST834 syllabus:

1. **IoT Functional Blocks** — the discrete building blocks (sensing, communication, processing, actuation, services, application).
2. **IoT Communication Models** — the *direction and style* of data exchange (Request–Response, Publish–Subscribe, Push–Pull, Exclusive Pair).
3. **IoT Communication APIs** — the *software-level interfaces* used to enforce those models (REST, WebSocket, etc.).
4. **IoT Levels of Deployment** — the *hierarchical complexity* of an IoT system (Level 1 → Level 6).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Framing (2024 Scheme):**
> Whenever a question says *"Explain the logical design of IoT"*, the expected answer **must** cover at minimum: (i) Functional Blocks, (ii) Communication Models, and (iii) Communication APIs. Levels of Deployment is the bonus high-yield content that elevates your answer to full marks.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of designing a **hospital information system** as an analogy:

| Element | Hospital Analogy | IoT Logical Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Functional Block | Departments (Reception, ICU, Pharmacy) | Sensing, Processing, Communication blocks |
| Communication Model | Doctor calls nurse, then nurse calls pharmacy (multi-hop) | Request–Response, Pub–Sub, etc. |
| Communication API | Intercom protocol rules | REST, WebSocket |
| Levels of Deployment | A small clinic (Level 1) vs. AIIMS (Level 6) | IoT Levels 1–6 |

A **small clinic** (Level 1) uses one thermometer and a phone call. **AIIMS** (Level 6) uses thousands of sensors, a private cloud, an AI diagnostic engine, and collaborates with research hospitals worldwide. The *logical* design is the *rulebook* that decides which "department" talks to which, in what manner, and through which channel — independent of whether the thermometer is digital or mercury.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Logical"?**
> The word *logical* (as opposed to *physical*) signifies that we are abstracting away hardware specifics. A *temperature sensor* is treated as a generic **"Sensing Block"** whether it is a DHT11, LM35, or a thermocouple. The logic — what it produces, who consumes it, and how — remains identical.

### Key Standard Metrics to Memorise

- The **six levels** of IoT deployment (Level 1 → Level 6).
- The **four** primary communication models.
- The **two** most commonly tested APIs: **REST** (Representational State Transfer) and **WebSocket**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 IoT Functional Blocks

An IoT system is logically partitioned into **six functional blocks**. Each block has a clearly defined *role*, *input*, and *output*, independent of vendor.

1. **Sensing / Identification Block** — *Role:* Capture physical phenomena (temperature, humidity, motion, RFID tag ID). *Input:* Real-world analog/digital signal. *Output:* Raw data points.

2. **Communication / Connectivity Block** — *Role:* Move data from sensing layer to processing layer using protocols like Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Cellular. *Why?* Because raw data at the sensor is useless without transport.

3. **Processing Block** — *Role:* On-board or edge-side computation; aggregation, filtering, simple decisions. *How?* Microcontrollers (Arduino, ESP32) or edge gateways.

4. **Actuation Block** — *Role:* The *reverse* of sensing — convert processed commands into real-world actions (relay ON, motor rotate, valve open).

5. **Services Block** — *Role:* Cloud-side storage, analytics, device management, security, identity. Exposed as PaaS/SaaS.

6. **Application Block** — *Role:* End-user facing dashboards, alerts, mobile apps, control panels.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Sensing ↔ Actuation** pair forms the *physical interface*; the **Communication + Processing + Services** triad forms the *cyber interface*; the **Application** is the *human interface*. This triad is the **most-cited diagram in KTU ESE answers** on logical design.

### 2.2 IoT Communication Models

| # | Model | Direction | Trigger | Typical Use | KTU Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Request–Response** | Client $\to$ Server $\to$ Client | Client initiates | Web dashboard reading sensor | "You ask, server answers." |
| 2 | **Publish–Subscribe** | Many-to-Many via Broker | Event occurs | MQTT-based telemetry | "Newspaper model — broker is the press." |
| 3 | **Push–Pull** | Producers $\to$ Queue $\to$ Consumers | Producer pushes, Consumer pulls | Log ingestion, Kafka pipelines | "Postbox — drop letter, receiver comes later." |
| 4 | **Exclusive Pair** | Bi-directional, full-duplex | Always-on connection | WebSocket, video streams | "Phone call — both can talk anytime." |

#### 2.2.1 Why Four Models? The Underlying 'Why'

- **Request–Response** is *synchronous* and *stateless* — best for infrequent, user-driven reads (HTTP REST GET of a temperature).
- **Publish–Subscribe** is *asynchronous* and *decoupled* — best for many sensors broadcasting to many consumers (smart factory).
- **Push–Pull** is *buffered* — best when the producer rate is bursty and the consumer is slow (e.g., camera pushes frames, analytics pulls).
- **Exclusive Pair** is *stateful* and *full-duplex* — best for real-time bidirectional control (remote surgery, live chat).

### 2.3 IoT Communication APIs

#### 2.3.1 REST (Representational State Transfer)

- **Architecture Style:** Client–Server, Stateless, Cacheable.
- **HTTP Verbs:** `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`.
- **Data Formats:** JSON (most common), XML, plain text.
- **Idempotency:** `GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE` are idempotent; `POST` is not.
- **Example KTU question form:** *"Fetch sensor data from `https://api.farm.com/sensors/temp`"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **REST is a Request–Response model.** If a question asks "which API implements Publish–Subscribe?", the answer is **MQTT or AMQP**, **never REST**.

#### 2.3.2 WebSocket

- **Protocol:** Starts as an HTTP `Upgrade` handshake, then becomes a **persistent, full-duplex TCP connection**.
- **Communication Model Used:** **Exclusive Pair**.
- **Latency:** Sub-millisecond after handshake — ideal for real-time control.
- **Use Case:** Live dashboards updating every 200 ms, multiplayer gaming, live drone control.

#### 2.3.3 REST vs WebSocket — The 'Why' Comparison

| Property | REST | WebSocket |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | New TCP per request | Single persistent TCP |
| Direction | Half-duplex (req/resp) | Full-duplex |
| Overhead | High (HTTP headers every call) | Low (frames after handshake) |
| Stateful? | Stateless | Stateful |
| Real-time suitability | Poor | Excellent |
| Communication Model | Request–Response | Exclusive Pair |
| KTU Exam Keyword | "Stateless CRUD" | "Real-time full-duplex" |

> [!NOTE]
> If a question says *"low-latency bidirectional"* — the answer is **WebSocket (Exclusive Pair)**. If it says *"stateless CRUD over HTTP"* — the answer is **REST (Request–Response)**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Term | Definition (Verbatim Board-Ready) | Memory Anchor |
|---|---|---|
| Logical Design | Abstract functional view of IoT system behaviour | "Blueprint, not brick" |
| Sensing Block | Captures real-world data | "Eyes and ears" |
| Actuation Block | Performs real-world actions | "Hands and legs" |
| Services Block | Cloud-side storage, analytics, mgmt | "Brain on the cloud" |
| Application Block | End-user interface | "Face of the system" |
| Request–Response | Client asks, server answers | "Question–Answer" |
| Publish–Subscribe | Broker fans out events | "Newspaper press" |
| Push–Pull | Queue buffers producer–consumer | "Postbox" |
| Exclusive Pair | Persistent bidirectional channel | "Phone call" |
| REST | Stateless HTTP-based CRUD API | "Stateless" |
| WebSocket | Full-duplex persistent channel | "Real-time" |
| Level 1 IoT | Single sensor, single app | "Toy system" |
| Level 6 IoT | Multiple collaborating IoT systems | "Smart city" |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — IoT Levels of Deployment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Levels** describe *how complex* an IoT system is logically, ranging from a single sensor to a federation of cloud-integrated, AI-driven, cross-organisational systems. There are **six levels** in the canonical KTU reference (per Raj Kamal, *Internet of Things*, McGraw-Hill — the prescribed text for OECST834). Examiners love this 6-level table — it is worth **at least 4–5 marks** in any Part-B question on logical design.

### Step-by-Step Construction of the Levels (Analytical Derivation)

**Step 1 — Start with the simplest case.**
A system with one sensor producing data, going directly to one cloud application.
*Logic:* If the system has $N_s$ sensors and $N_a$ applications, the simplest case is $N_s = 1, N_a = 1$. This is **Level 1**.

**Step 2 — Add intelligence (knowledge) at the edge.**
Now we have multiple sensors feeding a single application, but the application is "smart" — it knows rules.
*Logic:* $N_s > 1, N_a = 1$, with rule-based processing. This is **Level 2** — *Knowledge-based level*.

**Step 3 — Introduce edge processing and gateways.**
The sensors don't talk to the cloud directly; an **edge node** (gateway) aggregates them.
*Logic:* Gateway $\to$ multiple sensors $\to$ single application. This is **Level 3** — *Edge / Network level*.

**Step 4 — Add the cloud.**
Now multiple edge nodes feed data to a **cloud platform** for storage, analytics, ML.
*Logic:* $N_g$ gateways $\to$ 1 cloud $\to$ 1 application. This is **Level 4** — *Cloud / Processing level*.

**Step 5 — Add multiple applications on top of the cloud.**
Different stakeholders consume the same cloud (e.g., farmer app + retailer app + insurance app).
*Logic:* 1 cloud $\to$ $N_a > 1$ applications. This is **Level 5** — *Application level*.

**Step 6 — Add cross-organisation collaboration.**
Multiple independent IoT systems (smart farm + smart logistics + smart retail) collaborate.
*Logic:* $N_{systems} > 1$ cooperating. This is **Level 6** — *Collaboration level*.

### The Complete Analytical Matrix (Board-Ready Table)

| Level | Name | Key Characteristics | Sensors / Apps | Edge? | Cloud? | Example | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Basic Level | Single sensor $\to$ Single IoT app, no analytics | $N_s = 1, N_a = 1$ | No | No | Home burglar alarm | "1-to-1 Toy" |
| **2** | Knowledge-Based | Multiple sensors $\to$ Single app, rule-based decisions | $N_s > 1, N_a = 1$ | No | Optional | Weather monitor with thresholds | "Smart Edge Rule" |
| **3** | Edge / Network Level | Edge gateway aggregates, local analytics | $N_s > 1$, $1$ gateway, $1$ app | **Yes** | No | Factory floor gateway | "Gateway Hub" |
| **4** | Cloud / Processing Level | Edge $\to$ Cloud $\to$ App, heavy analytics, ML | $N_g > 1, N_{cloud} = 1$ | Yes | **Yes** | Smart agriculture platform | "Cloud Brain" |
| **5** | Application Level | One cloud, **multiple applications** for different users | $1$ cloud, $N_a > 1$ | Yes | Yes | Smart city dashboard + mobile app | "Multi-Face" |
| **6** | Collaboration Level | **Multiple independent IoT systems** cooperate | $N_{systems} > 1$ | Yes | Yes | Smart supply chain (farm + transport + retail) | "Federation" |

### Step-by-Step Example Mapping (for a KTU-style 14-mark answer)

**Problem:** *A coffee shop uses 4 temperature sensors, a Raspberry Pi gateway that averages readings, pushes to AWS IoT Core, and is consumed by a manager dashboard plus a customer-facing mobile app. Identify the level and justify.*

**Solution (every step explicit):**

1. **Sensor count:** $N_s = 4$ (more than 1). $\Rightarrow$ Not Level 1.
2. **Single app or smart logic?** The Pi performs averaging — that's local knowledge. $\Rightarrow$ Strong indicator of **Level 2 or Level 3**.
3. **Edge gateway present?** Yes — Raspberry Pi is a gateway. $\Rightarrow$ This is **Level 3 (Edge Level)**.
4. **Cloud present?** AWS IoT Core is a cloud platform. $\Rightarrow$ Promotes to **Level 4 (Cloud Level)**.
5. **Multiple applications?** Yes — manager dashboard + customer app $\Rightarrow$ Promotes to **Level 5 (Application Level)**.
6. **Cross-organisation collaboration?** No mention of other IoT systems. $\Rightarrow$ Stays at **Level 5**.

$$\boxed{\text{Final Answer: Level 5 — Application Level IoT}}$$

**Justification (board-ready prose):**
> *"The system qualifies as Level 5 because (i) it uses multiple sensors feeding a single cloud platform (AWS IoT Core), (ii) the cloud performs analytics and storage, and (iii) the cloud output is consumed by more than one application — a manager dashboard and a customer mobile app. There is no collaboration with an independent external IoT system, so it does not reach Level 6."*

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Logical Design in the Real World

| Domain | Use of Logical Design |
|---|---|
| **Smart Agriculture** | Designing a Level 5 system (multi-app farm dashboard + irrigation actuator) using Pub-Sub (MQTT) for sensor fan-out. |
| **Healthcare** | Level 6 hospital networks — REST for EHR fetch, WebSocket for live ICU monitoring. |
| **Smart Cities** | Level 6 — traffic, pollution, energy IoT systems collaborating via shared data lake. |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Level 4 — OPC-UA over edge gateways, cloud analytics, digital twin on application layer. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IoT Functional Blocks — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical World] --> B[Identification and Sensing Block]
    B --> C[Communication Block]
    C --> D[Processing Block]
    D --> E[Services Block]
    E --> F[Application Block]
    F --> G[End User]
    D --> H[Actuation Block]
    H --> A

    classDef sense fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comm fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef serv fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class B sense
    class C comm
    class D proc
    class E serv
    class F app
    class H act
```

**How to read this in a KTU exam:** The loop on the *right side* (Actuation $\to$ Physical World) is what makes IoT a **closed-loop cyber-physical system**, not just a data-collection system. Examiners award 1 extra mark if you explicitly mention this loop.

### 4.2 The Four Communication Models — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RR[Model 1 Request Response]
        C1[Client] -->|HTTP GET| S1[Server]
        S1 -->|HTTP Response| C1
    end

    subgraph PS[Model 2 Publish Subscribe]
        P1[Publisher Sensors] -->|Publish| B1[Broker MQTT]
        B1 -->|Subscribe Push| SUB1[Subscriber 1]
        B1 -->|Subscribe Push| SUB2[Subscriber 2]
    end

    subgraph PUP[Model 3 Push Pull]
        PP1[Producer] -->|Push to Queue| Q1[Message Queue Kafka]
        Q1 -->|Pull| CP1[Consumer Analytics]
    end

    subgraph EP[Model 4 Exclusive Pair]
        WS1[Client Web App] <-->|Persistent Full Duplex Frames| WS2[Server WebSocket]
    end

    classDef model1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef model2 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef model3 fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef model4 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class RR model1
    class PS model2
    class PUP model3
    class EP model4
```

### 4.3 IoT Levels of Deployment — Vertical Complexity Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 1 Basic Level One Sensor One App]
    L2[Level 2 Knowledge Based Multiple Sensors One Smart App]
    L3[Level 3 Edge Network Level Edge Gateway Aggregates]
    L4[Level 4 Cloud Processing Level Cloud Analytics and ML]
    L5[Level 5 Application Level One Cloud Multiple Apps]
    L6[Level 6 Collaboration Level Multiple IoT Systems Cooperate]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6

    classDef level1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef level2 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef level3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef level4 fill:#9ACD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef level5 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef level6 fill:#8A2BE2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF

    class L1 level1
    class L2 level2
    class L3 level3
    class L4 level4
    class L5 level5
    class L6 level6
```

**Exam Tip:** Always draw this **bottom-up** in the answer sheet. It signals to the examiner that you understand the *complexity gradient*. Drawing it top-down loses 1 mark due to "incorrect logical flow" as per the KTU valuation key.

### 4.4 REST vs WebSocket — Communication Pattern Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REST[REST Request Response Stateless]
        RC[Client App] -->|GET request HTTP headers| RS[REST Server]
        RS -->|JSON response with headers| RC
        RC -.->|New TCP per call| RS
    end

    subgraph WS[WebSocket Exclusive Pair Stateful]
        WC[Client App] -->|HTTP Upgrade Handshake| WS1[WebSocket Server]
        WS1 -->|101 Switching Protocols| WC
        WC <-->|Persistent full duplex frames| WS1
    end

    classDef rest fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ws fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px

    class REST rest
    class WS ws
```

### 4.5 End-to-End Logical Design — Putting It All Together

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHY[Physical Layer]
        S1[Temperature Sensor]
        S2[Humidity Sensor]
        A1[Water Pump Actuator]
    end

    subgraph EDGE[Edge Layer Level 3]
        GW[Edge Gateway Raspberry Pi]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD[Cloud Layer Level 4 5]
        PL[IoT Platform AWS IoT Core]
        DB[(Cloud Storage)]
        ML[Analytics and ML Engine]
    end

    subgraph APP[Application Layer Level 5]
        D1[Farmer Dashboard]
        D2[Customer Mobile App]
    end

    S1 --> GW
    S2 --> GW
    GW -->|MQTT Publish| PL
    PL --> DB
    PL --> ML
    PL --> D1
    PL --> D2
    ML -->|Decision Rule| GW
    GW -->|Control Signal| A1

    classDef phy fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef edge fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cloud fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef app fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class S1,S2,A1 phy
    class GW edge
    class PL,DB,ML cloud
    class D1,D2 app
```

This is the **master diagram** you should be able to redraw from memory in 90 seconds. It covers *every* component the KTU syllabus lists under logical design.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define the logical design of IoT. List the four functional blocks used in the logical design.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Logical design of IoT is the abstract, functional representation of an IoT system that defines the roles, data flow, and communication patterns of its components *without* being tied to any specific hardware or vendor.

**Four Functional Blocks (2 marks):**

1. **Sensing / Identification Block** — captures real-world data such as temperature, humidity, RFID tag IDs.
2. **Communication / Connectivity Block** — transports data from sensors to processing units using protocols like Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, or 6LoWPAN.
3. **Processing Block** — performs local computation, aggregation, filtering, and decision-making (microcontrollers, edge gateways).
4. **Services and Application Blocks** — cloud-side storage, analytics, device management, and end-user facing dashboards.

*(Alternative acceptable answer mentioning all six blocks: Sensing, Communication, Processing, Actuation, Services, Application — full 2 marks.)*

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between the Publish-Subscribe and Request-Response communication models with one example each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Request–Response | Publish–Subscribe |
|---|---|---|
| Initiation | Client initiates | Event triggers publish |
| Coupling | Tight (client knows server) | Loose (publisher and subscriber are decoupled) |
| Communication Pattern | Synchronous, point-to-point | Asynchronous, many-to-many |
| Example | Fetching sensor data via REST `GET` | MQTT topic `farm/temperature` broadcast to multiple dashboards |

**Example 1:** A mobile app sending `GET /api/temperature` to a server = **Request–Response (REST)**.
**Example 2:** A humidity sensor publishing to the MQTT broker on topic `greenhouse/humidity`, consumed by both an irrigation app and a logging service = **Publish–Subscribe**.

*(1 mark for differentiation table, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for correct model-to-example mapping.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse Communication **Models** with Communication **APIs** — Models are *patterns*; APIs are *implementations*. Students routinely mix them up and lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Do NOT** list only 3 levels in the IoT Levels question — the syllabus mandates **all 6 levels** with at least one distinguishing feature each.
> 3. **Do NOT** say "WebSocket is Request–Response" — it is **Exclusive Pair**. This is the most common single-fact error.
> 4. **Do NOT** draw the Levels diagram top-down; draw it **bottom-up** (Level 1 $\to$ Level 6).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the four IoT communication models in detail with suitable diagrams. (7 marks)**
**(b) Compare REST and WebSocket APIs. Which communication model does each implement? (7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Q.A(a) — Four IoT Communication Models (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):** IoT communication models define the *logical pattern* of data exchange between devices, gateways, servers, and applications. Four canonical models are defined in the KTU syllabus.

**1. Request–Response Model (1.5 marks):**
- A *client* sends a request to a *server*; the server processes it and returns a response.
- The communication is **synchronous**, **stateless**, and **point-to-point**.
- Example: An HTTP `GET https://api.farm.com/sensors/temp` call to retrieve the current temperature.
- Typical use: Infrequent, user-initiated reads (dashboard refresh, manual control).

**2. Publish–Subscribe Model (1.5 marks):**
- Devices **publish** messages to topics managed by a **broker** (e.g., MQTT broker, Kafka).
- Subscribers register interest in topics and receive messages asynchronously.
- Communication is **many-to-many**, **decoupled** — publishers and subscribers do not know each other.
- Example: A sensor publishing `farm/sensor/temp` to an MQTT broker; a dashboard, an analytics service, and a logging service all subscribe and receive the same message.

**3. Push–Pull Model (1.5 marks):**
- **Producers push** data into a **queue**; **consumers pull** data from the queue at their own pace.
- Decouples producer rate from consumer rate — useful for bursty traffic.
- Example: A high-resolution camera pushes images to a Kafka queue; an analytics service pulls and processes them.

**4. Exclusive Pair Model (1.5 marks):**
- A **persistent, bidirectional, full-duplex** connection is established between client and server.
- After setup, both parties can send messages at any time without re-establishing connection.
- Example: WebSocket-based live drone telemetry — the drone and control station continuously exchange control and sensor frames.

**Diagram (already provided in Section 4.2 — students should redraw the four-subgraph diagram).**

---

##### **Solution to Q.A(b) — REST vs WebSocket Comparison (7 marks)**

| Comparison Criterion | REST API | WebSocket API |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture Style** | Stateless client–server | Stateful, persistent connection |
| **Underlying Protocol** | HTTP/HTTPS | Starts as HTTP, upgrades to WebSocket protocol |
| **Connection Lifecycle** | New TCP connection per request (or pooled) | Single persistent TCP connection |
| **Direction of Data** | Half-duplex (request $\to$ response) | Full-duplex (both can send anytime) |
| **HTTP Headers Overhead** | Sent on every call (heavy) | Sent only once during handshake (light) |
| **Latency** | Higher (re-handshake per call) | Lower (sub-ms after handshake) |
| **Communication Model** | **Request–Response** | **Exclusive Pair** |
| **Data Format** | JSON, XML | JSON, binary frames |
| **Statefulness** | Stateless — server holds no session state | Stateful — server maintains session per connection |
| **Best-Suited Use Case** | CRUD operations, periodic polling | Real-time dashboards, live control, chat, gaming |
| **Example** | `GET /api/sensor/temp` returning `{"value": 28.4}` | Live ICU patient monitor pushing ECG every 200 ms |

**Key Verbatim Conclusion (1 mark for stating both models):**
> *"REST implements the Request–Response communication model, while WebSocket implements the Exclusive Pair communication model. REST is preferred for stateless, infrequent, user-initiated data exchange, whereas WebSocket is preferred for low-latency, real-time, bidirectional streaming."*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Describe the six levels of IoT deployment with a neat diagram. (7 marks)**
**(b) With a real-world example, illustrate how a smart agriculture system is logically designed using functional blocks, communication models, and APIs. (7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Q.B(a) — Six Levels of IoT Deployment (7 marks)**

| Level | Name | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1** | **Basic Level** | A single sensor connects directly to a single IoT application. No edge, no cloud, no analytics. | Home burglar alarm with one PIR sensor. |
| **Level 2** | **Knowledge-Based Level** | Multiple sensors feed a single application that applies *rule-based* logic (if-then) for decisions. | Weather station with 5 sensors triggering SMS alerts. |
| **Level 3** | **Edge / Network Level** | An **edge gateway** aggregates sensors, performs local analytics, and forwards summarised data. | Factory floor with PLC + SCADA gateway. |
| **Level 4** | **Cloud / Processing Level** | Edge nodes push data to a **cloud platform** for storage, big-data analytics, and ML. | Smart agriculture on AWS IoT Core with ML crop-yield prediction. |
| **Level 5** | **Application Level** | A single cloud serves **multiple applications** owned by different stakeholders. | Smart city: same traffic data feeds city dashboard, Google Maps, and a private logistics app. |
| **Level 6** | **Collaboration Level** | **Multiple independent IoT systems** cooperate across organisations to deliver a unified outcome. | Smart supply chain: farm IoT + transport IoT + retail IoT coordinate end-to-end. |

**Diagram:** Redraw the vertical ladder from Section 4.3. (1 mark reserved for the diagram.)

**Key Insight (1 mark for stating the progression logic):**
> *"The progression from Level 1 to Level 6 is driven by three orthogonal axes: (i) number of sensors/applications, (ii) introduction of edge processing, and (iii) introduction of cloud and cross-system collaboration. Each level adds one of these axes."*

---

##### **Solution to Q.B(b) — Smart Agriculture Logical Design (7 marks)**

**Scenario:** A 50-acre farm monitors soil moisture, temperature, and humidity, automates irrigation, and serves data to both the farmer and a cooperative retailer.

**Logical Design (Step-by-Step, 7 marks):**

1. **Functional Blocks (2 marks):**
   - *Sensing Block:* 20 soil-moisture sensors, 5 DHT22 temperature/humidity sensors, 1 rain gauge.
   - *Communication Block:* LoRaWAN modules (long range, low power) + Wi-Fi at the gateway.
   - *Processing Block:* Raspberry Pi edge gateway running local averaging and threshold logic.
   - *Actuation Block:* 4 solenoid-controlled water valves + 1 main pump relay.
   - *Services Block:* AWS IoT Core + DynamoDB + Lambda for analytics.
   - *Application Block:* Farmer web dashboard (React) + retailer mobile app (Flutter).

2. **Communication Models Used (2 marks):**
   - **Publish–Subscribe (MQTT):** Sensors $\to$ Edge gateway $\to$ AWS IoT Core — chosen because many sensors need to fan out data to many consumers (dashboard, analytics, retailer).
   - **Request–Response (REST):** Mobile app and dashboard fetch historical data and trigger manual override via HTTPS `GET`/`POST`.
   - **Exclusive Pair (WebSocket):** Farmer dashboard receives live sensor updates every 5 seconds with sub-second latency.

3. **APIs Used (1 mark):**
   - **REST over HTTPS** for CRUD operations (sensor metadata, irrigation schedules).
   - **WebSocket** for live streaming.
   - **MQTT** as the IoT-specific pub-sub protocol (technically a protocol, not a REST-style API).

4. **Level Classification (2 marks):**
   - 20+ sensors, an edge gateway, a cloud platform, and **two distinct applications** (farmer + retailer) $\Rightarrow$ **Level 5 — Application Level IoT**.
   - If the cooperative retailer's IoT system integrates back to share demand forecasts and the farm auto-adjusts planting schedules, it becomes **Level 6**.

**Conclusion (board-ready closing line, mandatory for full marks):**
> *"The smart agriculture system is a textbook Level 5 IoT deployment, employing a hybrid of Publish–Subscribe (MQTT) for telemetry fan-out, Request–Response (REST) for control and CRUD, and Exclusive Pair (WebSocket) for real-time dashboard updates. Its logical design cleanly separates sensing, edge processing, cloud services, and multi-stakeholder applications."*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this in the last 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Logical Design** = *what* each block does, *how* data flows, *which* model is used — NOT *which* hardware.
- The **six functional blocks** are: Sensing, Communication, Processing, Actuation, Services, Application.
- **Four Communication Models:** Request–Response, Publish–Subscribe, Push–Pull, Exclusive Pair.
- **REST** $\to$ Request–Response (stateless, HTTP, JSON, CRUD).
- **WebSocket** $\to$ Exclusive Pair (full-duplex, persistent, real-time).
- **MQTT** is the most common implementation of Publish–Subscribe in IoT.
- **Six IoT Levels** (1 $\to$ 6): Basic, Knowledge, Edge, Cloud, Application, Collaboration.
- **Level 1** = 1 sensor, 1 app, no edge, no cloud.
- **Level 6** = multiple independent IoT systems collaborating.
- The progression axes: **sensors/apps count** + **edge** + **cloud** + **cross-system**.
- The **actuation block** creates a *closed loop* back to the physical world — IoT is **cyber-physical**, not just monitoring.
- In the answer sheet, **draw diagrams bottom-up** for levels and **label every arrow** in the functional block diagram.
- **One real-world example** (smart agriculture / smart healthcare / smart city) is worth at least 2 extra marks in any 14-mark answer.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Never say "WebSocket uses Request–Response" or "MQTT is a REST API" — these are the two most common single-fact errors in KTU valuation.
- **Examiner love letter:** Mention the *closed-loop cyber-physical nature* of IoT at least once — it signals deep understanding and adds 1–2 grace marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Levels and Deployment Templates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST834 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT Levels and Deployment Templates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**IoT Levels** refer to a classification scheme that defines the role and capability of an IoT device (or "thing") within an IoT system, based on the **number of layers** of processing and intelligence it handles. These levels, conceptually defined in widely referenced IoT architectures, categorize devices from **Level 1 (lowest intelligence, single-purpose data collection)** to **Level 6 (highest intelligence, autonomous coordination with other devices)**.

**IoT Deployment Templates**, on the other hand, are **standardized reference architectures** that describe *how* IoT levels are practically combined and configured to solve real-world problems. They define the structural blueprint — including sensors, communication networks, cloud services, and applications — for a specific class of IoT use cases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to **identify, differentiate, and justify** the use of various IoT levels (1 through 6) and deployment templates. This is a **high-weightage** module topic typically appearing in **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** questions.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hospital hierarchy**:
- A **Level 1 IoT device** is like a **thermometer** — it just reads temperature and sends it. No thinking.
- A **Level 2 device** is like a **nurse** — it collects data AND does a tiny bit of processing (e.g., "Is temperature > 100°F? Alert!").
- A **Level 3 device** is like a **junior doctor** — it analyzes multiple data points, makes simple diagnoses.
- A **Level 4–5 device** is like a **senior doctor** — it can reason, store historical data, and even predict diseases.
- A **Level 6 device** is like a **panel of specialist doctors** — it collaborates with other senior systems to make complex, autonomous decisions.

**Deployment Templates** are the **blueprints** the hospital uses — like a "Standard ICU Setup Template" or a "Standard Outpatient Monitoring Template." Different problems need different setups.

### Key Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **6 IoT Levels** (Level 1 → Level 6) of device intelligence.
- **5 Common Deployment Templates** for IoT systems.
- Each level is characterized by **4 functional layers**: **Sensing, Processing, Communication, and Power Management**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept Map:**
> - **IoT Levels** = *How smart is a single device?*
> - **Deployment Templates** = *How do we assemble these devices into a working system?*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six IoT Levels — Detailed Breakdown

#### **Level 1: Basic Data Collection Device**
- **Function:** Single-purpose sensor node; **no local processing**.
- **Data flow:** Sensor → MCU → Communication Module → Cloud.
- **Example:** A simple soil moisture sensor pushing raw values to a server every minute.
- **Intelligence:** Minimal — only formatting and transmission.

#### **Level 2: Data Collection with Local Processing**
- **Function:** Sensor node with **basic on-device processing** (e.g., threshold checks).
- **Example:** A temperature sensor that only sends an alert when the reading exceeds a configured threshold, rather than streaming every reading.
- **Benefit:** Reduces network traffic and power consumption.

#### **Level 3: Local Processing with Event Filtering**
- **Function:** Performs **event-driven filtering and decision-making** at the edge.
- **Example:** A security camera that triggers recording only when motion is detected.
- **Advantage:** Significant bandwidth savings; pre-processed data sent to cloud.

#### **Level 4: Local Processing with Event Summarization**
- **Function:** **Aggregates and summarizes** data over time before transmission.
- **Example:** A vibration sensor on a motor that tracks average vibration per hour and only sends a daily summary.
- **Advantage:** Greatly reduces data volume; suitable for low-bandwidth networks.

#### **Level 5: Local Processing with Predictive Analytics**
- **Function:** Runs **basic ML/statistical models** locally for forecasting.
- **Example:** A smart thermostat that learns user patterns and predicts heating/cooling needs.
- **Advantage:** Edge AI; works offline.

#### **Level 6: Coordinated Multi-Device Intelligence**
- **Function:** **Multiple Level 5 devices** coordinate, share insights, and make collective decisions.
- **Example:** A swarm of agricultural drones sharing soil data to optimize irrigation collectively.
- **Advantage:** Distributed intelligence; no single point of failure.

### 2.2 The Five IoT Deployment Templates

| Template # | Template Name | Core Characteristic | Typical Use Case |
|:----------:|---------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **T1** | **Device-Centric Template** | Data flows from devices to a **centralized cloud** for storage and analysis. | Smart home energy monitoring. |
| **T2** | **Edge-Centric Template** | Significant processing occurs at the **edge gateway** before cloud upload. | Industrial automation. |
| **T3** | **Hybrid Template** | Combination of edge + cloud processing; **balanced workload**. | Smart cities. |
| **T4** | **Fog Computing Template** | Hierarchical processing across **multiple layers** (device → fog node → cloud). | Healthcare monitoring. |
| **T5** | **Mesh/Swarm Template** | **Peer-to-peer** device communication with no central controller. | Disaster response networks. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet / Conceptual Cheat Sheet**

| Concept | Key Attribute | Engineering Utility |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|
| **IoT Level 1** | Pure data collection, no processing | Used in low-cost sensor deployments |
| **IoT Level 2** | Local threshold checking | Reduces network bandwidth by **up to 80%** |
| **IoT Level 3** | Event-driven filtering | Optimizes **latency-sensitive** applications |
| **IoT Level 4** | Time-window summarization | Suitable for **batch analytics** |
| **IoT Level 5** | On-device ML inference | Enables **offline AI** |
| **IoT Level 6** | Multi-device coordination | Powers **autonomous systems** |
| **T1: Device-Centric** | Cloud-dependent | Lowest cost, highest latency |
| **T2: Edge-Centric** | Gateway-processed | Low latency, moderate cost |
| **T3: Hybrid** | Cloud + Edge balance | **Most widely used** in industry |
| **T4: Fog Computing** | Multi-tier hierarchy | Best for **mission-critical** apps |
| **T5: Mesh/Swarm** | Decentralized P2P | Highest resilience |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **Smart Agriculture:** Levels 1–3 + Template T2 (edge processing for remote farms).
- **Healthcare:** Levels 4–5 + Template T4 (hierarchical fog for patient monitoring).
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Levels 3–5 + Template T3 (hybrid for predictive maintenance).
- **Disaster Management:** Level 6 + Template T5 (resilient mesh networks).

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Levels — Engineering Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need 6 levels instead of just "smart" vs "dumb"?**
> - **Cost Optimization:** Higher levels require more powerful (expensive) MCUs and memory.
> - **Power Budgeting:** Battery-powered sensors cannot run ML models (Level 5+).
> - **Network Constraints:** In bandwidth-limited environments (LoRa, NB-IoT), we *need* local summarization (Level 4).
> - **Application Matching:** A simple "is the door open?" sensor doesn't *need* Level 5 capabilities — wasting resources.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Quantitative Justification for Local Processing (Level 2 vs Level 1)

**Problem:** A sensor samples temperature at 1 Hz. Without local processing (Level 1), it transmits every sample. With Level 2 (threshold of 30°C), it transmits only when crossed.

**Given:**
- Sampling rate $f_s = 1$ Hz
- Observation period $T = 1$ hour $= 3600$ s
- Probability of temperature exceeding threshold $P_{event} = 0.05$ (5%)

**Step 1: Total samples in observation period**

$$N_{total} = f_s \times T$$

$$N_{total} = 1 \text{ Hz} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 3600 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 2: Number of samples transmitted in Level 1 (no processing)**

$$N_{L1} = N_{total} = 3600 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 3: Number of samples transmitted in Level 2 (threshold-based)**

$$N_{L2} = P_{event} \times N_{total}$$

$$N_{L2} = 0.05 \times 3600 = 180 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 4: Bandwidth reduction ratio**

$$R = \frac{N_{L1} - N_{L2}}{N_{L1}} \times 100\%$$

$$R = \frac{3600 - 180}{3600} \times 100\%$$

$$R = \frac{3420}{3600} \times 100\% = 95\%$$

**Conclusion:** Level 2 processing achieves a **95% bandwidth reduction** — a critical metric for IoT systems on cellular or LPWAN networks where every byte costs money and power.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Edge vs Cloud Latency Trade-off (Template T1 vs T2)

**Problem:** Calculate the round-trip time (RTT) for a smart alarm system under two templates.

**Template T1 (Device-Centric — direct to cloud):**
- Sensor → Cloud RTT: $t_{sensor-cloud} = 150$ ms
- Cloud processing time: $t_{cloud} = 80$ ms
- Cloud → User notification: $t_{cloud-user} = 200$ ms

$$RTT_{T1} = t_{sensor-cloud} + t_{cloud} + t_{cloud-user}$$

$$RTT_{T1} = 150 + 80 + 200 = 430 \text{ ms}$$

**Template T2 (Edge-Centric — gateway processes first):**
- Sensor → Edge Gateway: $t_{sensor-gw} = 5$ ms
- Gateway processing time: $t_{gw} = 10$ ms
- Gateway → User (local network): $t_{gw-user} = 15$ ms

$$RTT_{T2} = t_{sensor-gw} + t_{gw} + t_{gw-user}$$

$$RTT_{T2} = 5 + 10 + 15 = 30 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Latency improvement factor**

$$Improvement = \frac{RTT_{T1}}{RTT_{T2}} = \frac{430}{30} \approx 14.33\times$$

**Conclusion:** Edge-centric deployment (T2) is **~14× faster** than device-centric (T1) — justifying its use in **latency-critical** applications like autonomous vehicles or industrial control.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting the Right IoT Level

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Selection Criteria for IoT Levels**

| Application Requirement | Recommended Level | Justification |
|------------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| Pure telemetry (e.g., weather station) | **Level 1** | Cheapest; no on-device compute needed |
| Alarm systems (e.g., smoke detector) | **Level 2** | Threshold-based alerts save battery |
| Motion-triggered camera | **Level 3** | Event filtering avoids continuous recording |
| Daily activity tracking | **Level 4** | Hourly/daily summaries sufficient |
| Predictive maintenance | **Level 5** | ML models detect failure patterns |
| Swarm robotics | **Level 6** | Coordination requires shared intelligence |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IoT Level Progression Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Level 1: Data Collection Only]
    L2[Level 2: Local Threshold Processing]
    L3[Level 3: Event-Driven Filtering]
    L4[Level 4: Time-Window Summarization]
    L5[Level 5: Predictive Analytics on Device]
    L6[Level 6: Coordinated Multi-Device Intelligence]

    L1 -->|Add threshold logic| L2
    L2 -->|Add event triggers| L3
    L3 -->|Add data aggregation| L4
    L4 -->|Add ML models| L5
    L5 -->|Add inter-device coordination| L6

    style L1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style L2 fill:#c5e8fa
    style L3 fill:#a3dbf5
    style L4 fill:#7accef
    style L5 fill:#4fbce8
    style L6 fill:#1f9ed8
```

### 4.2 IoT Deployment Template Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph T1[Template T1: Device-Centric]
        T1S[Sensor Device]
        T1C[Cloud Server]
        T1A[Application]
        T1S -->|Raw Data Stream| T1C
        T1C -->|Processed Insights| T1A
    end

    subgraph T2[Template T2: Edge-Centric]
        T2S[Sensor Device]
        T2E[Edge Gateway]
        T2C[Cloud Optional]
        T2S -->|Filtered Data| T2E
        T2E -->|Local Alerts| T2A[Local Application]
        T2E -.->|Summary Only| T2C
    end

    subgraph T3[Template T3: Hybrid]
        T3S[Sensor Devices]
        T3E[Edge Gateway]
        T3C[Cloud Server]
        T3A[Application Dashboard]
        T3S --> T3E
        T3E -->|Critical Data| T3C
        T3E -->|Routine Data| T3A
        T3C --> T3A
    end

    subgraph T4[Template T4: Fog Computing]
        T4S[Sensor Devices]
        T4F[Fog Node Layer]
        T4C[Cloud Server]
        T4S --> T4F
        T4F --> T4C
        T4F -->|Local Decisions| T4L[Local Control]
    end

    subgraph T5[Template T5: Mesh Swarm]
        T5D1[Device A]
        T5D2[Device B]
        T5D3[Device C]
        T5D4[Device D]
        T5D1 <--> T5D2
        T5D2 <--> T5D3
        T5D3 <--> T5D4
        T5D4 <--> T5D1
    end

    style T1 fill:#fff4e1
    style T2 fill:#e1ffe1
    style T3 fill:#e1e1ff
    style T4 fill:#ffe1f0
    style T5 fill:#f0e1ff
```

### 4.3 Functional Layer Architecture of an IoT Device (Applied Across Levels)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Device[IoT Device Functional Stack]
        A[Application Layer - User Interface Logic]
        B[Processing Layer - MCU / Microprocessor]
        C[Communication Layer - WiFi BLE LoRa etc]
        D[Sensing and Actuation Layer - Sensors Actuators]
        E[Power Management Layer - Battery Solar etc]
    end

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> B
    B --> D
    E -.->|Powers all| A
    E -.->|Powers all| B
    E -.->|Powers all| C
    E -.->|Powers all| D

    style A fill:#ffcccc
    style B fill:#ccffcc
    style C fill:#ccccff
    style D fill:#ffccff
    style E fill:#ffffcc
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow: Selecting the Right IoT Level

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Start: New IoT Application]
    Q1{Does it need local processing?}
    Q2{Network bandwidth limited?}
    Q3{Need ML or prediction?}
    Q4{Multiple devices must coordinate?}

    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -->|No| L1[Use Level 1]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2
    Q2 -->|No| L3[Use Level 3]
    Q2 -->|Yes| L4[Use Level 4]
    Q3 -->|Yes| L5[Use Level 5]
    Q3 -->|No| L4
    Q4 -->|Yes| L6[Use Level 6]
    Q4 -->|No| L5

    style L1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style L3 fill:#c5e8fa
    style L4 fill:#7accef
    style L5 fill:#4fbce8
    style L6 fill:#1f9ed8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define IoT Level 1 and IoT Level 6 devices. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Level 1 Device (1 mark):** A Level 1 IoT device is a single-purpose data collection node that performs **no local processing**. It only senses data and transmits it directly to a cloud server or gateway. All intelligence resides in the remote application.
  - **Example:** A basic temperature/humidity sensor in a greenhouse that sends raw readings every 10 seconds.

- **Level 6 Device (2 marks):** A Level 6 IoT device represents **coordinated multi-device intelligence** where multiple Level 5-capable devices share data, reason collectively, and make autonomous decisions without central control.
  - **Example:** A swarm of autonomous agricultural drones that share soil moisture data and collectively decide optimal irrigation zones without a central controller.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Edge-Centric and Device-Centric IoT deployment templates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Device-Centric (T1) | Edge-Centric (T2) |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Processing Location** | Cloud only (1 mark) | Edge gateway (1 mark) |
| **Latency** | High (100s of ms) | Low (10s of ms) |
| **Network Dependency** | Constant connectivity needed | Can operate offline |
| **Best Use Case** | Non-critical telemetry (1 mark) | Latency-sensitive industrial control |

---

### Part B: Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE from the following:**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **six levels of IoT** with a neat diagram. For each level, state one practical example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss any **three IoT deployment templates** in detail. Compare their advantages and disadvantages for industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) The Six IoT Levels (7 marks):**

The IoT levels classify devices based on the **amount of local intelligence and processing capability** they possess. Higher levels indicate greater on-device autonomy and reduced dependency on cloud infrastructure.

**Level 1 — Data Collection Devices (1 mark):**
These are the simplest IoT devices with no local processing. They consist of sensors connected to a microcontroller that simply reads sensor values and transmits them to a cloud server. The device has no decision-making capability.
- **Example:** A weather monitoring station that continuously streams raw temperature, humidity, and pressure values to a cloud database.

**Level 2 — Data Collection with Local Processing (1 mark):**
These devices perform basic on-device processing such as threshold checks before transmitting data. This reduces network traffic significantly.
- **Example:** A smoke detector that only transmits an alert when gas concentration exceeds a safe threshold, rather than streaming every reading.

**Level 3 — Local Processing with Event Filtering (1 mark):**
