**(b) A wireless sensor node is powered by two AA alkaline batteries (each 1.5 V, 1500 mAh in series = 3.0 V, 1500 mAh). The node draws 20 mA when active and 10 µA when sleeping. The node operates with a duty cycle of 0.5%. Calculate: (i) the average current, (ii) the battery lifetime in days, and (iii) the total energy delivered. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Convert units.**
$I_{sleep} = 10 \, \mu A = 0.010 \, mA$, $DC = 0.5\% = 0.005$, $C_{batt} = 1500 \, mAh$.

**Step (ii) — Average current.**

$$I_{avg} = I_{active} \cdot DC + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC)$$

$$I_{avg} = (20 \times 0.005) + (0.010 \times 0.995)$$

$$I_{avg} = 0.100 + 0.00995 = 0.10995 \, mA \approx 0.110 \, mA$$

**Step (iii) — Lifetime in hours, then days.**

$$L = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{1500}{0.110} \approx 13636.36 \, \text{hours}$$

$$L_{days} = \frac{13636.36}{24} \approx 568.18 \, \text{days} \approx 1.56 \, \text{years}$$

**Step (iv) — Total energy delivered.**

$$E = V \times C_{batt} = 3.0 \times 1500 = 4500 \, mWh = 4.5 \, Wh$$

Converting to Joules:

$$E = 4.5 \times 3600 = 16200 \, J = 16.2 \, kJ$$

**Final Answer Box:**
- (i) $I_{avg} \approx 0.110 \, mA$
- (ii) Lifetime $\approx 568 \, \text{days} \approx 1.56 \, \text{years}$
- (iii) Energy $= 4.5 \, \text{Wh} = 16200 \, \text{J}$

*(Mark split: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 3 marks, (iii) 2 marks for arithmetic; full marks require proper unit conversion in step (i).)*

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) Discuss the various energy harvesting techniques for IoT. For each, state the underlying physical principle and a representative power density range. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Harvesting Technique | Physical Principle | Power Density Range | Typical IoT Use Case |
|---------------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Photovoltaic (Solar)** | Photovoltaic effect — photons excite electrons across a semiconductor junction | 10 µW/cm² (indoor) to 100 mW/cm² (outdoor) | Weather stations, smart agriculture |
| **Piezoelectric** | Mechanical strain in crystals (e.g., PZT, PVDF) produces charge separation | 10–1000 µW/cm² | Footstep-powered floors, bridge vibration sensors |
| **Thermoelectric (TEG)** | Seebeck effect — temperature gradient drives charge carriers | 10–100 µW/cm² per °C gradient | Wearables, industrial boiler monitoring |
| **Electromagnetic (Induction)** | Faraday's law — moving magnet through coil induces EMF | 1–100 mW | Self-powered push buttons, kinetic watches |
| **RF / Rectenna** | Rectifying antenna captures ambient EM waves (TV, Wi-Fi, GSM) | 0.1–10 µW/cm² | Passive RFID, smart cards |
| **Wind / Fluid Flow** | Micro-turbine converts kinetic energy of air/water to rotational → electrical | 1–10 mW | Outdoor environmental motes |

**Key Observations (2 marks):**
- **Solar dominates in outdoor deployments** with the highest power density.
- **Indoor and body-area applications** rely on hybrid kinetic + thermal harvesting.
- **RF harvesting is universally available** but yields the lowest power — sufficient only for ultra-low-power protocols (e.g., backscatter communication).

---

**(b) Explain the duty cycling technique for energy conservation in IoT. Derive the average power consumption and show with a numerical example how reducing the duty cycle from 5% to 0.1% affects the battery lifetime. Assume $C_{batt} = 1000 \, mAh$, $I_{active} = 30 \, mA$, $I_{sleep} = 5 \, \mu A$, and battery voltage 3 V. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):**
Duty cycling is the practice of switching the IoT node between an **active** state (sensing, processing, transmitting) and a **sleep** state. The node is active for only a small fraction $DC$ of the total period. This is the single most impactful energy-saving technique because the radio (the dominant power consumer) is energized only when absolutely necessary.

**Derivation of Average Power (2 marks):**

$$P_{avg} = DC \cdot P_{active} + (1 - DC) \cdot P_{sleep}$$

Since $P_{sleep} \ll P_{active}$ (typically by 3–4 orders of magnitude), this reduces to:

$$P_{avg} \approx DC \cdot P_{active}$$

**Numerical Example (3 marks):**

Convert sleep current: $I_{sleep} = 5 \, \mu A = 0.005 \, mA$.

**Case 1: DC = 5% = 0.05**

$$I_{avg,1} = (30 \times 0.05) + (0.005 \times 0.95) = 1.500 + 0.00475 = 1.50475 \, mA$$

$$L_1 = \frac{1000}{1.50475} \approx 664.55 \, \text{hours} \approx 27.69 \, \text{days}$$

**Case 2: DC = 0.1% = 0.001**

$$I_{avg,2} = (30 \times 0.001) + (0.005 \times 0.999) = 0.030 + 0.004995 = 0.034995 \, mA$$

$$L_2 = \frac{1000}{0.034995} \approx 28575.5 \, \text{hours} \approx 1190.6 \, \text{days} \approx 3.26 \, \text{years}$$

**Comparison:**

$$\text{Lifetime Ratio} = \frac{L_2}{L_1} = \frac{28575.5}{664.55} \approx 43.0$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Reducing the duty cycle from 5% to 0.1% (a 50× reduction) extends battery life from ~28 days to ~3.26 years — a **~43× lifetime improvement**. This quantitatively demonstrates the power of duty cycling as the dominant energy conservation strategy in IoT.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> **[!WARNING]**
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in the Energy Module:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Students frequently mix µA and mA in the average current formula. Always convert sleep current to mA **before** substituting into the duty cycle equation. (Loss: 1–2 marks per question.)
> 2. **Forgetting the Sleep Term:** Writing $I_{avg} = I_{active} \times DC$ and ignoring the $I_{sleep} \times (1-DC)$ term is acceptable when $I_{sleep}$ is negligible, but if the problem gives a specific sleep current (e.g., 5 µA), you **must** include it for full marks.
> 3. **Duty Cycle Confusion:** Some students treat duty cycle as a percentage (e.g., 5) rather than a fraction (0.05) in formulas. Always express DC as a decimal between 0 and 1 inside equations.
> 4. **Confusing Energy and Power:** $E = P \times t$ is energy. If the question asks for energy in Joules and you report Watt-hours (or vice versa), you lose the unit-conversion mark.
> 5. **Skipping the Final Conversion:** Examiners expect lifetime answers in **both hours AND days/years**. A student who writes "L = 13636 hours" without converting to days forfeits 1 mark.
> 6. **Neglecting the PMU/Buck Regulator Loss:** In long-form 14-mark questions, mentioning the power management unit's conversion loss (typically 70–95% efficiency) earns an extra conceptual mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **[!TIP]**
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin this before the exam.**

- **Energy in IoT** = the total electrical work (Joules / Wh) consumed, stored, or harvested by an end device.
- **Two source classes:** **Stored** (batteries, supercaps — finite, high density) vs. **Harvested** (solar, vibration, thermal, RF, wind — renewable, low density, intermittent).
- **Solar** is the highest-power harvester outdoors; **RF** is the lowest-power but universally available.
- **Communication is the #1 energy hog** (10–100× sensing/compute); always minimize radio on-time.
- **The single most important formula:**

$$I_{avg} = I_{active} \cdot DC + I_{sleep} \cdot (1-DC) \quad \text{and} \quad L = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}}$$

- **Duty cycle** $DC = T_{active} / T_{period}$. Reducing DC by 50× → ~50× longer life.
- **Energy Neutral Operation (ENO):** $P_{harvest} \times T_{harvest} \times \eta \geq P_{avg} \times 86400$ — the condition for perpetual IoT operation.
- **Power management strategies:** duty cycling, data aggregation, multi-hop routing, edge AI/compression, wake-up radios, dynamic voltage scaling (DVS).
- **Key reference values:** CR2032 = 3 V, 220 mAh (~2400 J); BLE TX = 5–15 mA; Wi-Fi TX = 170–300 mA.
- **Conversion:** $1 \, Wh = 3600 \, J$; battery energy $E = V \times C$ (in Wh if $C$ is in Ah).
- **A 1% duty cycle + efficient harvester** = the standard recipe for 5–10 year unattended IoT operation.
- **In the exam:** always state units explicitly, convert µA ↔ mA, and present lifetime in **both hours and days/years**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agriculture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST834 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Agriculture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Internet of Things (IoT) in Agriculture**, formally termed **Smart Agriculture** or **Precision Agriculture (PA)**, is the integration of cyber-physical sensor networks, embedded communication hardware, and cloud-edge analytics into traditional farming workflows to enable real-time, site-specific, data-driven crop and resource management.

According to the KTU 2024 OEC syllabus, this domain is defined as the application layer deployment of IoT where heterogeneous sensor nodes (soil, climate, biometric) collaborate over Low-Power Wide-Area Networks (LPWAN) such as **LoRaWAN**, **ZigBee**, and **NB-IoT** to optimize agronomic inputs—water, fertilizers, and pesticides—thereby maximizing yield per unit area while minimizing ecological footprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Definition Anchor (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Smart Agriculture is the cyber-physical orchestration of sensor–actuator–gateway–cloud tiers that converts agronomic micro-variations into actionable automation decisions, governed by data latency budgets not exceeding 5 seconds for irrigation loops."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Farmhand" Mental Model

Imagine replacing a farmer's traditional five-sense judgment with a tireless, hyper-accurate electronic assistant:

| Human Farmer's Sense | IoT Counterpart | Field Action Triggered |
|---|---|---|
| Touching soil for dryness | **Soil Moisture Sensor (Capacitive/VWC)** | Auto-stops irrigation at 35% VWC |
| Looking at sky for rain forecast | **DHT22 + Weather API** | Delays irrigation if rain probability > 70% |
| Walking the field to spot pests | **Multispectral Camera + ML Edge Node** | Releases targeted pheromone dispensers |
| Checking animal health visually | **Cattle Biometric Collar (Heart-Rate, Rumination)** | Alerts veterinary module on anomaly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** IoT in Agriculture is simply giving the farm a *nervous system* (sensors), a *brain* (cloud/edge analytics), and a *muscle* (actuators like solenoid valves and drones). The farmer becomes the *supervisor* instead of the *laborer*.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Favored Constants)

The following **bolded** metrics are high-yield for KTU numericals:

- **Latency budget for irrigation loop:** ≤ **5 seconds**
- **Volumetric Water Content (VWC) optimal range:** **30% – 45%**
- **LoRaWAN Spreading Factor (SF) for farms:** **SF7 – SF12**
- **Typical soil sensor deployment density:** **1 node per 0.5 – 2 hectares**
- **Power autonomy target (battery + solar):** **≥ 5 years**
- **NB-IoT uplink payload:** **≤ 125 bytes** per transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Four-Tier Model

Smart Agriculture IoT follows a strict layered architecture. Each tier has a *Why* (motivation) and a *How* (mechanism):

### Tier 1 — Perception Layer (The Sensory End-Nodes)
- **Why:** Convert physical/chemical field variables into electrical signals.
- **How:** Deploy MEMS-based transducers (capacitive hygrometers, thermistors, NDIR CO₂ cells, electrochemical pH probes) on **STM32 / ESP32 / Arduino-class** MCUs.
- **Key components:** Soil Moisture, Soil Temperature, Ambient Humidity (DHT22), Light (BH1750), pH, NPK ion-selective sensors.

### Tier 2 — Network Layer (The Communication Backbone)
- **Why:** Transport sensor payloads over distances of 500 m – 10 km with minimal energy.
- **How:** Choose protocol based on **range–bandwidth–power** trade-off.

| Protocol | Range | Bandwidth | Power | KTU Farm Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LoRaWAN** | 2 – 10 km | 0.3 – 50 kbps | Ultra-low | Soil telemetry over hectares |
| **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** | 10 – 100 m | 250 kbps | Low | Greenhouse mesh |
| **NB-IoT** | 1 – 10 km | 26 kbps (DL) / 62 kbps (UL) | Low | Cellular rural coverage |
| **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** | ≤ 50 m | 54+ Mbps | High | In-door farm analytics only |

### Tier 3 — Edge / Fog Layer (Local Intelligence)
- **Why:** Avoid round-trip cloud latency for time-critical irrigation decisions.
- **How:** Deploy **Raspberry Pi / Jetson Nano** gateways running lightweight ML (e.g., TFLite) to act on threshold breaches locally.

### Tier 4 — Cloud + Application Layer (Decision Engine)
- **Why:** Long-term analytics, weather correlation, yield prediction.
- **How:** Ingest into **AWS IoT Core / Azure IoT Hub / ThingsBoard**; visualize on Grafana dashboards; trigger SMS/alert APIs.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Smart Agriculture Engineering Mathematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **highest-frequency numerical targets** in KTU ESE papers. All use the `\vert` notation to comply with the markdown-table pipe escape rule.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Soil Moisture (VWC by Capacitance)** | $VWC = \dfrac{C_{soil} - C_{dry}}{C_{sat} - C_{dry}} \times 100\%$ | $C$ in pF | Trigger irrigation at VWC < **30%** |
| 2 | **Actual Evapotranspiration (FAO-56 Penman–Monteith)** | $ET_{c} = K_{c} \times ET_{0}$ | $K_{c}$ = crop coefficient, $ET_{0}$ in mm/day | Calculate daily irrigation need |
| 3 | **Net Irrigation Requirement** | $IR_{n} = ET_{c} - P_{eff} + \Delta SM$ | $P_{eff}$ = effective rainfall (mm), $\Delta SM$ = soil moisture deficit (mm) | Schedule precise water dosing |
| 4 | **Gross Irrigation (with efficiency)** | $IR_{g} = \dfrac{IR_{n}}{E_{a}}$ | $E_{a}$ = application efficiency (0.6 – 0.9) | Account for drip losses |
| 5 | **LoRaWAN Time-on-Air (simplified)** | $T_{air} = T_{preamble} + T_{payload}$ | ms | Energy budgeting per node |
| 6 | **Sensor Node Battery Life** | $T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{batt}}{(I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep} + I_{tx} \cdot t_{tx}) / T_{cycle}}$ | hours; $C$ in mAh | Solar-sizing calculation |
| 7 | **Path Loss (Log-Distance Model)** | $PL(d) = PL(d_{0}) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_{0}}\right) + X_{\sigma}$ | $n$ = path loss exponent (cropland ≈ 2.7 – 3.5) | Gateway placement |
| 8 | **Yield Improvement Index** | $YI = \dfrac{Y_{IoT} - Y_{conv}}{Y_{conv}} \times 100\%$ | % gain | Justify system ROI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Equation 3 is the most commonly tested numerical in KTU Module-1. A typical 14-mark question will give rainfall data, soil deficit, and crop coefficient, and ask the student to compute water-saving versus flood irrigation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Net Irrigation Requirement Model

This is a **board-favorite 14-mark derivation**. We derive how an IoT system computes the exact litres of water needed per square metre of farmland.

### Given (Typical KTU Numerical Setup)
- Crop: Tomato
- Reference Evapotranspiration $ET_{0} = 6.2$ mm/day
- Crop coefficient $K_{c} = 1.15$ (mid-season)
- Effective rainfall $P_{eff} = 2.0$ mm/day
- Soil moisture deficit $\Delta SM = 1.8$ mm/day
- Drip application efficiency $E_{a} = 0.85$

### Step 1 — Start from the FAO-56 Penman–Monteith relationship

The crop's actual water consumption equals the atmospheric demand modified by the crop's biological stage:

$$
ET_{c} = K_{c} \times ET_{0}
$$

**Logic:** $K_{c}$ scales the generic reference grass evapotranspiration to the specific crop's leaf-area and stomatal behaviour. For tomatoes in the flowering stage, $K_{c} > 1$ because transpiration exceeds reference grass.

### Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values

$$
ET_{c} = 1.15 \times 6.2 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

$$
ET_{c} = 7.13 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

**Logic:** This is the **gross water depth** the tomato crop draws from the soil each day.

### Step 3 — Apply the Water Balance Equation

The field's water balance states: *what the crop uses must equal what the soil supplies plus what rain contributes plus what we irrigate.*

$$
ET_{c} = P_{eff} + IR_{n} - \Delta SM
$$

Rearranging to solve for net irrigation:

$$
IR_{n} = ET_{c} - P_{eff} + \Delta SM
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and compute

$$
IR_{n} = 7.13 - 2.0 + 1.8 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

$$
IR_{n} = 6.93 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

**Logic:** The IoT system must supply **6.93 mm of water depth** per day, because rainfall only offsets 2.0 mm and the soil reservoir is being depleted at 1.8 mm/day.

### Step 5 — Convert depth to volume per hectare

For 1 hectare = 10,000 m², every 1 mm of depth = 10,000 L = 10 m³. Therefore:

$$
V_{n} = IR_{n} \times 10 \times 10^{3} \; \text{L/hectare}
$$

$$
V_{n} = 6.93 \times 10^{4} \; \text{L/hectare}
$$

$$
V_{n} = 69{,}300 \; \text{L/day/hectare}
$$

### Step 6 — Adjust for dripper system efficiency

Drip systems lose water to evaporation and runoff. The *gross* volume the pump must deliver is:

$$
IR_{g} = \frac{IR_{n}}{E_{a}} = \frac{6.93}{0.85}
$$

$$
IR_{g} = 8.153 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

$$
V_{g} = 8.153 \times 10^{4} = 81{,}530 \; \text{L/day/hectare}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Engineering Conclusion

> The IoT-controlled drip system must command the solenoid valve to release **81,530 litres per hectare per day**, computed every 24 hours by the cloud engine, replacing the farmer's manual guess of ~120,000 L (flood irrigation). **Water saving = 32%.**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sensor-to-Decision Latency Budget

| Stage | Component | Typical Latency | Cumulative |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensor ADC sampling | **200 ms** | 200 ms |
| 2 | MCU processing (ESP32) | **50 ms** | 250 ms |
| 3 | LoRaWAN uplink (SF7) | **~120 ms** | 370 ms |
| 4 | Gateway → Cloud MQTT publish | **150 ms** | 520 ms |
| 5 | Cloud threshold check | **80 ms** | 600 ms |
| 6 | Cloud → Gateway downlink | **180 ms** | 780 ms |
| 7 | Actuator (solenoid) energize | **300 ms** | 1.08 s |
| 8 | Hydraulic valve open + water arrival | **~3 s** | **~4 s** |

**Total loop latency ≈ 4 seconds**, comfortably within the **5-second KTU benchmark**.

> [!NOTE]
> The above matrix is a *mandatory* construction in KTU 14-mark answers. It demonstrates that the student understands the *end-to-end* responsiveness of an IoT system, not just individual components.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Smart Agriculture IoT — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block maps the complete cyber-physical pipeline from field sensor to farmer's mobile alert. Note the **alphanumeric node-ID rule** (no reserved keywords as bare names) and **raw uppercase labels** (no markdown inside quotes).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[FIELD NODE A] --> B1[SOIL MOISTURE SENSOR]
    A2[FIELD NODE B] --> B2[NPK ION SENSOR]
    A3[GREENHOUSE] --> B3[DHT22 HUMIDITY]
    A4[CATTLE SHED] --> B4[RFID EAR TAG]
    B1 --> C1[STM32 MCU GATEWAY]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C2[ESP32 MESH NODE]
    B4 --> C2
    C1 --> D1[LoRaWAN GATEWAY 868MHZ]
    C2 --> D2[ZIGBEE COORDINATOR]
    D1 --> E1[EDGE AI JETSON NANO]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[AWS IOT CORE MQTT BROKER]
    E1 --> F2[THINGSBOARD DASHBOARD]
    F1 --> G1[DECISION ENGINE THRESHOLD LOGIC]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[SOLENOID VALVE ACTUATOR]
    G1 --> H2[DRONE SPRAYER]
    G1 --> H3[SMS EMAIL PUSH ALERT]
    H1 --> I1[FARMER MOBILE APP]
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I1
    F1 --> J1[LONG TERM YIELD ANALYTICS DB]
    J1 --> K1[ML CROP YIELD PREDICTION]
    K1 --> G1
```

## 4.2 Data Flow Block Diagram (Tier-wise)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1[TIER 1 PERCEPTION LAYER]
        s1[SOIL VWC SENSOR]
        s2[AMBIENT TEMP SENSOR]
        s3[LEAF WETNESS SENSOR]
    end
    subgraph T2[TIER 2 NETWORK LAYER]
        n1[LoRaWAN SF7]
        n2[NB-IoT CAT-M1]
    end
    subgraph T3[TIER 3 EDGE FOG LAYER]
        e1[RASPBERRY PI GATEWAY]
    end
    subgraph T4[TIER 4 CLOUD APPLICATION LAYER]
        c1[MQTT BROKER]
        c2[TIMESCALE DB]
        c3[GRAFANA DASHBOARD]
        c4[ML YIELD MODEL]
    end
    s1 --> n1
    s2 --> n1
    s3 --> n2
    n1 --> e1
    n2 --> e1
    e1 --> c1
    c1 --> c2
    c2 --> c3
    c2 --> c4
    c4 --> c1
    c1 --> e1
```

## 4.3 Decision-Trigger State Machine (Irrigation Loop)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    stateIDLE[IDLE MONITORING]
    stateREAD[READ SENSOR VWC]
    stateCHECK[CHECK THRESHOLD]
    stateIRRIG[ACTIVATE VALVE]
    stateWAIT[WAIT 60 SECONDS]
    stateALERT[SEND ALERT]
    stateIDLE --> stateREAD
    stateREAD --> stateCHECK
    stateCHECK --> stateIRRIG : VWC LESS THAN 30 PERCENT
    stateCHECK --> stateIDLE : VWC GREATER THAN 45 PERCENT
    stateIRRIG --> stateWAIT
    stateWAIT --> stateREAD
    stateIRRIG --> stateALERT : VALVE FAULT DETECTED
    stateALERT --> stateIDLE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing **Volumetric Water Content (VWC %)** with **gravimetric moisture (θg)** — they are *not* numerically equal.
> 2. Forgetting to convert **mm depth → litres/hectare** (1 mm = 10,000 L/ha). KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for unit mismatches.
> 3. Writing "Wi-Fi" as the primary farm protocol — incorrect for outdoor >50 m. **LoRaWAN/ZigBee** is the KTU-expected answer.
> 4. Omitting the **Edge layer** in architecture diagrams — must appear between Network and Cloud.
> 5. Stating the irrigation latency as "real-time" without a numerical budget — always quote the **5-second** benchmark.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Recall)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Define Smart Agriculture. List any two IoT-based applications in agriculture.**

**Model Answer (Memorize This Structure):**

**Definition:** Smart Agriculture is the application of IoT technologies—sensors, communication networks, and cloud analytics—to monitor field conditions in real time and automate irrigation, fertilization, and pest control for optimized resource utilization and yield enhancement.

**Two Applications:**
1. **Precision Irrigation:** Soil moisture sensors coupled with automated solenoid valves deliver water only when the Volumetric Water Content (VWC) falls below the crop-specific threshold (typically **30%** for tomatoes, **25%** for wheat), saving up to **30–50%** of water.
2. **Greenhouse Climate Control:** DHT22 temperature/humidity sensors and CO₂ sensors regulate exhaust fans, misters, and shade nets via edge controllers to maintain microclimates optimal for high-value crops.

*(Marks: Definition 1 + 2 applications × 1 mark each = 3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Differentiate between LoRaWAN and ZigBee in the context of agricultural IoT networks.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | LoRaWAN | ZigBee |
|---|---|---|
| Range | 2 – 10 km | 10 – 100 m |
| Topology | Star-of-stars | Mesh |
| Power | Ultra-low (battery 5–10 yrs) | Low (mesh relays drain faster) |
| Data Rate | 0.3 – 50 kbps | 250 kbps |
| **Best Farm Use** | Open-field soil telemetry over hectares | **Greenhouse** in-door sensor mesh |

*(1 mark for the core distinction; 2 marks for the table justification = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-tier architecture of an IoT-based smart agriculture system**, listing the function of each tier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wheat field of 2 hectares has the following weekly IoT sensor data: average $ET_0 = 4.5$ mm/day, $K_c = 1.05$ (tillering stage), effective rainfall $P_{eff} = 1.2$ mm/day, soil moisture deficit $\Delta SM = 0.8$ mm/day, and sprinkler efficiency $E_a = 0.75$. Compute **(i)** Crop Evapotranspiration $ET_c$, **(ii)** Net Irrigation Requirement $IR_n$, **(iii)** Gross Irrigation Requirement in litres per day for the entire 2-hectare field. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The four-tier architecture is:

1. **Perception Layer:** Houses the MEMS sensors (soil moisture, DHT22, NPK, pH) that transduce physical agronomic variables into electrical signals. These are interfaced to microcontrollers (ESP32/STM32) via ADC/I2C/SPI.

2. **Network Layer:** Transports the digitized sensor data using **LoRaWAN** (open-field) or **ZigBee** (greenhouse). This layer handles MAC addressing, encryption (AES-128), and duty-cycle regulation to comply with regional radio norms (e.g., **868 MHz** in India).

3. **Edge/Fog Layer:** Performs local analytics on **Raspberry Pi / Jetson Nano** gateways. It executes threshold checks and runs lightweight ML models (TFLite) to avoid cloud round-trip latency for time-critical irrigation decisions.

4. **Cloud + Application Layer:** Ingest via **MQTT** brokers (Mosquitto/AWS IoT Core) into time-series databases (InfluxDB/TimescaleDB). Visualization on Grafana; long-term yield prediction via ML; alert dispatch through Twilio SMS or push APIs.

*(Block diagram from Section 4.1 referenced. 7 marks = 1 per tier explanation + 3 for diagram.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Complete Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Crop Evapotranspiration:**

$$
ET_c = K_c \times ET_0 = 1.05 \times 4.5
$$

$$
ET_c = 4.725 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Net Irrigation Requirement:**

$$
IR_n = ET_c - P_{eff} + \Delta SM
$$

$$
IR_n = 4.725 - 1.2 + 0.8
$$

$$
IR_n = 4.325 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Gross Irrigation Requirement (depth):**

$$
IR_g = \frac{IR_n}{E_a} = \frac{4.325}{0.75}
$$

$$
IR_g = 5.767 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to litres per day for 2 hectares:**

For 1 mm depth on 1 hectare = 10,000 L. For 2 hectares:

$$
V_g = 5.767 \times 10{,}000 \times 2
$$

$$
V_g = 1{,}15{,}340 \; \text{L/day}
$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** The IoT system must command the sprinkler pump to deliver **115,340 L/day across the 2-hectare wheat field**, replacing the conventional flood-irrigation practice of ~200,000 L/day — a **42% water saving**.

*(Marks: (i) 2 + (ii) 2 + (iii) 2 + units/conversion 1 = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain **precision irrigation** as an IoT application. Describe the role of capacitive soil moisture sensors and solenoid valves with a closed-loop control diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vineyard deploys 20 LoRaWAN soil sensor nodes. Each node transmits a 20-byte uplink every 15 minutes using **SF7** (time-on-air ≈ 120 ms per packet). The node consumes 8 mA while transmitting and 12 µA while sleeping. The node runs on a 2400 mAh battery. Calculate: **(i)** the average current draw per cycle, **(ii)** the theoretical battery life in years. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Precision Irrigation** is the site-specific delivery of water based on real-time soil moisture data. The closed-loop has four elements:

- **Sensor:** Capacitive soil moisture probe (e.g., **DFRobot SEN0193**) outputs a 0–3 V analog voltage proportional to VWC.
- **Controller:** ESP32 reads the ADC, computes VWC via the calibration formula $VWC = \dfrac{C_{soil} - C_{dry}}{C_{sat} - C_{dry}} \times 100\%$.
- **Actuator:** A 12 V DC latching solenoid valve (e.g., Hunter PGV) governs the drip line.
- **Feedback:** VWC re-sampled after 60 s; if still below 30%, irrigation continues; else valve closes.

*(7 marks: definition 2 + sensor explanation 2 + control loop 2 + diagram reference 1)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Step-by-Step

**Given:** Cycle time $T_{cycle} = 15$ min $= 900$ s; $T_{tx} = 0.120$ s; $I_{tx} = 8$ mA; $I_{sleep} = 12$ µA $= 0.012$ mA; $C_{batt} = 2400$ mAh.

**Step 1 — Charge consumed per transmission cycle:**

$$
Q_{tx} = I_{tx} \times T_{tx} = 8 \times \frac{0.120}{3600}
$$

$$
Q_{tx} = 0.000267 \; \text{mAh per cycle}
$$

**Step 2 — Charge consumed by sleep per cycle:**

$$
Q_{sleep} = I_{sleep} \times T_{sleep} = 0.012 \times \frac{(900 - 0.120)}{3600}
$$

$$
Q_{sleep} = 0.012 \times 0.24997
$$

$$
Q_{sleep} = 0.002999 \; \text{mAh per cycle}
$$

**Step 3 — Total charge per cycle:**

$$
Q_{total} = 0.000267 + 0.002999 = 0.003266 \; \text{mAh/cycle}
$$

**Step 4 — Cycles per day:**

$$
N_{day} = \frac{24 \times 60}{15} = 96 \; \text{cycles/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Daily energy consumption:**

$$
Q_{day} = 96 \times 0.003266 = 0.3135 \; \text{mAh/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Battery life in years:**

$$
T_{life} = \frac{2400}{0.3135 \times 365} = \frac{2400}{114.43}
$$

$$
T_{life} \approx 20.97 \; \text{years}
$$

**Note (Examiner-Pleasing Remark):** In practice, battery self-discharge (≈ 2–3% per year) and temperature derating reduce this to ~**15–18 years**, which is why solar trickle-charging is often unnecessary for SF7 nodes.

*(Marks: 7 = (i) 3 + (ii) 3 + realistic caveat 1)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this high-density checklist the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **Smart Agriculture = Sensors + Network + Edge + Cloud** — four tiers, never collapse.
- ✅ **Irrigation loop latency must be ≤ 5 s** (KTU benchmark).
- ✅ **VWC threshold:** 30% trigger, 45% cutoff for most vegetables.
- ✅ **LoRaWAN** is the KTU-preferred outdoor protocol; **ZigBee** for greenhouse mesh.
- ✅ **NB-IoT** when cellular coverage exists; payload ≤ 125 bytes.
- ✅ **FAO-56 Penman–Monteith** is the canonical ET model — memorize $ET_c = K_c \times ET_0$.
- ✅ **Water balance:** $IR_n = ET_c - P_{eff} + \Delta SM$ — appears in 60% of numericals.
- ✅ **Unit conversion:** 1 mm depth on 1 hectare = **10,000 L** (1 m³) — non-negotiable.
- ✅ **Gross irrigation:** $IR_g = IR_n / E_a$ where $E_a$ is application efficiency.
- ✅ **Sensors covered:** Capacitive VWC, DHT22, NPK ion-selective, pH, NDIR CO₂, BH1750 light.
- ✅ **Actuators covered:** 12 V DC latching solenoid, motor-driven drip pump, drone sprayer, greenhouse vent motor.
- ✅ **Topologies:** Star-of-stars (LoRaWAN) vs Mesh (ZigBee).
- ✅ **Battery-life formula:** Divide cycle mAh by daily consumption; convert to years using 365.
- ✅ **Path-loss exponent for crop canopy:** $n \approx 2.7$ (mature) – $3.5$ (dense).
- ✅ **Edge layer uses TFLite/Raspberry Pi**, *not* cloud round-trip for irrigation.
- ✅ **Yield Improvement Index** $YI = \dfrac{Y_{IoT} - Y_{conv}}{Y_{conv}} \times 100\%$ is the ROI justification.
- ✅ **Common pitfalls to avoid:** Mixing VWC with gravimetric moisture; using Wi-Fi outdoors; omitting Edge layer in diagrams; unit mismatches in mm→L conversions; failing to state the latency budget explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Health and Lifestyle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST834 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Health and Lifestyle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *OECST834 - Internet of Things*, the topic **"Health and Lifestyle"** refers to the application domain of IoT that integrates smart sensors, wearable technology, cloud computing, and data analytics to monitor, manage, and improve human health, fitness, and daily living activities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *IoT for Health and Lifestyle is the network of interconnected medical and personal devices—including wearables, ambient sensors, and implantable gadgets—that collect physiological and environmental data, transmit it over the internet, and use intelligent algorithms to deliver actionable insights for preventive healthcare, chronic disease management, fitness tracking, and assisted living.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way:**
> Imagine your body is a **car dashboard**, and traditionally, you only get to check the fuel gauge (your weight) when something feels wrong. IoT in Health and Lifestyle is like fitting your car with **real-time sensors on the engine, oil, tire pressure, and GPS** — all transmitting data to a smart dashboard (your smartphone/cloud) that warns you *before* a breakdown occurs. Instead of visiting the mechanic (doctor) reactively, you are continuously monitored, and predictive maintenance (preventive healthcare) becomes possible.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following are the core metrics standardized for health-IoT applications as per industry norms (IEEE 11073, HL7, WHO standards):

- **Body Mass Index (BMI):** Standardized body fat indicator based on height and weight. Unit: $\text{kg/m}^2$.
- **Heart Rate (HR):** Beats per minute (bpm). Normal resting range: **60–100 bpm**.
- **Blood Oxygen Saturation (SpO₂):** Percentage of hemoglobin saturated with oxygen. Healthy range: **95%–100%**.
- **Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR):** Minimum calories required at rest. Unit: **kcal/day**.
- **Signal Latency for Critical Alerts:** Typically under **< 5 seconds** for life-threatening conditions.
- **Sampling Rate for ECG:** **250–500 Hz** (clinical grade).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:**
> Questions on Health and Lifestyle often test your understanding of *how* sensors capture these metrics and *which* communication protocol is suited for the application (e.g., BLE for wearables vs. Wi-Fi for ambient monitoring).

### 1.4 Core Sub-Domains of IoT in Health and Lifestyle

| Sub-Domain | Primary Focus | Example Device |
|---|---|---|
| **Fitness & Wellness** | Activity, sleep, calorie tracking | Fitbit, Mi Band, Apple Watch |
| **Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM)** | Vital sign tracking for chronic patients | Bluetooth BP monitors, glucose sensors |
| **Telemedicine & mHealth** | Remote consultation and diagnostics | Smart pill bottles, telehealth apps |
| **Assisted Living / Eldercare** | Fall detection, medication reminders | Smart fall mats, GPS trackers for dementia |
| **Clinical IoT (IoMT)** | Hospital-grade connected devices | Smart infusion pumps, connected ventilators |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of Health & Lifestyle IoT

The architecture is typically modelled as a **4-layer reference model** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus:

**Layer 1 — Perception / Sensor Layer**
- Wearable and ambient sensors capture physiological signals.
- Examples: PPG (Photoplethysmography) sensors for HR, IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) for steps, galvanic skin response (GSR) for stress.
- Key challenges: **miniaturization, low power, biocompatibility**.

**Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer**
- Short-range: **BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy)**, ZigBee, NFC, ANT+
- Long-range: **Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, 5G mMTC**
- Body Area Networks (BAN) connect on-body sensors to a gateway (smartphone).

**Layer 3 — Processing / Edge-Cloud Layer**
- Edge computing filters noise and aggregates data locally.
- Cloud platforms (e.g., AWS IoT, Azure Health) perform **ML-based anomaly detection**, trend analysis, and storage in **EHR (Electronic Health Records)**.

**Layer 4 — Application Layer**
- End-user interfaces: smartphone apps, clinician dashboards, alert systems.
- Delivers services like remote diagnostics, emergency alerts, fitness coaching.

### 2.2 Operational Workflow (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Acquisition:** Sensors sample physiological parameters at a defined frequency $f_s$.
2. **Pre-processing:** Analog signals are digitized via **ADC**; noise is filtered (e.g., moving average, Kalman filter).
3. **Local Storage:** MCU buffers data (e.g., for offline transmission).
4. **Transmission:** Encrypted data packets are sent over BLE/Wi-Fi to gateway.
5. **Cloud Processing:** Big-data analytics and ML models (e.g., LSTM for arrhythmia detection) process streams.
6. **Decision & Feedback:** Alerts are pushed to patient/caregiver; periodic reports are generated.
7. **Actuation (optional):** Smart drug delivery systems (e.g., automated insulin pumps) act based on feedback.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-yield formulas for the KTU board exam. Memorize the units and conditions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Body Mass Index (BMI)** | $\text{BMI} = \dfrac{m}{h^2}$ | $m$ = mass in kg; $h$ = height in m |
| 2 | **Heart Rate from RR interval** | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}}$ | $T_{RR}$ = R-R interval in seconds |
| 3 | **Basal Metabolic Rate (Mifflin-St Jeor, Male)** | $\text{BMR} = 10m + 6.25h - 5a + 5$ | $m$ = kg, $h$ = cm, $a$ = age in years |
| 4 | **Basal Metabolic Rate (Female)** | $\text{BMR} = 10m + 6.25h - 5a - 161$ | Same units as above |
| 5 | **Calories burned (activity)** | $C = \text{MET} \times m \times t$ | MET = metabolic equivalent; $t$ = hours |
| 6 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | $P$ = power in W |
| 7 | **Shannon Channel Capacity** | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz); $S/N$ = SNR |
| 8 | **Battery Life Estimation** | $L_{hrs} = \dfrac{C_{batt}\times V}{P_{load}}$ | $C$ in Ah; $V$ in V; $P$ in W |
| 9 | **Sampling Frequency (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | For ECG, $f_{max} \approx 150$ Hz |
| 10 | **Distance from RSSI (log-normal shadowing)** | $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{RSSI_0 - RSSI}{10n}}$ | $n$ = path loss exponent (2–4) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For the table, never use absolute value bars `|x|` inside table cells — use `\vert x \vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chronic Disease Management:** Continuous glucose monitors (CGM) like Dexcom G7 reduce HbA1c by **0.4–0.7%** in Type-1 diabetic patients (clinical evidence).
- **Eldercare:** IoT fall detection reduces emergency response time by **30–50%**.
- **Hospital IoT (IoMT):** Real-time location systems (RTLS) for equipment tracking save hospitals **~\$3,000 per bed annually** in lost-asset costs.
- **Preventive Cardiology:** Wearable ECG patches (e.g., iRhythm Zio) detect atrial fibrillation with **sensitivity > 95%**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Deriving Heart Rate from ECG R-R Interval

The **R-peak** is the most prominent deflection in the QRS complex of an ECG waveform. The time between two consecutive R-peaks is the **R-R interval**, denoted $T_{RR}$.

**Step 1 — Conceptual Setup**
Heart rate is the number of cardiac cycles per minute. If one cycle takes $T_{RR}$ seconds, then in 60 seconds the number of cycles is $60 / T_{RR}$.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Expression**

$$
\text{HR} = \frac{60}{T_{RR}} \quad \text{(beats per minute)}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example**
Let $T_{RR} = 0.8$ seconds (typical resting heart).

$$
\text{HR} = \frac{60}{0.8}
$$

$$
\text{HR} = 75 \text{ bpm}
$$

This falls within the normal **60–100 bpm** clinical range.

**Step 4 — Average HR over N intervals**
For a windowed estimate, the average $T_{RR}$ is computed first:

$$
\overline{T_{RR}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{RR,i}
$$

$$
\text{HR}_{avg} = \frac{60}{\overline{T_{RR}}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Battery Life of a Wearable Health Device

**Step 1 — Define Battery Capacity**
A typical coin-cell battery (e.g., CR2032) has:
- Capacity: $C_{batt} = 240 \text{ mAh}$
- Voltage: $V = 3 \text{ V}$

**Step 2 — Define Load Power**
Assume the wearable consumes:
- Active mode current: $I_{act} = 10 \text{ mA}$
- Sleep mode current: $I_{slp} = 0.05 \text{ mA}$
- Duty cycle: $\alpha = 1\%$ active, $(1-\alpha) = 99\%$ sleep

**Step 3 — Compute Average Current Draw**

$$
I_{avg} = \alpha \cdot I_{act} + (1-\alpha) \cdot I_{slp}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = (0.01)(10) + (0.99)(0.05)
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 0.10 + 0.0495 = 0.1495 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Average Power**

$$
P_{avg} = V \cdot I_{avg} = 3 \cdot 0.1495 = 0.4485 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Battery Life**

$$
L_{hrs} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{240}{0.1495} \approx 1605.35 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
L_{days} = \frac{1605.35}{24} \approx 66.9 \text{ days}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Aggressive duty-cycling (sleep mode dominance) extends wearable battery life by **>5×** compared to always-on operation. This is the engineering rationale behind BLE's low-power design.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sampling Rate Justification for ECG

**Step 1 — Identify Maximum Frequency Component**
The clinically relevant ECG bandwidth is $0.05$ Hz to $150$ Hz.

**Step 2 — Apply Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Industry Standard Margin**
Most clinical ECGs use $f_s = 500$ Hz to provide a safety margin and reduce aliasing.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Communication Protocol Selection

| Scenario | Recommended Protocol | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous HR streaming to watch | **BLE 5.0** | Low power, 2 Mbps peak, range ~50 m |
| Implanted glucose sensor → phone | **NFC / BLE** | Minimal energy, no battery in implant |
| Rural village vitals → cloud | **LoRaWAN / NB-IoT** | Long range (10+ km), ultra-low power |
| Hospital ICU real-time video | **Wi-Fi 6 / 5G** | High bandwidth, low latency (<10 ms) |
| Gym equipment sync | **ANT+ / BLE** | Established fitness ecosystem |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exam:** "**B**ig **L**atency **N**eeds" → **BLE** for short, **LoRa** for long, **NB-IoT** for narrowband, **Wi-Fi** for bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Architecture of an IoT Health Monitoring System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Patient Body Sensors] --> B[On-Body Gateway]
    B --> C[Bluetooth BLE Link]
    C --> D[Smartphone Edge App]
    D --> E[Cloud Platform]
    E --> F[ML Analytics Engine]
    F --> G[Clinician Dashboard]
    F --> H[Patient Mobile Alerts]
    F --> I[Emergency Services]
    E --> J[EHR Database Storage]

    subgraph SensorLayer[Layer 1 Perception]
        A
    end

    subgraph NetworkLayer[Layer 2 Network]
        B
        C
    end

    subgraph EdgeLayer[Layer 3 Edge Cloud]
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph AppLayer[Layer 4 Application]
        F
        G
        H
        I
        J
    end
```

### 4.2 Wearable Fitness Tracker — Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR[Battery 3V CR2032] --> REG[Voltage Regulator]
    REG --> MCU[Microcontroller MCU]

    HR[PPG Heart Rate Sensor] --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    IMU[Inertial Measurement Unit] --> MCU
    TEMP[Body Temperature Sensor] --> ADC
    GSR[Galvanic Skin Sensor] --> ADC

    ADC --> MCU
    MCU --> BLE[BLE 5.0 Radio]
    BLE --> PHONE[Smartphone App]
    MCU --> OLED[Display OLED]
    MCU --> VIB[Vibration Motor Alerts]
    PHONE --> CLOUD[Cloud Dashboard]
```

### 4.3 End-to-End Data Flow in Remote Patient Monitoring

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Vitals Sampled at 500Hz] --> S2[Digital Filter and Downsampling]
    S2 --> S3[Local MCU Aggregation Buffer]
    S3 --> S4{Threshold Check}
    S4 -->|Abnormal| S5[Immediate BLE Push Alert]
    S4 -->|Normal| S6[Scheduled Upload every 5 min]
    S5 --> S7[Caregiver Notification]
    S6 --> S8[Cloud Storage in HL7 FHIR Format]
    S8 --> S9[Trend Analysis LSTM Model]
    S9 --> S10[Monthly Report to Clinician]
    S9 --> S11[Predictive Risk Score]
```

### 4.4 Use-Case Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Users[User Groups]
        U1[Athletes]
        U2[Elderly Patients]
        U3[Chronic Disease Patients]
        U4[Hospital Inpatients]
    end

    subgraph Devices[IoT Devices]
        D1[Smartwatch]
        D2[Fall Detection Pendant]
        D3[CGM Glucose Monitor]
        D4[Smart Infusion Pump]
    end

    U1 --> D1
    U2 --> D2
    U3 --> D3
    U4 --> D4

    D1 --> P1[Fitness Coach App]
    D2 --> P2[Emergency Contact App]
    D3 --> P3[Diabetologist Portal]
    D4 --> P4[Nurse Station Dashboard]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** *Define IoT in the context of Health and Lifestyle applications. List any three key sub-domains.* `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**
IoT in Health and Lifestyle refers to the interconnection of medical and personal devices via the internet to monitor, analyze, and improve human health and daily activities. The system uses sensors, communication networks, and cloud analytics to deliver insights in real time.
**Three sub-domains:**
1. Fitness and wellness tracking (e.g., smartwatches).
2. Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM) for chronic diseases.
3. Assisted living and eldercare (e.g., fall detection pendants).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** *Explain the role of BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy) in wearable health devices.* `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**
BLE is a short-range, ultra-low-power wireless protocol ideal for wearables transmitting vitals to a smartphone gateway. It supports duty-cycled operation (sleep + active bursts), consumes only **~0.01–0.05 W** during transmission, and offers data rates up to **2 Mbps** in BLE 5.0, making it suitable for continuous heart rate, SpO₂, and step-count streaming without frequent recharging.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A]**
>
> **Q.A)** *(a)* Describe the **four-layer architecture of an IoT-based health monitoring system** with a neat block diagram. Clearly state the function of each layer. `[7 marks]`
>
> *(b)* A wearable ECG patch samples the heart's electrical activity at 500 Hz. The signal bandwidth extends up to 150 Hz. Using the Nyquist criterion, verify whether the sampling rate is adequate. If the R-R interval averages 0.78 s, calculate the heart rate in bpm. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four-Layer Architecture (7 marks)**

| Layer | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. Perception | Sensors (PPG, ECG, IMU, temperature) capture physiological signals. |
| 2. Network | Protocols (BLE, Wi-Fi, LoRa) transmit data to gateway/cloud. |
| 3. Edge-Cloud Processing | Data is stored, filtered, and analyzed using ML models. |
| 4. Application | End-user services — alerts, dashboards, telemedicine apps, EHR updates. |

A supporting block diagram should accompany the answer (refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes).

**(b) Nyquist Verification + Heart Rate (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Nyquist Check:**

$$
f_s = 500 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_{max} = 150 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
2 f_{max} = 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ Hz}
$$

Since $f_s = 500 \text{ Hz} \geq 300 \text{ Hz}$, the **Nyquist criterion is satisfied** with a margin of 200 Hz.

**Step 2 — Heart Rate Calculation:**

$$
\text{HR} = \frac{60}{T_{RR}} = \frac{60}{0.78}
$$

$$
\text{HR} \approx 76.92 \text{ bpm}
$$

This value lies in the normal clinical resting range of **60–100 bpm**, indicating a healthy patient.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B]**
>
> **Q.B)** *(a)* With relevant examples, explain **any four application areas** of IoT in the Health and Lifestyle domain. For each, identify the sensor used and the data transmitted. `[7 marks]`
>
> *(b)* A fitness band uses a 3 V, 240 mAh coin-cell battery. The active current is 12 mA and the sleep current is 0.04 mA. If the device operates in active mode for 1.5% of the time, estimate the **battery life in days**. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Application Areas (7 marks)**

| Application | Sensor | Data Transmitted |
|---|---|---|
| Heart-rate monitoring | PPG / ECG electrodes | HR in bpm, HRV |
| Sleep tracking | IMU (accelerometer), PPG | Sleep stages, duration, SpO₂ |
| Fall detection (eldercare) | 3-axis accelerometer, gyroscope | Sudden orientation change, impact g-force |
| Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) | Electrochemical enzymatic sensor | Interstitial glucose in mg/dL |
| Smart pill dispensers | Weight sensor + RTC | Pill removal timestamp, missed-dose alert |

**(b) Battery Life Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Duty Cycle:**
$\alpha = 0.015$ (active), $1 - \alpha = 0.985$ (sleep).

**Step 2 — Average Current:**

$$
I_{avg} = (0.015)(12) + (0.985)(0.04)
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 0.18 + 0.0394 = 0.2194 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3 — Battery Life in Hours:**

$$
L_{hrs} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{240}{0.2194} \approx 1093.89 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Days:**

$$
L_{days} = \frac{1093.89}{24} \approx 45.58 \text{ days}
$$

**Conclusion:** The fitness band can operate for approximately **45–46 days** on a single CR2032 cell under the given duty cycle.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Units mismatch** — Always write $h$ in *meters* (not cm) when computing BMI. Forgetting this yields off-by-two-orders-of-magnitude errors.
> 2. **Nyquist direction** — The theorem says $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. Writing $f_s \leq 2 f_{max}$ is a *direct contradiction* of the theorem and will fetch 0 marks.
> 3. **Duty cycle interpretation** — $\alpha$ is the *fraction* (0.015), not the percentage (1.5). Convert correctly.
> 4. **Confusing BMR with daily calorie needs** — BMR is *resting*; total daily energy expenditure (TDEE) = BMR × activity factor.
> 5. **Generic IoT answers** — For Health & Lifestyle questions, always mention *at least one* sensor and *one* protocol specifically. Vague "IoT connects devices" answers score poorly.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams** — KTU examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for neat, labelled architecture diagrams in 14-mark questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Definition:** IoT for Health and Lifestyle = smart sensors + connectivity + cloud analytics for monitoring wellbeing.
- **Four Layers:** Perception → Network → Edge-Cloud → Application.
- **Key Sub-domains:** Fitness, RPM, Telemedicine, Eldercare, IoMT.
- **Top Protocols:** **BLE** (short-range, low-power), **Wi-Fi** (high-bandwidth), **LoRaWAN/NB-IoT** (long-range, low-power), **NFC** (implantables).
- **Standard Metrics:** HR (60–100 bpm), SpO₂ (95–100%), BMI ($\text{kg/m}^2$), BMR (kcal/day), ECG sampling ≥ **300 Hz**.
- **Critical Formulas to Memorize:** BMI, HR = $60/T_{RR}$, BMR (Mifflin-St Jeor), Battery life = $C/I_{avg}$, Nyquist $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$.
- **Engineering Trade-off:** Battery life vs. sampling rate vs. data rate — optimize via duty cycling and edge processing.
- **Standards Bodies:** IEEE 11073 (medical device communication), HL7 FHIR (data exchange), WHO for clinical benchmarks.
- **Privacy Concern:** HIPAA / GDPR compliance is mandatory for any health-IoT deployment — expect a 1–2 mark sub-question on this.
- **Emerging Trend:** **Digital Twins** of patients — virtual replicas updated in real time from wearable sensor data.
- **Exam Rule of Thumb:** Always pair your answer with **(Sensor + Protocol + Use-case)** triplet for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT and M2M
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST834 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT and M2M

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Internet of Things (IoT) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a globally distributed network of uniquely identifiable physical *things* (sensors, actuators, RFID tags, embedded devices, smartphones) that are capable of sensing, gathering, capturing, and transmitting data over the Internet without requiring explicit human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction. Each "thing" is uniquely addressable (via IPv4/IPv6 or URI) and interoperable within the existing Internet infrastructure.

The term was officially coined by **Kevin Ashton** in **1999** in the context of supply chain management, and was later popularized by the **ITU Internet Reports 2005**.

## 1.2 Machine-to-Machine (M2M) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Machine-to-Machine (M2M) communication** refers to the automated, direct exchange of data between machines/devices (sensors, meters, controllers, vehicles) over a wired or wireless communication channel **without requiring human intervention**. M2M is often considered a *precursor* or *subset enabler* of the broader IoT ecosystem, typically operating over cellular (GSM/3G/4G/LTE), satellite, or short-range radio networks.

The **European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI)** standardized M2M through its **M2M Service Layer** (later rebranded as **oneM2M** in 2012).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal System"

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| **M2M** | Two factory robots on the **same assembly line** exchanging "Part Ready" signals via a dedicated wired cable | M2M is **closed, point-to-point, and pre-engineered**. It rarely touches the public Internet. |
| **IoT** | The **same factory robot** now sends its production statistics to a cloud dashboard in **another country**, which an analyst views on a smartphone | IoT is **global, IP-based, multi-protocol, and Internet-routed**. It integrates sensors, cloud, analytics, and humans-in-the-loop. |

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Intuition:**
> *All IoT contains M2M, but not all M2M is IoT.* M2M is the **conversation between machines**; IoT is the **conversation between machines and the open Internet** — a much larger, more open, and IP-centric universe.

## 1.4 IoT vs M2M — The "Big Picture" View

- **IoT = M2M + Internet + Cloud + Analytics + Open APIs**
- M2M emphasizes *device connectivity*; IoT emphasizes *service creation and intelligence* on top of that connectivity.
- M2M uses **proprietary or vertical-specific protocols** (e.g., Modbus, CAN bus, ZigBee); IoT prefers **open, IP-based, RESTful protocols** (HTTP, CoAP, MQTT, AMQP).

## 1.5 The Role of Standards Bodies

| Standards Body | Contribution |
|---|---|
| **ITU-T (Y.2060)** | Defined the IoT reference model (4-layer: devices, network, support, application) |
| **ETSI / oneM2M** | M2M and IoT service layer architecture |
| **IETF** | CoAP, 6LoWPAN, RPL routing protocols |
| **IEEE** | 802.15.4, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth standards |
| **3GPP** | Cellular IoT (NB-IoT, LTE-M) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Layer IoT Reference Model (ITU-T Y.2060)

The **ITU-T Recommendation Y.2060** defines the IoT as a structured **4-layer architectural framework** (plus 2 cross-cutting capabilities). This is a **high-yield KTU 2024 topic**.

### Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

1. **Perception / Device Layer**
   - Physical sensors, actuators, RFID readers.
   - Function: *Sensing the physical world* (temperature, motion, humidity, light).
   - Examples: DHT22, BMP280, MPU6050, HC-SR04.

2. **Network / Transport Layer**
   - Provides connectivity (Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, NB-IoT, Ethernet, 5G).
   - Performs **routing, addressing, and reliable delivery** of sensor data.
   - Bridges short-range sensor networks to the broader Internet.

3. **Support / Middleware / Service Layer**
   - Cloud platforms, message brokers, data storage, analytics engines.
   - Examples: AWS IoT Core, Google Cloud IoT, Azure IoT Hub, ThingsBoard.
   - Manages **device registry, identity, security, and data normalization**.

4. **Application Layer**
   - End-user services: smart homes, smart agriculture, industrial dashboards.
   - Provides *intelligence* to act on the data.

### Cross-Cutting Capabilities
- **Security & Privacy** (end-to-end encryption, key management)
- **Management** (device provisioning, firmware updates — FOTA/SOTA)

## 2.2 The ETSI / oneM2M M2M Architecture

The **ETSI M2M** and **oneM2M** standards define a **3-domain M2M architecture**:

1. **Device Domain** — M2M devices, area networks (PAN/LAN).
2. **Network Domain** — Access networks (PLMN, xDSL, WiMAX) + Core Network (M2M Service Capabilities).
3. **Application Domain** — M2M applications (utility meters, fleet management).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Common Services Entity (CSE)** is the heart of oneM2M. It provides 12 service capabilities such as *data management, device management, subscription/notification, group handling, security, and location* — all via **RESTful APIs**.

## 2.3 M2M vs IoT — Detailed Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | **M2M** | **IoT** |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Machine-to-Machine | Internet of Things |
| **Originator** | Industrial/telecom automation (1980s SCADA era) | Coined by Kevin Ashton, 1999 |
| **Connectivity** | Point-to-point or via cellular/satellite | IP-based, Internet-routed |
| **Communication Type** | Usually 1-to-1, machine-centric | Many-to-many, network-centric |
| **Data Destination** | Local server or SCADA master | Cloud platforms, big-data lakes |
| **Standards** | Proprietary (Modbus, CAN, PROFIBUS), ETSI M2M | Open (CoAP, MQTT, HTTP, 6LoWPAN) |
| **Addressing** | Device ID, SIM number | IPv4 / IPv6 address, URI, MAC |
| **Heterogeneity** | Low (homogeneous devices) | High (mixed vendors, mixed protocols) |
| **Intelligence Location** | At the device or local controller | In the cloud/edge/fog |
| **Scalability** | Limited to specific deployment | Highly scalable (millions of devices) |
| **Internet Dependency** | Not mandatory (can be isolated) | **Mandatory** (uses TCP/IP stack) |
| **Data Volume** | Small, structured | Massive, often unstructured (Big Data) |
| **Security** | Custom/PKI-based, isolated | Standard TLS/DTLS, OAuth, X.509 |
| **Human Interaction** | Minimal / none | Can involve humans via dashboards |
| **Examples** | Smart meter → utility server, ATM networks | Wearables → cloud analytics, smart cities |

## 2.4 Key Network and Communication Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Metric / Symbol | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $E_{tx}(k, d)$ | Energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$ | Dominant in low-power IoT |
| $E_{rx}(k)$ | Energy to receive $k$ bits | Lower than $E_{tx}$ |
| $P_{ch}$ | Channel propagation power loss | $\propto d^{\alpha}$, $\alpha \in [2, 4]$ |
| $N$ | Number of IoT devices | Up to $10^{6}$/km² (5G IoT) |
| $\lambda$ | Packet arrival rate (Poisson) | Varies by application |
| $T_{latency}$ | End-to-end delay (s) | < **1 ms** (URLLC), ~**50 ms** (mMTC) |

## 2.5 First-Order Radio Energy Model (LEACH / IoT Reference)

For a transmitter-receiver pair separated by distance $d$, the energy consumed to transmit a $k$-bit packet is:

$$
E_{tx}(k, d) \;=\; E_{tx\text{-}elec} \cdot k \;+\; E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{\alpha}
$$

where:
- $E_{tx\text{-}elec}$ = **per-bit electronics energy** (≈ **50 nJ/bit** in classic LEACH).
- $E_{amp}$ = **amplifier energy** (≈ **100 pJ/bit/m²** for free space, $\alpha = 2$).
- $d^{\alpha}$ = **path-loss term** (Friis free-space: $\alpha = 2$; multipath: $\alpha = 4$).

The receiving energy is:

$$
E_{rx}(k) \;=\; E_{rx\text{-}elec} \cdot k
$$

with $E_{rx\text{-}elec} \approx E_{tx\text{-}elec} \approx$ **50 nJ/bit**.

> [!NOTE]
> This model is **high-yield for KTU problems** in Module 2 / Module 4 (Wireless Sensor Networks). It is the basis for LEACH, PEGASIS, and HEED clustering protocols.

## 2.6 Communication Protocols — At a Glance

| Protocol | Layer (OSI) | Use Case | Transport | Energy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi (802.11)** | Data Link / Physical | Indoor high bandwidth | TCP/IP | High |
| **Bluetooth / BLE** | Data Link | Wearables, beacons | Custom | Low |
| **ZigBee (802.15.4)** | Data Link | Home automation, mesh | Custom | Very Low |
| **LoRaWAN** | MAC | Long-range, low data | LoRa | Ultra-Low |
| **NB-IoT** | Cellular | Smart metering | LTE | Low |
| **MQTT** | Application | Pub/Sub messaging | TCP | Low |
| **CoAP** | Application | RESTful constrained devices | UDP | Very Low |
| **HTTP/HTTPS** | Application | Web dashboards | TCP | High |
| **Modbus** | Application | Industrial SCADA | Serial/TCP | Medium |
| **CAN bus** | Data Link | Automotive | Differential signaling | Low |

## 2.7 M2M Communication Flow (Step-by-Step)

1. **Sensor / Device** captures an event (e.g., temperature = 42°C).
2. **Embedded processor** encodes the value into a data packet.
3. **Communication module** (GSM/LoRa/NB-IoT) transmits to a **remote server**.
4. **M2M platform** parses, stores, and analyzes the data.
5. **Action** is triggered — either autonomous (e.g., shut down a valve) or alert-based (notify a technician).
6. **No human is in the loop** at any stage — this is the defining property of M2M.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids:** M2M smart meters report consumption to utility; IoT analytics predict demand.
- **Connected Vehicles:** M2M handles OBD-II telemetry; IoT enables V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) over 5G.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** M2M streams sensor readings; IoT ML models predict failures.
- **Telemedicine:** M2M transmits vitals from patient wearables; IoT dashboards let doctors interact globally.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Optimal Cluster Radius and Energy Trade-Off

A classical **KTU problem** in IoT/M2M wireless sensor networks asks you to compute the energy consumed per round and find the optimal hop-distance or number of clusters. We now derive the **First-Order Radio Energy** step by step.

### Step 1 — Define the problem

We have $N$ sensor nodes uniformly distributed in an $M \times M$ square region. Each node transmits $k$ bits per round to a Base Station (BS) located at distance $d_{toBS}$ either **directly** or via a **Cluster Head (CH)**.

### Step 2 — Transmit energy equation (from § 2.5)

$$
E_{tx}(k, d) \;=\; E_{elec} \cdot k \;+\; E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{\alpha}
$$

### Step 3 — Receive energy equation

$$
E_{rx}(k) \;=\; E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

### Step 4 — Total energy per round for a single node

The energy a non-CH node spends transmitting to its CH at distance $d_{toCH}$:

$$
E_{non\text{-}CH} \;=\; k \cdot E_{elec} \;+\; k \cdot E_{amp} \cdot d_{toCH}^{\alpha}
$$

The energy a CH spends **receiving** $N-1$ member packets, aggregating them, and forwarding to the BS:

$$
E_{CH} \;=\; (N - 1) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} \;+\; k \cdot E_{DA} \cdot N \;+\; k \cdot E_{elec} \;+\; k \cdot E_{amp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{\alpha}
$$

where $E_{DA}$ is the **data-aggregation energy per bit** (typical value: **5 nJ/bit**).

### Step 5 — Substituting standard LEACH values

Let:
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit
- $E_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m² (free space, $\alpha = 2$)
- $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit
- $k = 4000$ bits (typical LEACH packet)
- $N = 100$ nodes
- $M = 100$ m → region side
- $d_{toBS} = 75$ m (BS at center of edge)

### Step 6 — Compute average $d_{toCH}$

For $N$ nodes uniformly random in an $M \times M$ area, expected squared distance to a CH is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[d_{toCH}^{2}] \;=\; \frac{M^{2}}{2 \pi N_{opt}} \cdot N_{opt} \;=\; \frac{M^{2}}{2 \pi}
$$

Wait — let us carefully recompute using the LEACH derivation.

For $N_{c}$ clusters in an $M \times M$ region, the **average node-to-CH distance** is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[d_{toCH}^{2}] \;=\; \frac{M^{2}}{2 \pi N_{c}}
$$

### Step 7 — Numerical substitution

$$
\mathbb{E}[d_{toCH}^{2}] \;=\; \frac{100^{2}}{2 \pi \cdot 5} \;=\; \frac{10000}{31.416} \;\approx\; 318.31 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Taking the square root:

$$
\mathbb{E}[d_{toCH}] \;\approx\; \sqrt{318.31} \;\approx\; 17.84 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 8 — Compute $E_{non\text{-}CH}$

$$
E_{non\text{-}CH} \;=\; (4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9}) \;+\; (4000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 17.84^{2})
$$

$$
E_{non\text{-}CH} \;=\; 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \;+\; 4000 \cdot 10^{-10} \cdot 318.31
$$

$$
E_{non\text{-}CH} \;=\; 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \;+\; 1.273 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{non\text{-}CH} \;\approx\; 3.273 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}}
$$

### Step 9 — Compute $E_{CH}$ (for $N - 1 = 99$ member nodes)

$$
E_{CH} \;=\; 99 \cdot 4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} \;+\; 4000 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 100 \;+\; 4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} \;+\; 4000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 75^{2}
$$

Compute each term in order:

$$
T_{1} \;=\; 99 \cdot 4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} \;=\; 99 \cdot 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 1.98 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J}
$$

$$
T_{2} \;=\; 4000 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 100 \;=\; 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}
$$

$$
T_{3} \;=\; 4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} \;=\; 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

$$
T_{4} \;=\; 4000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 5625 \;=\; 4.0 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 5625 \;=\; 2.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}
$$

Summing:

$$
\boxed{E_{CH} \;\approx\; 1.98 \times 10^{-2} \;+\; 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \;+\; 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \;+\; 2.25 \times 10^{-3} \;\approx\; 2.425 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Amplification:** $E_{CH}$ is **~74 times** larger than $E_{non\text{-}CH}$. This is precisely why **cluster head rotation (LEACH)** is critical — to distribute this high energy cost equitably among all nodes.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — IoT vs M2M Decision Framework

| Engineering Question | Use M2M | Use IoT |
|---|---|---|
| Are devices isolated from the public Internet? | ✓ | ✗ |
| Need to scale to millions of devices? | ✗ | ✓ |
| Need cloud-based analytics & ML? | ✗ | ✓ |
| Latency-sensitive (sub-ms) control loop? | ✓ | Either |
| Custom SCADA / Modbus protocol? | ✓ | ✗ |
| Open RESTful APIs (HTTP/CoAP)? | ✗ | ✓ |
| Heavy compliance (e.g., industrial safety)? | ✓ | Either |
| Heterogeneous device ecosystem? | ✗ | ✓ |

## 3.3 Throughput Estimation for an IoT Gateway

Consider a gateway serving $N = 500$ sensors, each reporting a payload of $k = 128$ bytes every $T = 60$ s.

**Step 1 — Bits per node per second**

$$
R_{node} \;=\; \frac{k \cdot 8}{T} \;=\; \frac{128 \cdot 8}{60} \;\approx\; 17.07 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate gateway throughput**

$$
R_{agg} \;=\; N \cdot R_{node} \;=\; 500 \cdot 17.07 \;\approx\; 8.53 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3 — With 50% protocol overhead**

$$
R_{eff} \;=\; R_{agg} \cdot 1.5 \;\approx\; 12.8 \text{ kbps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is *extremely* low compared to typical Wi-Fi (54 Mbps) or 5G (1 Gbps) capacities — confirming that **IoT is a *low-bandwidth, high-density* regime**, ideal for low-power protocols like LoRa (0.3–50 kbps) and NB-IoT (26 kbps DL / 62 kbps UL).

## 3.4 Numerical Q — Path-Loss Calculation

Using the **Log-Distance Path Loss Model**:

$$
PL(d) \;=\; PL(d_{0}) \;+\; 10 \alpha \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_{0}}\right) \;+\; X_{\sigma}
$$

Given $PL(d_{0} = 1\text{ m}) = 40$ dB, $\alpha = 3$, and $d = 100$ m:

$$
PL(100) \;=\; 40 \;+\; 10 \cdot 3 \cdot \log_{10}(100) \;=\; 40 \;+\; 60 \;=\; 100 \text{ dB}
$$

So an **IoT receiver at 100 m** in a multipath-rich indoor environment suffers **100 dB of path loss**, requiring careful link-budget design.
