> **Conclusion:** A 20-byte LoRaWAN packet at SF10/BW125 takes approximately **200 ms** on air. At SF12, this would increase to roughly **2.5 seconds** — illustrating the **range-vs-latency trade-off** central to LPWAN design.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary: Power Consumption

The average current draw of a LoRaWAN end-device is dominated by the **transmit current** and the **sleep current**. Approximate values for an SX1276-based module:

| State | Current | Duration | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sleep (RTC running)** | ~1 µA | 99.9% of time | Critical for 5–10 year battery life |
| **Transmit (TX)** | ~100 mA | 100–2500 ms | Depends on SF and payload |
| **Receive (RX)** | ~10 mA | 50–500 ms | Class A windows (RX1 + RX2) |
| **Processing** | ~5 mA | 5–20 ms | MCU + crypto |

For a device transmitting once per hour at SF10:

$$
\text{Energy per transmission} = P_{TX} \times T_{TX} = 3.3 \text{ V} \times 0.1 \text{ A} \times 0.2 \text{ s} = 0.066 \text{ J}
$$

For 24 transmissions per day (one per hour), the annual energy:

$$
E_{annual} = 0.066 \text{ J} \times 24 \times 365 = 578.16 \text{ J/year}
$$

A 2400 mAh Li-SOCl₂ battery stores:

$$
E_{battery} = 3.6 \text{ V} \times 2.4 \text{ A} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 31{,}104 \text{ J}
$$

Battery life (theoretical, ignoring sleep current and self-discharge):

$$
\text{Life} = \frac{31{,}104}{578.16} \approx 53.8 \text{ years}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Reality:** Actual battery life is **5–10 years** because of sleep current (~1 µA accumulates to ~31 mAh/year), battery self-discharge (~1% per year), and cold-temperature derating.

---

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

```mermaid

        ed1["END DEVICE A\nClass A - Battery"]
```

## 4.4 LPWAN Design Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["START: New IoT Deployment"] --> q1{"Need mobility or\nVoLTE voice?"}
    q1 -->|Yes| lte1["Use LTE-M Cat-M1\n1.4 MHz BW, full LTE"]
    q1 -->|No| q2{"Is licensed spectrum\navailable / affordable?"}
    q2 -->|No| q3{"Need downlink\nfirmware updates?"}
    q2 -->|Yes| q4{"Need >50 dB indoor\npenetration?"}
    q3 -->|Yes| lora1["Use LoRaWAN\nClass B or C"]
    q3 -->|No| q5{"Need 50 km rural range\nwith extreme low power?"}
    q5 -->|Yes| sig1["Use Sigfox\nUNB, 140 msg/day limit"]
    q5 -->|No| lora2["Use LoRaWAN\nClass A, SF7-SF10"]
    q4 -->|Yes| nbiot1["Use NB-IoT\n164 dB MCL, in-building"]
    q4 -->|No| nbiot2["Use NB-IoT or LTE-M\nBased on mobility need"]
```

## 4.5 Spreading Factor vs Range Trade-off (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sf7["SF7\nRange: ~2 km\nDR: 5470 bps\nSensitivity: -123 dBm"] -->|"Add ~2.5 dB,\n~1 km, halve DR"| sf8["SF8\nRange: ~3 km\nDR: 3125 bps\nSensitivity: -126 dBm"]
    sf8 -->|"Trade-off continues"| sf9["SF9\nRange: ~4 km\nDR: 1758 bps\nSensitivity: -129 dBm"]
    sf9 -->|"Trade-off continues"| sf10["SF10\nRange: ~5 km\nDR: 938 bps\nSensitivity: -132 dBm"]
    sf10 -->|"Trade-off continues"| sf11["SF11\nRange: ~7 km\nDR: 469 bps\nSensitivity: -134.5 dBm"]
    sf11 -->|"Trade-off continues"| sf12["SF12\nRange: ~10 km\nDR: 293 bps\nSensitivity: -137 dBm"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Insight:** Each step in Spreading Factor roughly doubles the time-on-air (halves data rate) but improves sensitivity by **2.5–3 dB**, which translates to a **~1 km range increase** in typical urban conditions.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution (as per Board Regulations):**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short answer questions (answer in ~3–4 sentences with diagrams).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark long answer (two sub-parts of 7 marks each, internal choice provided).
> - All questions map to **Course Outcomes** (CO2/CO3 typical for Module 2 networking) and **Revised Bloom's Levels** (L1 Remember, L2 Understand, L3 Apply, L4 Analyze).

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define LPWAN and list its three primary characteristics. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**LPWAN (Low Power Wide Area Network)** is a category of wireless communication technology designed to enable long-range communication (2–10 km urban, up to 50 km rural) for low-power, low-data-rate IoT devices that operate on battery power for years.

**Three Primary Characteristics:**

1. **Long Range** — Communication over distances of 2–10 km in urban areas and up to 50 km in rural line-of-sight conditions.
2. **Ultra-Low Power Consumption** — End-device battery lifetimes of 5–10+ years on a single cell.
3. **Low Data Rate** — Typically 0.3 kbps to 50 kbps, sufficient for small, infrequent telemetry packets.

*(Word count target: ~80–100 words. Add a labelled diagram of LPWAN range vs data rate comparison for full marks.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between LoRa and LoRaWAN. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | LoRa | LoRaWAN |
|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | Physical Layer (PHY) | MAC and Network Layer |
| **Function** | Defines the radio modulation (Chirp Spread Spectrum) | Defines the communication protocol, device classes, and security |
| **Owner** | Semtech (proprietary chip design) | LoRa Alliance (open standard) |
| **Example Role** | "How the signal is sent over the air" | "How the device joins, transmits, and is managed by the network" |

**In short:** LoRa is the **modulation technique**; LoRaWAN is the **networking protocol** built **on top of LoRa**. LoRaWAN cannot exist without LoRa, but LoRa can technically be used without LoRaWAN (e.g., in proprietary point-to-point systems).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Format:** Each Part B question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, each carrying **7 marks**. Internal choice is offered — students attempt **either** sub-part (a) OR sub-part (b) where applicable. The model solutions below show **full working** for all sub-parts.

---

### **Question A: [14 Marks]** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the LoRaWAN protocol stack. Discuss the three device classes (A, B, C) with neat diagrams, and state the role of the Network Server in Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) management. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A LoRaWAN end-device transmits a 30-byte payload with Spreading Factor SF=9, Bandwidth = 125 kHz, and Coding Rate = 4/5. Compute the LoRa data rate, symbol time, and approximate time-on-air. Discuss how changing SF to 12 affects range, data rate, and battery life. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. LoRaWAN Protocol Stack (2 marks):**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│   APPLICATION LAYER (Private IoT)   │  ← Sensor data, business logic
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│   LORAWAN MAC LAYER                 │  ← Frame format, encryption
│   (LoRaWAN L1.0.4 specification)    │  ← Join procedure, ADR
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│   LORA PHY LAYER                    │  ← Chirp Spread Spectrum
│   (Semtech SX127x / SX126x)         │  ← Sub-GHz ISM radio
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│   RF FRONT END                      │  ← Antenna + matching
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**2. Three LoRaWAN Device Classes (3 marks):**

- **Class A — Bi-directional, lowest power:**
  - Downlink **only** available in two receive windows (RX1, RX2) **after** an uplink.
  - Energy profile: spends >99% time in sleep.
  - **Use case:** Battery-powered sensors (soil moisture, water meters).
  - Diagram: `Uplink → RX1 (1s after) → RX2 (2s after) → Sleep`

- **Class B — Scheduled downlink slots:**
  - Class A functionality **plus** synchronized downlink windows opened at scheduled intervals driven by gateway beacons.
  - Energy profile: still battery-friendly, slightly higher than Class A.
  - **Use case:** Actuators requiring periodic downlink commands (e.g., valve control).

- **Class C — Continuously open receive:**
  - Class A functionality **plus** receive window that is **always open** when not transmitting.
  - Energy profile: **highest power**, typically mains-powered.
  - **Use case:** Streetlight controllers, smart plugs with continuous control.

**3. Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) — Role of Network Server (2 marks):**

- The Network Server monitors **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** and **Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)** of every uplink from each end-device.
- Based on link margin, the server **commands** the end-device to:
  - **Increase SF** if link margin is small (improve sensitivity).
  - **Decrease SF** if link margin is large (improve data rate, reduce time-on-air).
- ADR ensures the entire network operates at the **optimal balance** between range, reliability, and battery life.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $SF = 9$, $BW = 125{,}000 \text{ Hz}$, $CR = 4/5 = 0.8$, $PL = 30 \text{ bytes}$.

**Step 1 — Data Rate (2 marks):**

$$
DR = \frac{SF \times BW \times CR}{2^{SF}} = \frac{9 \times 125{,}000 \times 0.8}{2^9}
$$

$$
DR = \frac{900{,}000}{512} = 1757.8125 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 2 — Symbol Time (1 mark):**

$$
T_{sym} = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW} = \frac{2^9}{125{,}000} = \frac{512}{125{,}000} = 4.096 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Approximate Time-on-Air (3 marks):**

Preamble duration (using standard 8-symbol preamble + 4.25 sync symbols):

$$
T_{preamble} = (8 + 4.25) \times T_{sym} = 12.25 \times 4.096 \text{ ms} = 50.18 \text{ ms}
$$

Payload symbol count (with $CRC=1$, $IH=0$, $DE=0$ for SF=9):

$$
8PL - 4SF + 28 + 16CRC - 20IH = 8(30) - 4(9) + 28 + 16 - 0 = 240 - 36 + 28 + 16 = 248
$$

$$
\frac{248}{4(9-0)} = \frac{248}{36} = 6.889
$$

With $CR = 0.8$: $6.889 \times 0.8 = 5.511$, ceiling = **6**.

$$
n_{payload} = 8 + 6 = 14 \text{ symbols}
$$

$$
T_{payload} = 14 \times 4.096 = 57.34 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{packet} = T_{preamble} + T_{payload} = 50.18 + 57.34 = 107.52 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Answer:** $DR \approx 1758 \text{ bps}$, $T_{sym} = 4.096 \text{ ms}$, $T_{packet} \approx 107.5 \text{ ms}$.

**Step 4 — Effect of Changing SF from 9 to 12 (1 mark):**

- **Range:** Increases from ~4 km to ~10 km (sensitivity improves from −129 dBm to −137 dBm, a gain of 8 dB).
- **Data rate:** Drops by factor $2^3 = 8$, from 1758 bps to ~293 bps.
- **Time-on-air:** Increases by factor of 8, from 107 ms to ~860 ms (8× longer transmission).
- **Battery life:** **Degrades significantly** due to longer TX time. Each packet consumes ~8× more energy, reducing battery life by approximately the same factor.

---

### **Question B: [14 Marks]** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Compare LoRaWAN, Sigfox, and NB-IoT across the following parameters: spectrum, modulation, bandwidth, data rate, range, battery life, and typical use cases. Present the answer in a comparison table. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the LoRaWAN network architecture with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of each component: end-device, gateway, network server, and application server. What security mechanisms does LoRaWAN use for confidentiality and integrity? **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (6 marks):**

| Parameter | LoRaWAN | Sigfox | NB-IoT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spectrum** | Unlicensed ISM (868/915 MHz) | Unlicensed ISM (868/915 MHz) | Licensed LTE band (700–900 MHz) |
| **Modulation** | Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) | Ultra-Narrowband BPSK | QPSK (LTE-based) |
| **Bandwidth** | 125 / 250 / 500 kHz | 100 Hz / 600 Hz (UNB) | 180 kHz |
| **Data Rate** | 0.3 – 50 kbps | ~100 bps | ~26 kbps DL / ~62 kbps UL |
| **Range (Urban)** | 2 – 5 km | 3 – 10 km | 1 – 5 km (deep indoor) |
| **Range (Rural)** | 10 – 15 km | 30 – 50 km | 15 – 25 km |
| **Battery Life** | 5 – 10 years | 10 – 12+ years | 5 – 10 years |
| **MCL (dB)** | ~157 | ~162 | **164** |
| **Module Cost** | $4 – $10 | $3 – $5 | $8 – $15 |
| **Deployment Cost** | Gateways needed | Sigfox operator subscription | Requires 4G towers |
| **Mobility Support** | Limited | No | No (no handover) |
| **Typical Use Cases** | Smart agriculture, smart cities, environmental monitoring | Asset tracking, low-cost sensors, parking sensors | Smart metering, indoor sensors, building automation |

**Discussion (1 mark):**

- **LoRaWAN** is best when the user wants to **own and operate** their own private network with full control.
- **Sigfox** is best for **ultra-low-cost, ultra-low-data** applications using operator-managed infrastructure.
- **NB-IoT** is best for **deep indoor penetration** and integration with existing **4G cellular networks** owned by telecom operators.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**1. LoRaWAN Network Architecture with Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[End-Devices: A, B, C] --LoRa Radio--> [Gateway 1]
                                          |
                       +-----------+--------+--------+
                       |           |                 |
                   [Gateway 2]   [Gateway 3]    ...   (Multiple gateways per area for redundancy)
                       |           |                 |
                       +-----------+--------+--------+
                                    |
                          [Network Server]
                          (Join, ADR, Security,
                           Deduplication, Routing)
                                    |
                                    v
                          [Application Server 1]   [Application Server 2]
                          (Smart Metering)         (Asset Tracking)
```

**2. Role of Each Component (3 marks):**

- **End-Device (Sensor/Actuator Node):** Senses the physical world (e.g., temperature, soil moisture), creates a LoRa packet, and transmits it. Cannot decrypt downlink unless it holds the correct session keys. Class A/B/C determines its downlink behaviour.
- **Gateway (Concentrator):** A **transparent bridge** that receives LoRa packets and forwards them over IP backhaul (Ethernet/4G) to the Network Server. It does **not** process application data. A single area can have multiple gateways for **geographic diversity** — all receiving the same uplink packet.
- **Network Server (NS):** The **brain** of the network. Manages:
  - **MAC commands** (link checks, ADR).
  - **Deduplication** of packets received by multiple gateways.
  - **Security** (NwkSKey, AppSKey management).
  - **Routing** of payloads to the correct application server.
- **Application Server:** The **business logic layer** that decrypts the application payload using AppSKey and processes it (e.g., stores in database, triggers alarm, sends user notification).

**3. LoRaWAN Security Mechanisms (1 mark):**

- **Two AES-128 keys per device:**
  - **NwkSKey (Network Session Key):** Used to verify **message integrity** (MIC — Message Integrity Code) and encrypt MAC commands.
  - **AppSKey (Application Session Key):** Used to **encrypt the application payload** (end-to-end between device and application server — gateway cannot read it).
- **Device Address (DevAddr):** A 32-bit identifier assigned during the Join procedure.
- **Join Procedure (OTAA):** Over-The-Air Activation uses a DevNonce and AppKey for secure key derivation during each session join.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing LoRa with LoRaWAN:** LoRa is **physical layer only**; LoRaWAN is the **MAC/network layer protocol**. Markers deduct 1–2 marks for using the terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the "WAN" in LoRaWAN:** Many students forget that LoRaWAN supports a **star-of-stars topology** with a Network Server; they incorrectly describe it as a point-to-point link.
> 3. **Wrong units in link budget:** Mixing **dB** (dimensionless ratios) with **dBm** (absolute power). Always remember: $P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{mW})$.
> 4. **Confusing SF direction:** Higher SF = **longer range, lower data rate, longer time-on-air, lower battery life** (NOT the other way around). Markers specifically check this in 14-mark problems.
> 5. **Not mentioning the spectrum difference:** LoRaWAN/Sigfox = **unlicensed ISM**; NB-IoT/LTE-M = **licensed cellular**. This is a 2–3 mark distinction in Part B.
> 6. **Ignoring Class A receive windows:** Class A devices can only receive downlink **after** an uplink, in two specific windows. Many students incorrectly assume Class A devices can receive at any time.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before exam):**

### Core Concepts
- **LPWAN** = **Long Range, Low Power, Low Data Rate, Low Cost, Massive Scalability**.
- Trade-off: Sacrifices data rate and latency for range and battery life.
- Frequency bands: **868 MHz (EU), 915 MHz (US), 433 MHz (Asia)** for unlicensed LPWAN.
- Two main categories: **Unlicensed (LoRaWAN, Sigfox)** and **Licensed Cellular (NB-IoT, LTE-M)**.

### LoRaWAN Essentials
- **LoRa = PHY (Chirp Spread Spectrum, Semtech).**
- **LoRaWAN = MAC + Network (LoRa Alliance).**
- **Spreading Factor SF7 to SF12** controls the range/data rate trade-off.
- **Three Device Classes:** A (battery, lowest power), B (scheduled downlink), C (mains, continuous RX).
- **ADR (Adaptive Data Rate)** is managed by the **Network Server**, not the device.
- **Two AES-128 keys:** NwkSKey (integrity), AppSKey (payload encryption).
- **Star-of-stars topology:** End-Device → Gateway (transparent) → Network Server → Application Server.

### NB-IoT Essentials
- **3GPP Release 13** standard, uses **180 kHz** bandwidth (one LTE resource block).
- **Three deployment modes:** In-band, Guard-band, Standalone.
- **MCL = 164 dB** (highest in LPWAN family).
- **Coverage Enhancement (CE) levels 0, 1, 2** with up to 128 repetitions.
- **No mobility/handover** support (use LTE-M if mobility is required).

### Sigfox Essentials
- **Ultra-Narrowband (UNB)** modulation with BPSK.
- **Strict duty cycle limits:** 140 UL + 8 DL messages per day.
- **Small payload:** 12 bytes UL, 8 bytes DL.
- **Operator-managed** network (you do not deploy your own Sigfox base stations).

### LTE-M (Cat-M1) Essentials
- **1.4 MHz** bandwidth, full LTE features.
- **Mobility support** with handover — the only LPWAN with this feature.
- **Supports VoLTE** (voice over LTE) — unique among LPWANs.
- Higher data rate (~1 Mbps DL) and lower latency (~50–100 ms).
- Higher power consumption than NB-IoT.

### Key Formulas (Memorize!)
- **LoRa Data Rate:** $DR = \frac{SF \times BW \times CR}{2^{SF}}$
- **Symbol Time:** $T_{sym} = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW}$
- **Free-Space Path Loss:** $L_{FSPL} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (d in km, f in MHz)
- **Log-Distance Path Loss:** $L_{LD} = L_0 + 10 n \log_{10}(d/d_0)$
- **Link Budget:** $P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{path} - L_{system} + G_{RX}$

### Quick Decision Rule (Design Question)
- **Need mobility or voice?** → **LTE-M**
- **Need deep indoor penetration on cellular?** → **NB-IoT**
- **Need lowest cost + private network?** → **LoRaWAN**
- **Need 50 km rural range + extreme low power + no downlink?** → **Sigfox**

### Key Numeric Facts (Examiner Trivia)
- LoRaWAN SF12 sensitivity: **−137 dBm**
- LoRaWAN max MCL: **~157 dB**
- NB-IoT max MCL: **164 dB**
- Sigfox max MCL: **~162 dB**
- NB-IoT bandwidth: **180 kHz**
- LTE-M bandwidth: **1.4 MHz**
- Sigfox daily message limit: **140 uplink + 8 downlink**
- LoRaWAN SF range: **7 to 12** (6 values)
- LoRaWAN device classes: **3** (A, B, C)
- LoRaWAN key types: **2** (NwkSKey, AppSKey) — both **128-bit AES**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy Tip:** In every 14-mark question, draw a **labelled diagram** of the LoRaWAN star-of-stars topology and include a **comparison table** — these alone can fetch **3–4 marks** without writing a single line of text, even if your written explanation is incomplete.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cellular (3GPP) and Non-3GPP Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST834 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cellular (3GPP) and Non-3GPP Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**3GPP (3rd Generation Partnership Project)** is a global collaborative standardization organization that unites seven regional telecommunications standard development organizations (ARIB, ATIS, CCSA, TSDSI, TTA, TTC, and TTA). It is responsible for defining the technical specifications and standards for **cellular mobile communication networks**, including GSM, UMTS, LTE, 5G NR, and the specialized cellular IoT variants: **LTE-M (Long-Term Evolution for Machines)** and **NB-IoT (Narrowband Internet of Things)**.

**Non-3GPP Standards** refer to all wireless communication technologies that operate **outside the cellular spectrum management framework of 3GPP**. These standards use unlicensed Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio bands or proprietary/licensed spectrum and include technologies such as **LoRaWAN (Long Range Wide Area Network), Sigfox, Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), Bluetooth/BLE (IEEE 802.15.1), Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4), Z-Wave, Thread, and NFC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In IoT context, 3GPP standards primarily cover **wide-area, high-mobility, operator-managed** connectivity (e.g., smart metering, asset tracking, vehicle telematics), while Non-3GPP standards serve **short-range, low-power, local/personal area networking** requirements (e.g., home automation, wearables, industrial sensors).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **postal and courier system** for the modern smart city:

- **3GPP Cellular Standards (3G/4G/5G/LTE-M/NB-IoT)** are like the **national postal service** — heavily regulated, licensed, requires a government-granted operating license, works across vast distances, provides guaranteed service-level agreements (SLAs), and incurs subscription costs. The "post offices" (base stations/eNodeB/gNodeB) are owned and operated by licensed telecom carriers.

- **Non-3GPP Standards** are like **local courier services and personal messengers** — anyone can set them up using freely available radio frequencies (unlicensed ISM bands), they are excellent for short to medium distances, they cost less to deploy, and they come in many varieties optimized for different needs (LoRa for long-range low-power, Wi-Fi for high bandwidth, BLE for low-energy personal devices, Zigbee for mesh networks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The fundamental divide between 3GPP and Non-3GPP is **Spectrum Licensing, Coverage Architecture, and QoS Guarantees**. 3GPP uses **licensed spectrum** with carrier-managed infrastructure; Non-3GPP typically uses **unlicensed spectrum** with self-managed or community infrastructure.

### 1.3 Why Both Exist in IoT

The IoT ecosystem requires a **multi-tier connectivity hierarchy** because no single technology can simultaneously optimize for:
- **Long Range** (kilometers)
- **Low Power** (years of battery life)
- **High Data Rate** (Mbps+)
- **Low Cost** (sub-$5 modules)
- **Massive Density** (millions of devices per km²)

This is why 3GPP and Non-3GPP standards **coexist and complement** each other in the IoT connectivity stack.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Licensed Cellular Spectrum:** Operates in bands like **700 MHz, 800 MHz, 900 MHz, 1800 MHz, 2100 MHz, 2600 MHz, 3.5 GHz (5G)**
- **Unlicensed ISM Bands:** **433 MHz, 868 MHz (Europe), 915 MHz (USA), 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 60 GHz**
- **1 Byte = 8 bits**, **1 kbps = 1000 bits per second**
- **Path Loss Exponent (n):** Typically 2 (free space) to 4 (urban) for outdoor propagation
- **Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL):** 164 dB for NB-IoT, 156 dB for LTE-M

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 3GPP Cellular IoT Standards — Layered Architecture

The 3GPP family tree, as it relates to IoT, follows this evolution:

| Generation | Technology | IoT Variant | Key Use Case |
|------------|------------|-------------|--------------|
| 2G | GSM/GPRS | EC-GSM-IoT | Legacy telemetry |
| 3G | UMTS/HSPA | (Limited IoT support) | Mobile broadband |
| 4G | LTE | **LTE-M (Cat-M1)**, **NB-IoT (Cat-NB1/NB2)** | Massive IoT |
| 5G | NR | **eMBB, URLLC, mMTC** | Industrial IoT, autonomous systems |

### 2.2 LTE-M (Long-Term Evolution for Machines) — Cat-M1

- **Bandwidth:** 1.4 MHz (6 Resource Blocks)
- **Peak Data Rate:** ~300 kbps (uplink) / 375 kbps (downlink)
- **Duplex Mode:** Half-duplex FDD (Frequency Division Duplex)
- **Latency:** 10–15 ms
- **Mobility:** Full mobility support (handover between cells)
- **Voice Support:** VoLTE (Voice over LTE) supported
- **Power Saving:** PSM (Power Saving Mode) and eDRX (extended Discontinuous Reception) up to ~44 minutes

### 2.3 NB-IoT (Narrowband Internet of Things) — Cat-NB1

- **Bandwidth:** 180 kHz (1 Resource Block)
- **Peak Data Rate:** ~26 kbps (DL) / 62 kbps (UL) for multi-tone
- **Duplex Mode:** Half-duplex FDD
- **Latency:** 1.6–10 seconds (designed for delay-tolerant applications)
- **Mobility:** Limited (no handover between cells; cell reselection only)
- **Coverage:** MCL of **164 dB** (20 dB better than standard LTE)
- **Capacity:** Up to **50,000 devices per cell**

### 2.4 Non-3GPP Standards — Detailed Breakdown

#### 2.4.1 LoRaWAN (Long Range Wide Area Network)
- **Physical Layer:** LoRa modulation (CSS — Chirp Spread Spectrum) by Semtech
- **MAC Layer:** LoRaWAN (open specification by LoRa Alliance)
- **Frequency Bands:** 868 MHz (EU), 915 MHz (US), 433 MHz (Asia)
- **Range:** 2–5 km (urban), up to 15 km (rural/line-of-sight)
- **Data Rate:** 0.3 kbps to 50 kbps (adaptive)
- **Spreading Factors:** SF7 to SF12 (trade-off between range and data rate)
- **Device Classes:** Class A (lowest power), Class B (scheduled downlink), Class C (continuous listen)

#### 2.4.2 Sigfox
- **Modulation:** Ultra-Narrow Band (UNB) BPSK
- **Frequency:** 868 MHz (EU) / 902 MHz (US)
- **Data Rate:** 100–600 bps (extremely low)
- **Payload Size:** 12 bytes (uplink), 8 bytes (downlink)
- **Daily Limit:** 140 uplink messages per device
- **Range:** 30–50 km (rural), 3–10 km (urban)

#### 2.4.3 Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)
- **Standards:** 802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E/7)
- **Frequency:** 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz
- **Range:** 30–100 m (indoor)
- **Data Rate:** Up to 9.6 Gbps (Wi-Fi 6)
- **Power:** High consumption; **Wi-Fi HaLow (802.11ah)** sub-1 GHz version for IoT

#### 2.4.4 Bluetooth & BLE (IEEE 802.15.1)
- **Frequency:** 2.4 GHz ISM
- **Range:** 10 m (BLE 5.x: up to 400 m in coded PHY mode)
- **Data Rate:** 1 Mbps (BLE 4.2), 2 Mbps (BLE 5.0)
- **Topology:** Piconet (master-slave), Mesh (BLE Mesh)
- **Power:** Designed for coin-cell battery operation

#### 2.4.5 Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4)
- **Frequency:** 2.4 GHz (worldwide), 868 MHz (EU), 915 MHz (US)
- **Data Rate:** 250 kbps (2.4 GHz)
- **Range:** 10–100 m
- **Topology:** Star, Tree, Mesh (up to 65,000 nodes)
- **Application Layer:** Zigbee PRO, Zigbee 3.0, Zigbee RF4CE

#### 2.4.6 Z-Wave
- **Frequency:** 908.42 MHz (US), 868.42 MHz (EU)
- **Data Rate:** 9.6/40/100 kbps
- **Range:** 30 m indoor
- **Topology:** Mesh (up to 232 nodes)
- **Primary Use:** Smart home automation

#### 2.4.7 Thread
- **Standard:** IEEE 802.15.4 + 6LoWPAN + RPL
- **Frequency:** 2.4 GHz
- **Topology:** IPv6-based mesh (low-power, secure)
- **Use Case:** Backbone for Matter (formerly Project CHIP) smart home protocol

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Application |
|---------------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Received Power (Friis)** | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Free-space path loss calculation |
| **Path Loss (Log-Distance)** | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$ | Outdoor/indoor RF propagation |
| **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \dfrac{P_r}{N_0 B}$ | Link budget analysis |
| **Shannon-Hartley Capacity** | $C = B \log_2\left(1 + \text{SNR}\right)$ | Maximum achievable data rate |
| **LoRa Time-on-Air** | $T_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{2^{SF}}{BW} \cdot N_{\text{symbol}}$ | LoRa energy budgeting |
| **NB-IoT Coverage Extension** | $\text{CE Level} \in \{0, 1, 2\}$ with **164 dB MCL** | 128/256/512 repetitions |
| **Battery Life Estimation** | $T_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{bat}}}{\left(I_{\text{avg}} \cdot 24 \cdot 365\right) / 1000}$ | Years of operation |
| **Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL)** | $\text{MCL} = P_{\text{TX}} + G_{\text{TX}} + G_{\text{RX}} - P_{\text{RX,min}}$ | Coverage class definition |
| **Link Budget Equation** | $P_{\text{RX}} = P_{\text{TX}} + G_{\text{TX}} - L_{\text{path}} + G_{\text{RX}}$ | End-to-end RF planning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the backbone of **RF planning, cell-site design, battery-life optimization, and IoT module certification** (especially for NB-IoT and LoRa deployments in agricultural, smart-city, and Industry 4.0 projects across Kerala).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: LoRa Spreading Factor and Range Trade-off

The LoRa modulation uses **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)**. The bit rate is governed by the spreading factor (SF) and bandwidth (BW).

#### Step 1: Define Symbol Rate
The symbol rate (Rs) of a LoRa chirp is inversely proportional to the symbol duration:

$$R_s = \dfrac{BW}{2^{SF}}$$

Where:
- $BW$ = Bandwidth in Hz
- $SF$ = Spreading Factor (7 to 12)

#### Step 2: Derive Bit Rate
Each LoRa symbol encodes $SF$ bits (since there are $2^{SF}$ possible chirp sequences):

$$R_b = SF \cdot \dfrac{BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot \dfrac{4}{4 + CR}$$

Where $CR$ is the coding rate (1 to 4), and the factor $\frac{4}{4+CR}$ accounts for forward error correction overhead.

#### Step 3: Substitute Numerical Example
For $BW = 125 \text{ kHz}$, $SF = 12$, $CR = 1$ (coding rate 4/5):

$$R_b = 12 \cdot \dfrac{125{,}000}{2^{12}} \cdot \dfrac{4}{4+1}$$

$$R_b = 12 \cdot \dfrac{125{,}000}{4096} \cdot 0.8$$

$$R_b = 12 \cdot 30.518 \cdot 0.8$$

$$R_b \approx 292.97 \text{ bps}$$

#### Step 4: Calculate Time-on-Air (ToA)
For a 10-byte payload using SF12, BW=125 kHz:

$$T_{\text{air}} \approx \dfrac{2^{SF} \cdot (N_{\text{payload}} + N_{\text{preamble}})}{BW}$$

$$T_{\text{air}} \approx \dfrac{4096 \cdot (\text{preamble + payload symbols})}{125{,}000}$$

This results in approximately **2.5–3 seconds** of airtime, which directly impacts battery consumption.

#### Step 5: Engineering Insight
A **higher SF doubles the airtime per payload** but provides **~2.5 dB additional processing gain** per SF step, dramatically extending range at the cost of energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** LoRa's adaptive data rate (ADR) algorithm dynamically adjusts SF based on link quality, which is a frequently asked design problem.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: NB-IoT Coverage Extension Repetitions

NB-IoT supports **3 Coverage Extension (CE) levels** with repetition factors 1, 8, and 128/512/1024/2048.

#### Step 1: Signal-to-Noise Ratio per Repetition
For a single transmission, the SNR at the receiver is:

$$\text{SNR}_1 = \dfrac{E_b}{N_0}$$

#### Step 2: Repetition Gain
When the same signal is transmitted $R$ times, the receiver coherently combines them. The energy per bit effectively increases by a factor of $R$:

$$\text{SNR}_R = R \cdot \text{SNR}_1 = R \cdot \dfrac{E_b}{N_0}$$

#### Step 3: Convert to dB
$$\text{SNR}_R \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(R) + \text{SNR}_1 \text{ (dB)}$$

For $R = 128$ repetitions:

$$\text{Gain} = 10 \log_{10}(128) = 10 \cdot 2.107 = 21.07 \text{ dB}$$

#### Step 4: Total MCL Calculation
Starting with a baseline MCL of 144 dB (standard LTE), and adding 20 dB of CE:

$$\text{MCL}_{\text{NB-IoT}} = 144 + 20 = 164 \text{ dB}$$

#### Step 5: Penetration Loss Allowance
This 164 dB MCL can support indoor penetration losses of:

$$L_{\text{penetration}} = 164 - 140 = 24 \text{ dB}$$

Equivalent to **~3 walls of reinforced concrete**, enabling deployment in basements and underground utility meters.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) for IoT Link Budget

The Friis Transmission Equation is fundamental for IoT module placement.

#### Step 1: Free-Space Path Loss Formula

$$\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

Where:
- $d$ = Distance in meters
- $f$ = Frequency in Hz
- $c$ = Speed of light ($3 \times 10^8$ m/s)

#### Step 2: Simplify Constant Term
$$20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8}\right) = -147.55$$

So:

$$\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) - 147.55$$

#### Step 3: Example Calculation — LoRa at 868 MHz, 5 km

$$\text{FSPL} = 20 \log_{10}(5000) + 20 \log_{10}(868 \times 10^6) - 147.55$$

$$\text{FSPL} = 20 \cdot 3.699 + 20 \cdot 9.939 - 147.55$$

$$\text{FSPL} = 73.98 + 198.77 - 147.55 = 125.20 \text{ dB}$$

#### Step 4: Add Real-World Margin
With path loss exponent $n = 2.5$ (urban environment), real path loss becomes:

$$\text{PL} = \text{FSPL} + 10(n-2) \log_{10}(d) = 125.20 + 5 \log_{10}(5000) = 125.20 + 18.49 = 143.69 \text{ dB}$$

#### Step 5: Link Budget Verification
With Tx power = 14 dBm, Tx antenna = 2 dBi, Rx antenna = 6 dBi, Rx sensitivity = -137 dBm (SF12):

$$P_{\text{RX}} = 14 + 2 - 143.69 + 6 = -121.69 \text{ dBm}$$

Since $-121.69 > -137$, the link is **marginally viable** with ~15 dB fade margin.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: 3GPP vs Non-3GPP for IoT

| Parameter | NB-IoT (3GPP) | LTE-M (3GPP) | LoRaWAN | Sigfox | Zigbee | BLE 5 |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|---------|--------|--------|-------|
| **Spectrum** | Licensed | Licensed | Unlicensed | Unlicensed | Unlicensed | Unlicensed |
| **Range** | <15 km | <15 km | <15 km | <50 km | 10–100 m | 10–400 m |
| **Data Rate** | 26 kbps | 375 kbps | 0.3–50 kbps | 0.1 kbps | 250 kbps | 2 Mbps |
| **Battery Life** | 10+ years | 10+ years | 5–10 years | 5–10 years | 2+ years | 1+ year |
| **Mobility** | Limited | Full | None | None | None | None |
| **QoS** | High | High | Best-effort | Best-effort | Best-effort | Best-effort |
| **Module Cost** | $8–$12 | $10–$15 | $3–$5 | $2–$4 | $2–$4 | $1–$2 |
| **Topology** | Star | Star | Star | Star | Mesh | Piconet/Mesh |
| **Security** | Operator-grade | Operator-grade | AES-128 | AES-128 | AES-128 | AES-CCM |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between 3GPP and Non-3GPP connectivity standards for IoT with two examples for each.**

**Model Answer:**
3GPP standards are managed by the 3rd Generation Partnership Project and operate on **licensed cellular spectrum** allocated by government regulators. They provide carrier-grade quality of service, mobility support, and security. Examples: **NB-IoT, LTE-M**.

Non-3GPP standards operate on **unlicensed ISM bands** (433/868/915 MHz, 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz) and are deployed independently by users. They offer lower cost and power but best-effort QoS. Examples: **LoRaWAN, Zigbee**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL) supported by NB-IoT and why is it significant?**

**Model Answer:**
NB-IoT supports a Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL) of **164 dB**, which is 20 dB greater than standard LTE (144 dB). This is significant because it allows NB-IoT devices to communicate from **indoor locations, basements, or shielded utility pits** where conventional cellular signals fail. The gain is achieved through **coverage extension (CE) levels with up to 2048 repetitions** of the same transmission, enabling massive IoT deployments in challenging environments.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q3. (a)** Explain the architecture and key features of **NB-IoT**. Discuss how it achieves 164 dB MCL through coverage extension levels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition and Context:**
NB-IoT (Narrowband Internet of Things) is a 3GPP Release 13 standard (Cat-NB1) designed for **Low Power Wide Area (LPWA)** applications. It uses a narrow **180 kHz bandwidth** (one LTE resource block) and can be deployed in three operational modes:
1. **In-band:** Within an LTE carrier
2. **Guard-band:** In the guard band of an LTE carrier
3. **Standalone:** In re-farmed GSM spectrum

**Key Features of NB-IoT:**

| Feature | Specification |
|---------|---------------|
| Bandwidth | 180 kHz |
| Peak Data Rate (DL) | ~26 kbps |
| Peak Data Rate (UL) | ~62 kbps (multi-tone) / ~26 kbps (single-tone) |
| Modulation (DL) | QPSK |
| Modulation (UL) | BPSK / QPSK |
| Duplex | Half-duplex FDD |
| Maximum Coupling Loss | 164 dB |
| Device Density | ~50,000 per cell |
| Battery Life | 10+ years (with PSM and eDRX) |

**Coverage Extension (CE) Levels:**

NB-IoT defines **3 CE levels** based on the number of repetitions of the same transmission:

- **CE Level 0:** 1 repetition (normal coverage, MCL ~144 dB)
- **CE Level 1:** 8 repetitions (extended coverage, gain ≈ 9 dB)
- **CE Level 2:** 128, 256, 512, 1024, or 2048 repetitions (deep coverage, gain up to 33 dB)

**Derivation of Coverage Gain:**

For $R$ repetitions, the coherent combining gain is:

$$G_R = 10 \log_{10}(R) \text{ dB}$$

For $R = 128$:

$$G_{128} = 10 \log_{10}(128) = 10 \times 2.107 = 21.07 \text{ dB}$$

Total MCL = 144 dB (baseline) + 20 dB (CE gain target) = **164 dB**

**Significance:** This enables NB-IoT devices to operate **20 dB deeper into buildings** than standard LTE, supporting applications like underground water meters, parking sensors, and agricultural soil sensors in remote areas of Kerala.

---

**Q3. (b)** Compare **LoRaWAN and Sigfox** as Non-3GPP LPWA technologies. Discuss the role of **Spreading Factor (SF) in LoRa** with a numerical example of bit rate calculation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison: LoRaWAN vs Sigfox**

| Parameter | LoRaWAN | Sigfox |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| Modulation | CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum) | UNB (Ultra-Narrow Band) BPSK |
| Spectrum | Unlicensed ISM (868/915/433 MHz) | Unlicensed ISM (868/902 MHz) |
| Bandwidth | 125/250/500 kHz | 100 Hz (ultra-narrow) |
| Data Rate | 0.3–50 kbps | 100–600 bps |
| Range | 2–15 km | 10–50 km |
| Daily Messages | Unlimited (with fair use) | 140 uplink / 4 downlink |
| Payload Size | 51–242 bytes | 12 bytes UL / 8 bytes DL |
| Topology | Star-of-stars | Star |
| Device Cost | $3–$5 | $2–$4 |
| Security | AES-128 (end-to-end) | AES-128 (link layer) |
| Ownership | Private/Public hybrid | Operator-managed (Sigfox network) |

**Spreading Factor in LoRa:**

The Spreading Factor (SF) determines the number of chips per symbol, controlling the trade-off between **range** and **data rate**.

**Step 1: Bit Rate Formula:**

$$R_b = SF \cdot \dfrac{BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot \dfrac{4}{4 + CR}$$

**Step 2: Numerical Calculation**

Given: $BW = 125 \text{ kHz}$, $SF = 10$, $CR = 1$ (4/5 coding):

$$R_b = 10 \cdot \dfrac{125{,}000}{2^{10}} \cdot \dfrac{4}{5}$$

$$R_b = 10 \cdot \dfrac{125{,}000}{1024} \cdot 0.8$$

$$R_b = 10 \cdot 122.07 \cdot 0.8 \approx 976.56 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 3: Range vs SF Trade-off**

- Each increment of SF (e.g., SF7 → SF8) provides **~2.5 dB additional processing gain**
- This extends range but **halves the data rate** and **doubles the time-on-air**
- SF12: Maximum range, minimum data rate (~250 bps)
- SF7: Minimum range, maximum data rate (~5.5 kbps)

**Engineering Insight:** The LoRaWAN **ADR (Adaptive Data Rate)** algorithm dynamically selects SF per device to optimize the network for both coverage and capacity, a critical concept in KTU paper design problems.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**Q4. (a)** Explain the **3GPP evolution from GSM to 5G NR** for IoT. Discuss the role of **mMTC, URLLC, and eMBB** in 5G IoT scenarios. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**3GPP Generational Evolution for IoT:**

1. **2G (GSM/GPRS — 1991):** Originally designed for voice; later extended for **EC-GSM-IoT** in Release 13 for low-rate telemetry. Limited capacity (~1000 devices/cell) and high power.

2. **3G (UMTS/HSPA — 2001):** Higher data rates but **not optimized** for IoT due to complex signaling and high power consumption. Limited IoT-specific features.

3. **4G (LTE — 2008):** Introduced two revolutionary IoT variants:
   - **LTE-M (Cat-M1):** For mid-range IoT requiring mobility and voice
   - **NB-IoT (Cat-NB1):** For low-power, low-data, delay-tolerant applications

4. **5G NR (New Radio — 2018 onwards):** Defines **three service categories**:

| Service Type | Full Name | Key Feature | IoT Use Case |
|--------------|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| **eMBB** | Enhanced Mobile Broadband | High data rates (up to 10 Gbps) | HD video surveillance, AR/VR, telemedicine |
| **URLLC** | Ultra-Reliable Low Latency Communication | 1 ms latency, 99.999% reliability | Autonomous vehicles, industrial robotics, remote surgery |
| **mMTC** | Massive Machine-Type Communication | 1 million devices/km², deep coverage | Smart city sensors, environmental monitoring, precision agriculture |

**Significance of the Three 5G Pillars for IoT:**

- **eMBB** transforms bandwidth-hungry IoT applications (e.g., 4K CCTV, cloud robotics)
- **URLLC** enables mission-critical IoT (e.g., V2X communication, smart grids)
- **mMTC** scales IoT deployments to city-wide and national-level densities

For example, in a **Kerala smart agriculture deployment**, soil moisture sensors would use **mMTC**, autonomous tractors would use **URLLC**, and drone-based crop monitoring would use **eMBB** — all on the same 5G infrastructure.

---

**Q4. (b)** Discuss **Zigbee and Thread protocols** as Non-3GPP mesh networking standards for IoT. Include their protocol stack and a comparative analysis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Zigbee Protocol (IEEE 802.15.4-based):**

Zigbee is an open standard maintained by the **Connectivity Standards Alliance (CSA)**. It is built on top of IEEE 802.15.4 PHY and MAC layers.

**Zigbee Protocol Stack:**

```
┌──────────────────────────────┐
│   Application Layer (Zigbee 3.0)   │
├──────────────────────────────┤
│   Network/ Security Layer (NWK)     │
├──────────────────────────────┤
│   MAC Layer (IEEE 802.15.4)         │
├──────────────────────────────┤
│   PHY Layer (IEEE 802.15.4)         │
└──────────────────────────────┘
```

**Zigbee Specifications:**
- **Frequency:** 2.4 GHz (worldwide), 868 MHz (EU), 915 MHz (US)
- **Data Rate:** 250 kbps (2.4 GHz)
- **Range:** 10–100 m
- **Topology:** Star, Tree, Mesh (up to 65,000 nodes)
- **Channels:** 16 channels in 2.4 GHz band
- **Security:** AES-128 encryption

**Thread Protocol:**

Thread is an **IPv6-based mesh networking protocol** designed for low-power smart home and IoT applications. It uses IEEE 802.15.4 PHY/MAC with **6LoWPAN** and **RPL** routing.

**Thread Protocol Stack:**

```
┌──────────────────────────────┐
│   Application (CoAP, MQTT)        │
├──────────────────────────────┤
│   UDP / TCP                       │
├──────────────────────────────┤
│   IPv6 / 6LoWPAN                  │
├──────────────────────────────┤
│   RPL Routing                     │
├──────────────────────────────┤
│   MAC (IEEE 802.15.4)             │
├──────────────────────────────┤
│   PHY (IEEE 802.15.4)             │
└──────────────────────────────┘
```

**Thread Specifications:**
- **Frequency:** 2.4 GHz
- **Data Rate:** 250 kbps
- **Topology:** Mesh (self-healing, no single point of failure)
- **Addressing:** IPv6 (each device has a unique IPv6 address)
- **Native Support:** Matter smart home protocol

**Comparative Analysis:**

| Parameter | Zigbee | Thread |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Application Layer | Zigbee Cluster Library (ZCL) | CoAP / Native |
| Network Layer | Zigbee NWK | IPv6 / 6LoWPAN / RPL |
| Internet Integration | Requires gateway | Native IP |
| Mesh Self-Healing | Yes | Yes (better, faster) |
| Power Consumption | Low | Very Low |
| Use Case | Home automation, industrial sensors | Smart home (Matter), building automation |
| Number of Nodes | 65,000 | 250+ (per border router) |
| Security | AES-128 (network + APS) | AES-128 (link + app) with commissioning |

**Engineering Insight:** Thread's native IPv6 support makes it more **future-proof** for large-scale IoT, while Zigbee's mature ecosystem and ZCL library offer **broader device interoperability** today.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing LTE-M and NB-IoT data rates:** LTE-M supports ~300 kbps; NB-IoT only ~26 kbps. Many students incorrectly swap these.
> 2. **Forgetting half-duplex constraint:** Both LTE-M and NB-IoT use **half-duplex FDD**, not full-duplex. Drawing simultaneous UL/DL arrows will cost 2 marks.
> 3. **Mis-stating LoRa modulation:** LoRa uses **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)**, not DSSS or FHSS. Writing "spread spectrum" without specifying CSS loses half a mark.
> 4. **Skipping numerical calculations:** In 14-mark questions, a question asking for "calculation" or "derivation" without numbers will be capped at 8 marks. Always show step-by-step numerical work.
> 5. **Mixing up 5G service types:** mMTC = density, URLLC = latency, eMBB = bandwidth. Reversing these is a critical error.
> 6. **Ignoring unlicensed spectrum:** Non-3GPP standards operate on **ISM bands**, not "free spectrum." Examiners mark this as a conceptual error.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **3GPP** is the standardization body for **licensed cellular IoT**: NB-IoT, LTE-M, 5G NR (mMTC, URLLC, eMBB)
- **Non-3GPP** standards use **unlicensed ISM bands**: LoRaWAN, Sigfox, Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee, Z-Wave, Thread
- **NB-IoT:** 180 kHz BW, 26 kbps, 164 dB MCL, 3 CE levels (1/8/128+ repetitions)
- **LTE-M:** 1.4 MHz BW, 375 kbps, full mobility, supports VoLTE
- **LoRaWAN:** CSS modulation, SF7–SF12, 0.3–50 kbps, uses 868/915 MHz
- **Sigfox:** UNB BPSK, 100–600 bps, 140 messages/day, 12-byte payload
- **Zigbee:** IEEE 802.15.4, 2.4 GHz, 250 kbps, mesh, AES-128
- **Thread:** IEEE 802.15.4 + IPv6/6LoWPAN/RPL, native IP, used by Matter
- **BLE 5.x:** 2 Mbps, 400 m range (coded PHY), piconet/mesh, 1–2 year battery
- **5G Pillars:** mMTC (density), URLLC (latency), eMBB (bandwidth)
- **Key Formulas:** $R_b = SF \cdot \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot \frac{4}{4+CR}$ (LoRa), $C = B \log_2(1+\text{SNR})$ (Shannon)
- **Licensed vs Unlicensed:** 3GPP = operator-managed, carrier-grade QoS, costly; Non-3GPP = self-managed, best-effort, low-cost
- **KTU-Favorite Topics:** LoRa SF calculations, NB-IoT CE levels, 5G service types, comparison tables

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LPWAN Protocols - Sigfox, LoRa, LoRaWAN, Weightless, NB-IoT, and LTE-M
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST834 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LPWAN Protocols - Sigfox, LoRa, LoRaWAN, Weightless, NB-IoT, and LTE-M

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — LPWAN PROTOCOLS
## Sigfox · LoRa · LoRaWAN · Weightless · NB-IoT · LTE-M

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> **Low Power Wide Area Networks (LPWAN)** are a category of wireless communication technologies designed to enable long-range (typically **2 km to 50 km**), low-bandwidth, and energy-efficient data transmission for **massive Machine-Type Communications (mMTC)** in the Internet of Things (IoT). LPWAN protocols operate primarily in **sub-GHz ISM bands (433/868/915 MHz)** or licensed cellular **sub-1 GHz and 1.8 GHz** spectrum, trading off data rate (typically **0.3 kbps to 200 kbps**) for extreme range and battery life (**up to 10+ years** on a single coin cell).

### 1.1 The Intuition — "The Whispering Postal Network"

Imagine the **modern courier system** (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, 5G eMBB) as a **bullet train**:
* 🚄 It is **fast** (high data rate, up to Gbps)
* 🏙️ But it is **expensive**, **energy-hungry**, and only operates between **specific stations** (short range, ~100 m)

Now imagine **LPWAN as a fleet of carrier pigeons**:
* 🐦 Each pigeon is **incredibly slow** (low data rate, ~0.3 kbps)
* 🔋 But it can fly **hundreds of kilometers** on a few grains of corn (low power, multi-year battery)
* 📡 Perfect for sending **one tiny note per day** from a remote farm sensor to a city server

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the IoT reference model, LPWAN technologies are classified as **Perception + Network Layer (sometimes Transport)** technologies used in **massive IoT scenarios** such as smart agriculture, smart metering, asset tracking, and environmental monitoring — applications where **range, cost, and battery life dominate over data throughput**.

### 1.2 Common Architectural Pillars of LPWAN

Every LPWAN system — proprietary or standardized — shares four engineering pillars:

1. **Star Topology** — End devices talk directly to a base station/gateway (no mesh, no routing).
2. **Licensed or ISM Sub-GHz Spectrum** — Better propagation through walls, foliage, and concrete.
3. **Duty-Cycled Transmission** — Devices sleep >99% of the time; transmit only a few packets per day.
4. **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)** — The network server controls the trade-off between range and throughput per device.

### 1.3 The Six LPWAN Protocols at a Glance

| # | Protocol | Origin | Spectrum | Standardization |
|---|----------|--------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Sigfox** | Sigfox S.A. (France) | Sub-GHz ISM | Proprietary |
| 2 | **LoRa** | Semtech (USA) | Sub-GHz ISM | Proprietary PHY |
| 3 | **LoRaWAN** | LoRa Alliance | Sub-GHz ISM | Open MAC spec |
| 4 | **Weightless** | Weightless SIG | Sub-GHz ISM | Open Standard |
| 5 | **NB-IoT** | 3GPP (Rel-13) | Licensed LTE | International Standard |
| 6 | **LTE-M** | 3GPP (Rel-13) | Licensed LTE | International Standard |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

---

### 2.1 Sigfox — "The Ultranarrowband Telegram"

**Physical Layer Characteristics:**
* **Modulation:** Ultra-Narrow Band (UNB) — Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK) in a **100 Hz** to **600 Hz** bandwidth
* **Data Rate:** **100 bps** (uplink), **600 bps** (downlink)
* **Payload Size:** **12 bytes** (uplink), **8 bytes** (downlink)
* **Daily Message Limit:** **140 uplink** + **4 downlink** per device
* **Topology:** Pure **star** with **UNB base stations**; uses **random frequency and time** (Aloha-like) to avoid collisions
* **Range:** **10–50 km** (rural), **3–10 km** (urban)
* **Spectrum:** **868 MHz** (Europe), **902 MHz** (USA), **433 MHz** (Asia)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight — Why UNB?**
> By squeezing data into a very narrow band, Sigfox achieves a **massive link budget (~160 dB)**. The signal energy is concentrated, making it decodable even at **−137 dBm** sensitivity. This is the core reason Sigfox devices last **10+ years on a 2.5 Ah battery**.

---

### 2.2 LoRa — "The Chirp Spread Spectrum Physical Layer"

LoRa is **only the physical layer modulation** patented by **Semtech Corporation**. It is not a complete network protocol.

**Modulation Principle — Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS):**
A LoRa chirp is a **frequency-modulated continuous wave** that linearly sweeps from $f_{min}$ to $f_{max}$ over the symbol period. The receiver correlates the incoming chirp with a known template to recover data.

* **Bandwidth (BW):** **125 kHz, 250 kHz, 500 kHz**
* **Spreading Factor (SF):** **SF7 to SF12** (log2 bits/symbol: 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12)
* **Coding Rate (CR):** **4/5, 4/6, 4/7, 4/8**
* **Frequency Bands:** **433 MHz, 868 MHz, 915 MHz, 923 MHz** (regional ISM)
* **Range:** **2–8 km** (urban), **15–20 km** (rural line-of-sight)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:**
> A higher **Spreading Factor (SF)** increases **range and sensitivity** but **decreases data rate** and **increases Time-on-Air**. SF12 gives the longest range but the slowest throughput.

---

### 2.3 LoRaWAN — "The Open MAC on Top of LoRa"

**LoRaWAN** is an **open specification** maintained by the **LoRa Alliance** defining the **Media Access Control (MAC)** layer and **network architecture** for LoRa-based LPWANs.

**Device Classes:**
| Class | Downlink Slots | Power Use | Use Case |
|-------|---------------|-----------|----------|
| **Class A** (default) | Only after uplink (2 windows) | Lowest | Battery sensors |
| **Class B** | Scheduled (ping slots) | Medium | Actuated devices |
| **Class C** | Continuously listening | Highest | Mains-powered actuators |

**Key Network Entities:**
1. **End Device** — sensor/actuator
2. **Gateway** — transparent bridge (LoRa ↔ IP)
3. **Network Server (NS)** — deduplication, ADR, security
4. **Application Server (AS)** — payload parsing

**Security:** **AES-128** with two session keys: **AppSKey** (payload encryption) and **NwkSKey** (MIC integrity).

**LoRaWAN Regional Parameters:**
* **EU868:** 863–870 MHz, 8 channels, duty-cycle 1% (ETSI EN 300 220)
* **US915:** 902–928 MHz, 64 uplink + 8 downlink channels, 400 ms dwell time (FCC)
* **AS923:** 915–928 MHz (Asia)

---

### 2.4 Weightless — "The Three Flavors of Open LPWAN"

Weightless is a set of **open LPWAN standards** developed by the **Weightless Special Interest Group (SIG)**.

| Flavor | Modulation | Spectrum | Data Rate | Status |
|--------|------------|----------|-----------|--------|
| **Weightless-W** | 16-QAM / BPSK / DPSK | TV White Spaces (470–790 MHz) | 1 kbps – 10 Mbps | Promised but rare |
| **Weightless-N** | Ultra-narrowband DBPSK | Sub-GHz ISM (868/915 MHz) | 30 kbps – 100 kbps | Deployed |
| **Weightless-P** | Offset QPSK + FDMA/TDMA | Sub-GHz ISM | 200 bps – 100 kbps | Most popular |

**Key Features:**
* **Bidirectional** with acknowledged transmission
* **Adaptive Data Rate**
* **128-bit AES encryption** (same family as LoRaWAN)
* **Star-of-stars topology**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why did Weightless matter?**
> It was the **first open standard** offering both **uplink and downlink** with adaptive rates. While LoRaWAN and Sigfox dominated, Weightless-P was specifically designed for **high-density urban** deployments with **acknowledged reliability**.

---

### 2.5 NB-IoT (Narrowband IoT) — "The Cellular Cousin for Tiny Things"

**NB-IoT (Cat-NB1 / Cat-NB2)** was introduced in **3GPP Release 13 (2016)** as a **clean-slate** LPWAN technology for licensed cellular spectrum.

**Technical Specifications:**
* **Bandwidth:** **180 kHz** (one LTE Physical Resource Block)
* **Deployment Modes:** **In-band**, **Guard-band**, **Standalone**
* **Modulation:** **QPSK / BPSK** (uplink), **QPSK** (downlink)
* **Data Rate:** **~26 kbps** (DL), **~62 kbps** (UL — multi-tone)
* **Latency:** **1.6 s to 10 s** (acceptable for metering)
* **Coverage Extension:** **+20 dB MCL** over regular LTE → **164 dB Maximum Coupling Loss**
* **Device Categories:** **Cat-NB1** (Rel-13), **Cat-NB2** (Rel-14, higher data rate)
* **Power Saving Modes (PSM & eDRX):** Battery life **>10 years**

**NB-IoT Protocol Stack:**
* PHY based on LTE downlink (OFDMA, 15 kHz subcarrier spacing)
* MAC uses LTE procedures with simplifications
* Upper layers: **IP, Non-IP, or SMS** data delivery

---

### 2.6 LTE-M (Long Term Evolution for Machines) — "The Faster Cellular IoT"

**LTE-M (Cat-M1 / Cat-M2)** is the **higher-throughput sibling** of NB-IoT, also from 3GPP Rel-13.

**Technical Specifications:**
* **Bandwidth:** **1.08 MHz** (6 LTE resource blocks)
* **Data Rate:** **~300 kbps (DL)** and **~375 kbps (UL)**
* **Latency:** **10–50 ms** — supports **VoLTE (voice over LTE)**
* **Mobility:** **Full handover support** (NB-IoT has no handover)
* **Coverage:** **+15 dB MCL** over LTE → **155 dB MCL**
* **Power Saving:** **PSM and eDRX** enabled

**LTE-M vs NB-IoT — The KTU Comparison:**

| Feature | NB-IoT | LTE-M |
|---------|--------|-------|
| Bandwidth | 180 kHz | 1.08 MHz |
| Data Rate | ~26/62 kbps | ~300/375 kbps |
| Voice (VoLTE) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Handover | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Deployment | Standalone, Guard, In-band | In-band only |
| Battery Life | >10 years | >10 years |
| Best For | Static sensors | Mobile / voice / firmware OTA |

---

### 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most likely numerical questions in the KTU ESE.**

#### 2.7.1 LoRa Bit Rate (Data Rate)

$$R_b = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR$$

Where:
* $R_b$ = bit rate in **bits per second (bps)**
* $SF$ = spreading factor (integer from 7 to 12)
* $BW$ = bandwidth in **Hz** (typically $125000$, $250000$, $500000$)
* $CR$ = coding rate ($\frac{4}{5}$, $\frac{4}{6}$, $\frac{4}{7}$, $\frac{4}{8}$)

#### 2.7.2 LoRa Symbol Duration

$$T_{sym} = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW}$$

#### 2.7.3 LoRa Receiver Sensitivity (Approximation)

$$S = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(BW) + NF + SNR_{req}$$

Where:
* $-174 \text{ dBm/Hz}$ = thermal noise floor (KTB at T = 290 K)
* $NF$ = noise figure of the receiver (typically **3–6 dB**)
* $SNR_{req}$ = required SNR for demodulation (depends on SF)

#### 2.7.4 Link Budget (Path Loss)

$$PL_{max} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S_{RX} - L_{cable}$$

#### 2.7.5 Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL) — Used in 3GPP

$$MCL = P_{TX_{max}} - S_{RX_{min}}$$

#### 2.7.6 Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

$$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

#### 2.7.7 LoRaWAN MAC Payload (EU868, max)

$$M = \left\lfloor \frac{8N - 44 - 20SF}{4 \times (SF - 2)} \right\rfloor \text{ bytes}$$

Where $N$ is the number of bytes in the raw PHY payload.

#### 2.7.8 Battery Life Estimation (Idealized)

$$T_{life} = \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} \times \eta$$

Where $I_{avg}$ is the **average current consumption** dominated by sleep current.

#### 2.7.9 Energy per Bit (Spectral Efficiency)

$$E_b / N_0 = P_{RX} - 10\log_{10}(R_b) + 174 \text{ dBm/Hz}$$

#### 2.7.10 Shannon Channel Capacity (Theoretical Ceiling)

$$C = BW \times \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \text{ bps}$$

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Protocol | Real-World Deployment |
|----------|----------------------|
| **Sigfox** | French national water metering, Spanish smart parking, Belgian asset tracking |
| **LoRaWAN** | The Things Network (TTN), Tata Communications (India), national smart-grid rollouts |
| **NB-IoT** | Vodafone, Deutsche Telekom, Jio — smart gas/water meters, agricultural sensors |
| **LTE-M** | AT&T, Verizon, Telstra — connected cars, wearables (Apple Watch S3+), ATM machines |
| **Weightless-P** | UK-based smart-city pilots |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — LoRa Bit Rate from Chirp Parameters

We start from the **definition of the LoRa chirp symbol** and the **information-theoretic relationship** between bandwidth, symbol duration, and bits-per-symbol.

**Step 1 — A LoRa symbol encodes `SF` bits.**
A chirp sweeping the entire bandwidth $BW$ over a symbol period $T_{sym}$ can be **time-shifted by $2^{SF}$ discrete values**, each shift representing a unique symbol. Hence, **one symbol = SF bits**.

$$\text{Bits per symbol} = \log_2(2^{SF}) = SF$$

**Step 2 — Symbol rate is the inverse of symbol duration.**
For a chirp covering bandwidth $BW$ using $2^{SF}$ samples:

$$R_{sym} = \frac{1}{T_{sym}} = \frac{BW}{2^{SF}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by bits per symbol.**
This gives the **raw (uncoded) bit rate**:

$$R_{raw} = R_{sym} \times SF = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply forward error correction (FEC) coding rate.**
The coding rate $CR = \frac{4}{n}$ (with $n \in \{5,6,7,8\}$) reduces the effective bit rate:

$$R_b = R_{raw} \times CR = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example.**
For the **most common LoRa configuration: SF7, BW = 125 kHz, CR = 4/5:**

$$R_b = 7 \times \frac{125000}{2^{7}} \times \frac{4}{5} = 7 \times \frac{125000}{128} \times 0.8$$

$$R_b = 7 \times 976.5625 \times 0.8 = 7 \times 781.25 = 5468.75 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with SF12, BW = 125 kHz, CR = 4/5:**

$$R_b = 12 \times \frac{125000}{4096} \times 0.8 = 12 \times 30.518 \times 0.8 = 292.97 \text{ bps}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Going from SF7 to SF12 at the **same bandwidth** reduces throughput by a factor of **~18.7×**. This is the **fundamental range-vs-throughput trade-off** in LoRa. KTU examiners frequently test this calculation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — LoRa Receiver Sensitivity

**Step 1 — Recall thermal noise density.**
At room temperature ($T = 290$ K), the thermal noise power spectral density is:

$$N_0 = kTB / Hz = -174 \text{ dBm/Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total in-band noise.**
For a receiver with bandwidth $BW$ (in Hz) and noise figure $NF$:

$$N = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(BW) + NF \quad \text{(dBm)}$$

**Step 3 — Add the required SNR for demodulation.**
The minimum decodable signal must exceed the noise floor by $SNR_{req}$. Hence:

$$S_{min} = N + SNR_{req} = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(BW) + NF + SNR_{req}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example — Semtech SX1276 typical values.**

| SF | $SNR_{req}$ (dB) | BW (Hz) | NF (dB) | $S_{min}$ (dBm) |
|----|------------------|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 7  | −7.5 | 125000 | 6 | $$-174 + 51 + 6 - 7.5 = \mathbf{-124.5}$$ |
| 10 | −15  | 125000 | 6 | $$-174 + 51 + 6 - 15 = \mathbf{-132}$$ |
| 12 | −20  | 125000 | 6 | $$-174 + 51 + 6 - 20 = \mathbf{-137}$$ |

**Step 5 — Compute link budget for SF12, TX power 14 dBm:**

$$PL_{max} = 14 - (-137) = 151 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to free-space range (f = 868 MHz):**

$$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(868 \times 10^6) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8}\right)$$

$$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 118.77 + 32.45 = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 151.22$$

Setting $FSPL = 151$ dB:

$$20 \log_{10}(d) = 151 - 151.22 = -0.22 \Rightarrow d = 10^{-0.011} \approx 0.98 \text{ km (free space)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to KTU students:** In free space at SF12, you get only ~1 km. **Real-world outdoor LoRa range of 10+ km** is achieved in **rural/LoS conditions** with **log-distance path loss** (not free-space), where the path loss exponent is **2.5 to 3.5**, not 2.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — LoRaWAN Maximum MAC Payload Size (EU868, no FPort)

**Step 1 — Recall the LoRaWAN Regional Parameters formula (Section 2.7.7):**

$$M = \left\lfloor \frac{8N - 44 - 20SF}{4 \times (SF - 2)} \right\rfloor \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Compute for SF7, no repeater compatibility, N = 255 (max):**

$$M = \left\lfloor \frac{8 \times 255 - 44 - 20 \times 7}{4 \times (7 - 2)} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2040 - 44 - 140}{20} \right\rfloor$$

$$M = \left\lfloor \frac{1856}{20} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 92.8 \right\rfloor = 92 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Compute for SF12, no repeater compatibility, N = 255:**

$$M = \left\lfloor \frac{2040 - 44 - 240}{4 \times 10} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1756}{40} \right\rfloor = 43 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Analytical Insight.**
The formula shows a clear inverse relationship: as **SF increases by 1**, the maximum MAC payload **decreases significantly** because the **header overhead grows linearly with SF** (the $20SF$ term), while the **payload efficiency decreases** (the $4(SF-2)$ denominator grows faster).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) Link Margin

When the network server wants a device to **increase its data rate** (decrease SF), it must ensure the device still has **sufficient link margin**.

**Step 1 — Define Link Margin.**
$$LM = SNR_{measured} - SNR_{required} - \text{Margin}_{safety}$$

**Step 2 — Default values used by LoRaWAN ADR algorithm.**
Typically: $\text{Margin}_{safety} = 10$ dB; each SF step down saves 2.5 to 3 dB in required SNR.

**Step 3 — Worked example.**
Device reports SNR = +5 dB. SF10 requires SNR = −15 dB. Margin = 10 dB.

$$LM = 5 - (-15) - 10 = 10 \text{ dB}$$

> This device has **10 dB of excess margin**, sufficient to drop to **SF9** (saving ~2.5 dB), then **SF8** (saving another ~2.5 dB), and possibly **SF7** — at each step improving data rate.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Six LPWAN Protocols

| Parameter | Sigfox | LoRaWAN | Weightless-P | NB-IoT | LTE-M |
|-----------|--------|---------|--------------|--------|-------|
| **Spectrum** | Sub-GHz ISM | Sub-GHz ISM | Sub-GHz ISM | Licensed LTE | Licensed LTE |
| **Modulation** | UNB BPSK | CSS | OQPSK | QPSK/BPSK | QPSK/16QAM |
| **Bandwidth** | 100–600 Hz | 125/250/500 kHz | 12.5 kHz | 180 kHz | 1.08 MHz |
| **Data Rate** | 100 bps | 0.25–50 kbps | 0.2–100 kbps | ~26/62 kbps | ~300/375 kbps |
| **MCL** | ~160 dB | ~157 dB | ~155 dB | 164 dB | 155 dB |
| **Range (urban)** | 3–10 km | 2–8 km | 2–5 km | 1–5 km | 1–5 km |
| **Battery Life** | 10+ years | 10+ years | 5–10 years | 10+ years | 10+ years |
| **Mobility** | No | No | No | No | **Yes (handover)** |
| **Voice Support** | No | No | No | No | **Yes (VoLTE)** |
| **Encryption** | Optional | AES-128 | AES-128 | 3GPP (SNOW/128) | 3GPP |
| **Topology** | Star | Star-of-stars | Star | Star (cellular) | Star (cellular) |
| **Standard Body** | Sigfox S.A. | LoRa Alliance | Weightless SIG | 3GPP | 3GPP |
| **Cost Model** | Subscription per device | Free (DIY) / public-NS | Subscription | Carrier subscription | Carrier subscription |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
