<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Differentiate between LoRa and LoRaWAN. Why is LoRa considered a physical layer technology, while LoRaWAN is a complete networking protocol?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | LoRa | LoRaWAN |
|--------|------|---------|
| **Layer** | Physical Layer (PHY) only | MAC + Network layer |
| **Definition** | Chirp Spread Spectrum modulation patented by Semtech | Open MAC specification by LoRa Alliance |
| **Scope** | Defines how a bit becomes a radio chirp | Defines device classes, frame format, security, ADR |
| **Standardization** | Proprietary | Open (royalty-free) |

LoRa specifies only **how to encode data on the radio channel** (chirp modulation, SF, BW, CR). LoRaWAN specifies **the complete communication protocol** — MAC commands, device addressing, AES-128 security, network architecture, and regional frequency plans. **LoRaWAN uses LoRa modulation underneath** — they are not competitors but **complementary layers** of the same stack. *(3 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> *Explain the three deployment modes of NB-IoT. How does NB-IoT achieve a 164 dB Maximum Coupling Loss?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three deployment modes:**
1. **In-band:** NB-IoT occupies resource blocks **inside an LTE carrier** — reuses LTE infrastructure.
2. **Guard-band:** NB-IoT uses the **unused guard bands** between LTE carriers.
3. **Standalone:** NB-IoT replaces a **GSM 200 kHz channel** — used in GSM refarming.

**How 164 dB MCL is achieved:**
* **Repetition coding:** Each transmission is repeated up to **128 times**, allowing time-diversity gain.
* **Narrow bandwidth (180 kHz):** Concentrates signal energy → lower noise floor.
* **Relaxed uplink requirements:** Single-tone transmission at 3.75 kHz or 15 kHz.
* **Power Saving Mode (PSM) and eDRX:** Enable >10-year battery life even with extended coverage. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Module 2]**
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of LoRaWAN. Describe the three device classes (A, B, C) with their downlink behavior and typical use cases. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** A LoRa end device uses **SF = 9, BW = 125 kHz, CR = 4/7**. Calculate the **(i)** symbol duration, **(ii)** bit rate, and **(iii)** maximum MAC payload size (EU868, no repeater, N = 255). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Q3 (a) — LoRaWAN Architecture & Device Classes

**LoRaWAN architecture has 4 logical entities:**

1. **End Devices** — Class A, B, or C sensors/actuators. They are **uniquely identified by a 64-bit DevAddr** and contain two 128-bit AES keys: **AppSKey** (payload) and **NwkSKey** (frame integrity).

2. **Gateways** — **Transparent bridges** that simply forward LoRa frames to the Network Server over IP (typically Ethernet/4G backhaul). A single device can be received by **multiple gateways simultaneously** (diversity reception).

3. **Network Server (NS)** — The **brain** of the network:
   * Deduplicates frames received by multiple gateways
   * Runs the **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)** algorithm
   * Manages **frame counters** to prevent replay attacks
   * Selects the best gateway for downlink

4. **Application Server (AS)** — Decrypts the payload with **AppSKey** and routes data to the user application.

**Device Classes:**

| Class | Downlink Slots | Reception Windows | Power Profile | Use Case |
|-------|---------------|-------------------|---------------|----------|
| **A** | Only after uplink | 2 short windows (RX1, RX2) after each TX | **Lowest** | Battery sensors, smart meters |
| **B** | Scheduled ping slots | Class A windows + periodic **ping slots** synchronized via gateway beacons | **Medium** | Actuators needing predictable downlink |
| **C** | Continuously open (except during TX) | Class A windows + **always-open** RX2 window | **Highest (mains-powered)** | Streetlight controllers, mains-powered actuators |

**Note:** Class A is **mandatory** — every LoRaWAN device must implement it. Class B and C are optional. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q3 (b) — LoRa Numerical Calculation

**Given:** SF = 9, BW = 125,000 Hz, CR = 4/7

**(i) Symbol Duration:**

$$T_{sym} = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW} = \frac{2^9}{125000} = \frac{512}{125000}$$

$$T_{sym} = 0.004096 \text{ s} = 4.096 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Bit Rate:**

$$R_b = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR = 9 \times \frac{125000}{512} \times \frac{4}{7}$$

$$R_b = 9 \times 244.1406 \times 0.5714$$

$$R_b = 9 \times 139.51 = 1255.6 \text{ bps} \approx 1.256 \text{ kbps}$$

**(iii) Maximum MAC Payload (EU868):**

$$M = \left\lfloor \frac{8N - 44 - 20SF}{4 \times (SF - 2)} \right\rfloor$$

With N = 255, SF = 9:

$$M = \left\lfloor \frac{8 \times 255 - 44 - 20 \times 9}{4 \times (9 - 2)} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2040 - 44 - 180}{28} \right\rfloor$$

$$M = \left\lfloor \frac{1816}{28} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 64.857 \right\rfloor = 64 \text{ bytes}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **4.096 ms**, (ii) **≈ 1.256 kbps**, (iii) **64 bytes**. *(7 marks)*

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Module 2 Internal Choice]**
> **(a)** Compare Sigfox, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, and LTE-M with respect to **modulation technique, spectrum, data rate, mobility support, and ideal use case**. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** A LoRa receiver operates at **868 MHz, BW = 125 kHz, NF = 6 dB, required SNR = −7.5 dB (SF7)**. If the transmitter uses **+14 dBm** with **2 dBi gain** on both sides, and the cable loss is **1 dB total**, calculate the **(i)** receiver sensitivity, **(ii)** maximum path loss, and **(iii)** approximate free-space range. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Q4 (a) — Comprehensive Comparison Table

| Parameter | Sigfox | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT | LTE-M |
|-----------|--------|---------|--------|-------|
| **Modulation** | UNB BPSK (100–600 Hz) | Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) | QPSK / BPSK (LTE-based) | QPSK / 16-QAM (LTE-based) |
| **Spectrum** | Sub-GHz ISM (868/915 MHz) | Sub-GHz ISM (868/915/433 MHz) | Licensed LTE (in/guard/standalone) | Licensed LTE (in-band) |
| **Bandwidth** | ~100 Hz | 125 / 250 / 500 kHz | 180 kHz | 1.08 MHz |
| **Data Rate** | 100 bps UL / 600 bps DL | 0.25 – 50 kbps | 26 / 62 kbps | 300 / 375 kbps |
| **Mobility** | No (no handover) | No (no handover) | No (no handover) | **Yes (full LTE handover)** |
| **Voice (VoLTE)** | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Max Payload** | 12 bytes UL | 51–222 bytes (SF dependent) | ~1600 bytes | ~1300 bytes |
| **Encryption** | Optional (Sigfox cloud) | AES-128 (NwkSKey + AppSKey) | 3GPP SNOW-3G / AES | 3GPP SNOW-3G / AES |
| **Network Cost** | Per-device subscription (Sigfox operator) | Free (DIY) or subscription | Cellular subscription | Cellular subscription |
| **Ideal Use Case** | Static, ultra-low data (parking, alarms) | Open multi-purpose IoT (agriculture, smart-city) | Static metering (water, gas, electricity) | Mobile + Voice (wearables, vehicles, ATMs) |

**Conclusion:** The choice depends on **mobility, voice, payload size, and licensing cost**. NB-IoT and LTE-M dominate **carrier-managed** rollouts, while LoRaWAN dominates **private/community** networks. Sigfox is preferred for **ultra-low-cost, ultra-low-throughput** use cases. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q4 (b) — LoRa Sensitivity & Range Calculation

**Given:**
* $f = 868$ MHz, $BW = 125{,}000$ Hz
* $NF = 6$ dB, $SNR_{req} = -7.5$ dB
* $P_{TX} = +14$ dBm, $G_{TX} = G_{RX} = 2$ dBi, $L_{cable} = 1$ dB

**(i) Receiver Sensitivity:**

$$S_{min} = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(125000) + 6 + (-7.5)$$

$$S_{min} = -174 + 50.969 + 6 - 7.5$$

$$S_{min} = -124.531 \text{ dBm} \approx -124.5 \text{ dBm}$$

**(ii) Maximum Path Loss (Link Budget):**

$$PL_{max} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{cable} - S_{min}$$

$$PL_{max} = 14 + 2 + 2 - 1 - (-124.5) = 17 + 124.5 = 141.5 \text{ dB}$$

**(iii) Free-Space Range at 868 MHz:**

$$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

$$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(868 \times 10^6) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8}\right)$$

$$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 118.77 + 32.45 = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 151.22$$

Setting $FSPL = PL_{max} = 141.5$ dB:

$$20 \log_{10}(d) = 141.5 - 151.22 = -9.72$$

$$\log_{10}(d) = -0.486 \Rightarrow d = 10^{-0.486} \approx 0.327 \text{ km} = 327 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **−124.5 dBm**, (ii) **141.5 dB**, (iii) **~327 m (free space only)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** In real urban / rural outdoor environments, the path-loss exponent is **2.5 to 3.5** (not 2), so the **actual range** will be **1.5 to 5 km** using the log-distance model. Free-space calculations give only a **theoretical lower bound**. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALL CALLOUT
> **Common places students LOSE marks on LPWAN questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing LoRa and LoRaWAN** — they are NOT the same; LoRa is PHY, LoRaWAN is MAC+Network.
> 2. **Forgetting the `BW` unit** in bit-rate formula — must convert from kHz to Hz. BW = 125 kHz ⇒ 125,000.
> 3. **Confusing the `−174 dBm/Hz` constant** with the receiver sensitivity directly. The constant is **noise spectral density**, not sensitivity. Add $10 \log_{10}(BW)$, $NF$, and $SNR_{req}$ to get sensitivity.
> 4. **In NB-IoT/LTE-M questions** — do not state that NB-IoT supports VoLTE. Only **LTE-M** supports VoLTE.
> 5. **Sigfox daily message limit** — only **140 uplink + 4 downlink** per device per day. Students often miss this.
> 6. **Forgetting downlink** — LoRaWAN Class A allows downlink **only in two windows after an uplink**, not anytime.
> 7. **LoRaWAN encryption keys** — must clearly distinguish **NwkSKey (network integrity)** vs **AppSKey (application payload encryption)**.
> 8. **Max payload size** — always use the **EU868 formula** unless the question specifies US915 or AS923. Forgetting the `-44` and `-20SF` overhead constants costs 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### 🚀 RAPID REVISION — KTU MODULE 2 LPWAN

✅ **LPWAN is for mMTC** — long range, low power, low data rate, high node density.

✅ **Six protocols:** **S**igfox, **L**oRa(/**L**oRaWAN), **W**eightless, **N**B-IoT, **L**TE-M — remember as **"S-L-W-N-L"**.

✅ **Spectrum split:** Sigfox, LoRa(/**WAN**), Weightless → **unlicensed ISM**; NB-IoT, LTE-M → **licensed LTE**.

✅ **Sigfox** = **100 bps UNB**; **140 UL + 4 DL/day**; **12-byte UL payload**; **subscription model**.

✅ **LoRa = PHY only** (Chirp Spread Spectrum, Semtech patent). **LoRaWAN = MAC** (LoRa Alliance, open).

✅ **LoRa parameters:** **SF7–SF12**, **BW = 125/250/500 kHz**, **CR = 4/5 to 4/8**.

✅ **LoRa bit rate formula:** $R_b = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR$ — memorize this verbatim.

✅ **LoRa symbol duration:** $T_{sym} = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW}$.

✅ **LoRa sensitivity formula:** $S_{min} = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(BW) + NF + SNR_{req}$.

✅ **LoRaWAN 3 device classes:** A (lowest power, UL-triggered DL), B (scheduled DL via ping slots), C (always-on DL, mains-powered).

✅ **LoRaWAN security:** **AES-128** with **NwkSKey** (MIC) + **AppSKey** (payload).

✅ **Weightless** has three flavors — **W (TVWS), N (UNB), P (OQPSK)**; **Weightless-P** is the deployed one.

✅ **NB-IoT** = 3GPP Rel-13, **180 kHz** BW, **164 dB MCL**, **no mobility, no VoLTE**.

✅ **LTE-M** = 3GPP Rel-13, **1.08 MHz** BW, **mobility + VoLTE support**, **155 dB MCL**.

✅ **NB-IoT deployment modes:** **In-band, Guard-band, Standalone** (replace a GSM channel).

✅ **Power saving in 3GPP IoT:** **PSM (Power Saving Mode)** + **eDRX (extended Discontinuous Reception)**.

✅ **LoRaWAN regional plans:** **EU868** (8 channels, 1% duty cycle), **US915** (64 UL + 8 DL channels), **AS923** (Asia).

✅ **Master the trade-off:** Higher SF = longer range + longer Time-on-Air + lower bit rate + more battery drain per packet.

✅ **Link budget equation:** $PL_{max} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{cable} - S_{min}$.

✅ **Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL)** is the 3GPP metric for coverage — NB-IoT leads at 164 dB.

✅ **Free-space path loss** uses exponent 2; real outdoor LoRa uses exponent **2.5–3.5**.

✅ **Network architectures:** LoRaWAN = **star-of-stars**; Sigfox/NB-IoT/LTE-M = **cellular star**; All use **gateway-based** star topology, **not mesh**.

✅ **Exam mantra:** "**Sigfox = tiny telegrams, LoRaWAN = open community, NB-IoT = cellular sensor, LTE-M = mobile voice-capable IoT**."

---

### END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — LPWAN PROTOCOLS
*All the best for your KTU 2024 ESE preparation! 🎓*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Developing IoT - IoT Design Methodology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST834 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Developing IoT - IoT Design Methodology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is IoT Design Methodology?

**IoT Design Methodology** is a structured, layered, and systematic engineering approach used to design, develop, and deploy Internet of Things (IoT) systems. It provides a sequence of well-defined steps that guide an engineer from understanding a real-world problem to delivering a fully functional IoT solution comprising sensors, actuators, communication networks, cloud/edge services, and end-user applications.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the IoT Design Methodology is treated as the **"blueprint"** for converting a problem statement into a working IoT ecosystem. It is domain-independent, meaning the same process can be applied to **smart homes, smart agriculture, healthcare, industrial automation, and smart cities**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board-Exam Ready):**
> "IoT Design Methodology is a multi-step, iterative, and hierarchical procedure that defines purpose, processes, domain entities, information models, services, IoT levels, functional roles, and operational behavior of an IoT system, ensuring interoperability, scalability, and reliability across the sensing–processing–actuation pipeline."

## 1.2 The Eight Steps of IoT Design Methodology (High-Level)

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3), the IoT Design Methodology consists of **eight sequential steps**:

| Step | Name | Purpose (One-Line) |
|------|------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Purpose & Requirements Specification** | Define *what* the system must do |
| 2 | **Process Specification** | Define *how* data flows between components |
| 3 | **Domain Model Specification** | Identify *real-world entities* involved |
| 4 | **Information Model Specification** | Structure the *data* being exchanged |
| 5 | **Service Specification** | List the *services* (CRUD: Create, Read, Update, Delete) |
| 6 | **IoT Level Specification** | Choose the *system architecture level* (1–6) |
| 7 | **Functional View Specification** | Define the *functional components* and their roles |
| 8 | **Operational View Specification** | Define *end-user activities, monitoring, and maintenance* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Idea:** Each step **refines the output of the previous step**, just like a top-down compiler design or waterfall model in software engineering.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Building a House" 🏠

Imagine you want to build a smart house:

- **Step 1 (Purpose):** Decide *why* you are building it (e.g., a 3-bedroom villa for a family of 4).
- **Step 2 (Process):** Sketch the *flow of activity* — how people enter, how water flows, how electricity is distributed.
- **Step 3 (Domain Model):** List the *physical objects* — doors, windows, rooms, garden, garage.
- **Step 4 (Information Model):** Describe the *data sheets* of each object — door has material, size, lock type.
- **Step 5 (Service):** Define *operations* — open door, close window, switch on light.
- **Step 6 (IoT Level):** Decide the *construction technology* — RCC frame, load-bearing, or pre-fabricated (just like IoT Level 1 to 6).
- **Step 7 (Functional View):** Assign *roles* — plumber does plumbing, electrician does wiring, architect does design.
- **Step 8 (Operational View):** Plan *post-construction activities* — who lives there, who maintains, what services run daily.

In the **same way**, IoT Design Methodology transforms a vague idea into a **deployable, maintainable, and scalable IoT product**.

## 1.4 Why a Methodology is Essential?

| Without Methodology | With Methodology |
|---------------------|------------------|
| Random hardware selection | Justified component selection |
| Data format mismatches | Standardized data models |
| Service duplication | Well-defined APIs and services |
| Unclear responsibility | Functional roles clearly defined |
| Difficult scaling | Built-in scalability via IoT Levels |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** Always begin any design question by saying — *"The IoT Design Methodology consists of 8 steps, and we will apply them to the given problem."* This shows the examiner that you understand the framework.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Detailed Analysis of the 8 Steps

### 🔹 Step 1: Purpose & Requirements Specification

This is the **most critical step** because it anchors the entire design. It answers three questions:
1. What problem are we solving?
2. Who are the stakeholders?
3. What are the non-functional requirements (NFRs) like latency, security, power, cost?

**Sub-tasks:**
- **Purpose:** A one-sentence statement describing the goal of the IoT system.
- **Use-case diagram:** Identifies actors (users, admins) and their interactions.
- **Requirements:** Functional + Non-functional.

**Example (Smart Irrigation System):**
- *Purpose:* "To automate irrigation by monitoring soil moisture and weather conditions."
- *Functional:* Read soil moisture, control water pump, send SMS alerts.
- *Non-functional:* Operate for 6 months on battery, work in −10 °C to 50 °C.

### 🔹 Step 2: Process Specification

Here we use **UML Activity Diagrams** or **Data Flow Diagrams (DFD)** to model the *dynamic behavior* of the system.

Key elements:
- **Input → Process → Output** flow
- **Decision nodes** (e.g., "Is moisture < threshold?")
- **Parallel/sequential activities**
- **Sensors and actuators** as nodes

### 🔹 Step 3: Domain Model Specification

The **Domain Model** identifies all *real-world physical entities* and their relationships. It is typically represented as a **UML Class Diagram**.

**Example entities (Smart Home):**
- *Room* (attributes: roomID, name, area)
- *Sensor* (attributes: sensorID, type, location)
- *Actuator* (attributes: actuatorID, type, state)
- *User* (attributes: userID, name, role)

Relationships:
- *Room* **has** *Sensors* (one-to-many)
- *Sensor* **controls** *Actuator* (one-to-one)
- *User* **monitors** *Room* (many-to-many)

### 🔹 Step 4: Information Model Specification

Defines the **data structure** of each entity. It is a detailed table describing:
- Attribute name
- Data type (integer, float, string, boolean)
- Units (°C, %, ppm)
- Range/validity
- Cardinality (single vs. multiple values)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information Model ≠ Database Schema.** It is a logical representation that maps to the database later.

### 🔹 Step 5: Service Specification

Lists the **services** each component provides. Standard service types:

| Service Type | Description | HTTP Equivalent |
|--------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **CRUD Services** | Create, Read, Update, Delete operations | POST, GET, PUT, DELETE |
| **Monitoring** | Real-time observation | WebSocket / MQTT subscribe |
| **Control** | Command execution | POST with actuation command |
| **Notification** | Alert generation | SMS / Email / Push |

For each service, we specify:
- **Inputs:** parameters required
- **Outputs:** returned data
- **Pre-conditions:** system state required
- **Post-conditions:** system state after execution

### 🔹 Step 6: IoT Level Specification

The IoT system is categorized into **6 levels** based on the number of *functional components* (sensors, actuators, services, edge, cloud) and their communication style.

| IoT Level | Components | Typical Use |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Level 1** | Single sensor + cloud service | Simple monitoring |
| **Level 2** | Sensor + actuator + cloud | Closed-loop control |
| **Level 3** | Multiple sensors + edge + cloud | Local decision making |
| **Level 4** | Multiple sensors + actuators + edge + cloud | Edge analytics |
| **Level 5** | Multiple nodes + gateway + cloud | Multi-node coordination |
| **Level 6** | Distributed cloud + federated services | Global-scale IoT |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** If the question mentions "local processing without cloud" → Level 3 or 4. If it mentions "multiple sites" → Level 5 or 6.

### 🔹 Step 7: Functional View Specification

Defines the **functional groups** of the system. KTU uses a 5-component model:

1. **Sensing / Actuation** (Hardware)
2. **Communication** (Protocols: MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, LoRa)
3. **Edge / Fog Computing** (Local processing)
4. **Cloud / Backend** (Storage, analytics)
5. **Application** (Mobile/Web UI)

Each functional group lists its **sub-functions** and **interactions**.

### 🔹 Step 8: Operational View Specification

Focuses on:
- **Run-time behavior**
- **User roles & activities**
- **Monitoring & maintenance**
- **Failure handling & fault tolerance**
- **Update and upgrade path**

Represented using **deployment diagrams** and **maintenance schedules**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For IoT Design Methodology, "formulas" are mostly **qualitative relationships and rules-of-thumb**. Below is the cheat sheet for board problems.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | Use-Case |
|---------|---------------|----------|----------|
| Number of Functional Components | $N_{fc} = N_s + N_a + N_e + N_c + N_{app}$ | $N_s$=sensors, $N_a$=actuators, $N_e$=edge, $N_c$=cloud, $N_{app}$=apps | IoT Level selection |
| Service Response Time (Latency) | $T_{total} = T_{sense} + T_{comm} + T_{proc} + T_{act}$ | All in **milliseconds (ms)** | NFR analysis |
| Throughput | $\theta = \frac{N_{events}}{T_{window}}$ | events per second (eps) | Capacity planning |
| Power Budget | $P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot t_i$ | Watt-hours (Wh) | Battery life |
| Battery Life | $L_b = \frac{C_{battery}}{P_{total}}$ | hours | $C$ in Wh, $P$ in W |
| Network Range Estimation | $R_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}$ where $\lambda$ = wavelength | metres | RF design |
| Data Size | $D = N_{samples} \times S_{sample} \times f_s$ | bytes | Storage planning |
| Cost Estimate | $C_{system} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i + C_{ops}$ | INR/USD | Budgeting |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Rule:** Always state the *unit* beside every numerical value. The marker looks for units in every answer.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Methodology |
|--------|----------------------------|
| **Smart Agriculture** | Soil sensors → Edge controller → Cloud analytics → SMS to farmer |
| **Healthcare** | Wearable ECG → Gateway → Cloud → Doctor dashboard |
| **Smart Cities** | Air-quality sensors across city → Fog node → Central dashboard |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Machine vibration sensor → Edge ML → Predictive maintenance |
| **Smart Home** | Motion sensor → Hub → Cloud → Mobile app notification |

> [!TIP]
> In every KTU design question, you can **pick any one of the above domains** as your case study. The examiner does not pre-specify the domain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Design Problem — "Smart Parking System"

We will apply the **8-step methodology** to design a *Smart Parking System* for a shopping mall. This is a classic KTU question (refer Dec 2023, June 2024 patterns).

### Step 1: Purpose & Requirements Specification

**Purpose:** To detect free parking slots in real-time and guide drivers via a mobile application.

**Functional Requirements (FR):**
- $FR_1$: Detect vehicle presence in each slot.
- $FR_2$: Display available slots on mobile app.
- $FR_3$: Reserve a slot for 15 minutes.
- $FR_4$: Send notification when slot becomes free.

**Non-Functional Requirements (NFR):**
- $NFR_1$: Latency $\leq$ **2 seconds**.
- $NFR_2$: System availability $\geq$ **99.9%**.
- $NFR_3$: Operate 24/7 on solar + battery.
- $NFR_4$: Cost per slot $\leq$ ₹500.

**Use-Case Description:**

$$
\text{Actor} \rightarrow \text{Action} \rightarrow \text{Outcome}
$$

$$
\text{Driver} \xrightarrow{\text{views app}} \text{Sees free slot} \xrightarrow{\text{reserves}} \text{15-min hold}
$$

### Step 2: Process Specification (Activity Flow)

Let us derive the processing flow:

$$
\text{Slot Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Edge Gateway} \rightarrow \text{Cloud DB} \rightarrow \text{Mobile App}
$$

Detailed steps using the process pipeline formula:

$$
T_{total} = T_{sense} + T_{comm} + T_{proc} + T_{act}
$$

Substituting typical values for a smart parking system:

$$
T_{total} = 50\text{ ms} + 200\text{ ms} + 300\text{ ms} + 100\text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 650\text{ ms} \le 2000\text{ ms (NFR requirement)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $T_{total} = 650$ ms $< 2000$ ms, the **latency NFR is satisfied**. This is a typical KTU numerical verification.

### Step 3: Domain Model Specification

**Entities (UML Class Diagram in text form):**

| Class | Attributes | Methods |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| **ParkingSlot** | slotID, status, zone, floor | updateStatus(), getStatus() |
| **Sensor** | sensorID, type, batteryLevel, location | readData(), sleep() |
| **User** | userID, name, vehicleNumber | reserve(), cancel() |
| **Reservation** | resvID, slotID, userID, expiryTime | create(), extend() |
| **Gateway** | gwID, IP, location | aggregate(), forward() |

**Relationships:**

$$
\text{ParkingSlot} \xrightarrow{1 \text{ to } 1} \text{Sensor}
$$

$$
\text{User} \xrightarrow{1 \text{ to } \text{many}} \text{Reservation}
$$

$$
\text{Reservation} \xrightarrow{\text{many to } 1} \text{ParkingSlot}
$$

### Step 4: Information Model Specification

| Attribute | Data Type | Unit | Range |
|-----------|-----------|------|-------|
| `slotID` | string | — | 3 characters |
| `status` | enum | — | $\{free, occupied, reserved\}$ |
| `zone` | char | — | A–D |
| `batteryLevel` | float | % | $[0, 100]$ |
| `timestamp` | ISO 8601 | UTC | valid date-time |
| `vehicleNumber` | string | — | 10 chars |

### Step 5: Service Specification

| Service Name | Inputs | Outputs | HTTP Method |
|--------------|--------|---------|-------------|
| `getSlotStatus(slotID)` | slotID | status JSON | GET |
| `reserveSlot(slotID, userID)` | slotID, userID | reservation JSON | POST |
| `cancelReservation(resvID)` | resvID | success/fail | DELETE |
| `getAllFreeSlots(zone)` | zone | list of slots | GET |
| `notifyUser(userID, msg)` | userID, msg | delivery status | POST |

> [!TIP]
> Use the **CRUD acronym (Create, Read, Update, Delete)** in the exam to remember service types.

### Step 6: IoT Level Specification

Since the system uses:
- Multiple sensors
- Edge gateway for aggregation
- Cloud database
- Mobile app

This matches **IoT Level 5**.

**Justification:**

$$
N_{fc} = N_s + N_a + N_e + N_c + N_{app}
$$

$$
N_{fc} = 200 + 0 + 5 + 1 + 2 = 208 \text{ functional components}
$$

Since $N_{fc} > 100$ and includes **multi-node + gateway + cloud** coordination, the system is classified as **Level 5**.

### Step 7: Functional View Specification

| Functional Group | Sub-functions | Technology |
|------------------|--------------|------------|
| **Sensing** | Vehicle detection, battery monitoring | IR sensor, ultrasonic |
| **Communication** | Sensor → Gateway | LoRaWAN, Zigbee |
| **Edge Computing** | Local aggregation, filtering | Raspberry Pi |
| **Cloud** | Storage, analytics | AWS IoT, Firebase |
| **Application** | User interface, notifications | React Native, Firebase Messaging |

### Step 8: Operational View Specification

- **Monitoring:** Live dashboard with slot heat-map.
- **Maintenance:** Sensor battery check every 30 days; firmware OTA updates monthly.
- **Fault Handling:** If sensor fails, mark slot as "unknown" and exclude from booking.
- **Backup:** Cloud DB replicated across 3 availability zones for **99.9%** uptime.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing IoT Levels

| IoT Level | Sensing | Edge | Cloud | App | Multi-Node | KTU Example |
|-----------|---------|------|-------|-----|------------|-------------|
| 1 | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Weather station |
| 2 | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Smart bulb |
| 3 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Local irrigation |
| 4 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Limited | Edge AI camera |
| 5 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Smart factory |
| 6 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Global | Smart city |

## 3.3 Latency Budget Derivation (Generic)

For any IoT system, the end-to-end latency is given by:

$$
T_{e2e} = T_{sense} + T_{tx} + T_{net} + T_{proc} + T_{rx} + T_{app}
$$

Where:
- $T_{sense}$ — time to read sensor data
- $T_{tx}$ — transmit time over physical layer
- $T_{net}$ — network propagation delay
- $T_{proc}$ — server processing time
- $T_{rx}$ — receive time at application
- $T_{app}$ — application rendering time

**Reliability constraint:**

$$
R_{system} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i \ge R_{target}
$$

Where $R_i$ is the reliability of the $i^{th}$ component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability multiplies, not adds.** If each of 4 components has 95% reliability, system reliability $= 0.95^4 = 0.8145 = 81.45\%$, which may violate the NFR.

## 3.4 Power Budget Derivation

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{(P_{active} \times t_{active}) + (P_{sleep} \times t_{sleep})}{t_{active} + t_{sleep}}
$$

For a duty-cycled sensor:

$$
D = \frac{t_{active}}{t_{active} + t_{sleep}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{avg} = P_{active} \cdot D + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D)
$$

**Battery life:**

$$
L_b = \frac{C}{P_{avg}}
$$

**Example:** If $C = 2200$ mAh × 3.7 V = 8140 mWh = **8.14 Wh**, and $P_{avg} = 0.1$ W, then:

$$
L_b = \frac{8.14 \text{ Wh}}{0.1 \text{ W}} = 81.4 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the type of numerical KTU loves to ask — combine a methodology step with a quick calculation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The 8-Step IoT Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["STEP 1 Purpose and Requirements"] --> A2["STEP 2 Process Specification"]
    A2 --> A3["STEP 3 Domain Model"]
    A3 --> A4["STEP 4 Information Model"]
    A4 --> A5["STEP 5 Service Specification"]
    A5 --> A6["STEP 6 IoT Level Selection"]
    A6 --> A7["STEP 7 Functional View"]
    A7 --> A8["STEP 8 Operational View"]
    A8 --> A9["DEPLOYED IoT SYSTEM"]
    A1 -.feedback.-> A3
    A5 -.feedback.-> A2
    A8 -.feedback.-> A6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Solid arrows** indicate forward progression; **dashed arrows** indicate iterative refinement — a key KTU point that the methodology is **not strictly linear**.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic IoT System

```mermaid
    node1 --> node5

    node5 --> node4
    node5 --> node6
    node5 --> node7
    node5 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10

    node10 --> node13

```

## 4.3 IoT Level Decision Tree (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1["Start Design"] --> B2{"Single sensor only?"}
    B2 -- Yes --> B3["LEVEL 1"]
    B2 -- No --> B4{"Actuator present?"}
    B4 -- No --> B5{"Multiple sensors?"}
    B4 -- Yes --> B6["LEVEL 2"]
    B5 -- No --> B3
    B5 -- Yes --> B7{"Edge processing?"}
    B7 -- No --> B8["LEVEL 3"]
    B7 -- Yes --> B9{"Multi-node network?"}
    B9 -- No --> B10["LEVEL 4"]
    B9 -- Yes --> B11["LEVEL 5"]
    B11 --> B12{"Federated cloud?"}
    B12 -- No --> B11
    B12 -- Yes --> B13["LEVEL 6"]
```

## 4.4 Mapping Steps to Design Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["Step 1 Purpose"] --> D1["Document Use Case Diagram"]
    C2["Step 2 Process"] --> D2["Activity Diagram"]
```
