(code, resp) = self.data(msg.as_bytes(policy=msg.policy)) 8. return {} # empty dict means "no refusals" ``` The actual wire commands are then: ``` MAIL FROM: RCPT TO: RCPT TO: DATA \r\n \r\n .\r\n ``` # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics > The following Mermaid diagrams are **compiled-safe**: all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; no reserved keywords are used; no markdown formatting appears inside node labels. --- ## 4.1 End-to-End Email Delivery from an IoT Sensor ```mermaid flowchart TD A[IoT Sensor MCU] -->|Reads Humidity| B[Edge Gateway Raspberry Pi] B -->|Threshold Breached 80 percent| C[Python Script dht11_alert.py] C -->|Build EmailMessage| D[EmailMessage Object] D -->|smtplib SMTP Port 587| E[STARTTLS Handshake] E -->|TLS 1.2 Encrypted| F[SMTP Server smtp.gmail.com] F -->|AUTH LOGIN| G[Google SMTP Authenticator] G -->|Return 235 Accepted| F F -->|MAIL FROM RCPT TO DATA| H[Google Outbound Queue] H -->|DNS MX Lookup| I[Recipient Mail Server] I -->|Stores in INBOX| J[Owner Mobile Gmail App] J -->|Push Notification| K[User Sees Alert] ``` --- ## 4.2 SMTP State Machine — Internal Lifecycle of `smtplib.SMTP` ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Connected: connect host port Connected --> Greeted: ehlo hostname Greeted --> Encrypted: starttls Encrypted --> ReGreeted: ehlo hostname ReGreeted --> Authenticated: login user pass Greeted --> NoAuth: open relay permits no auth NoAuth --> MailSession: send_message Authenticated --> MailSession: send_message MailSession --> DataStream: data command issued DataStream --> MailSession: 250 OK queued MailSession --> [*]: quit 221 Encrypted --> [*]: exception 530 STARTTLS required Authenticated --> [*]: exception 535 auth failed ``` --- ## 4.3 Block Architecture — IoT Alert Pipeline Using SMTPLib ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph EdgeLayer["Edge Layer"] S1[Temp Sensor] --> S2[HVAC Actuator] S2 --> S3[Edge Gateway Python Runtime] end subgraph ApplicationLayer["Application Layer"] S3 --> S4[Threshold Logic Module] S4 --> S5[Email Composer EmailMessage] S5 --> S6[SMTP Transport smtplib] end subgraph CloudLayer["Cloud Layer"] S6 --> S7[TLS Tunnel Port 587] S7 --> S8[Provider SMTP Relay] S8 --> S9[Recipient MX Server] S9 --> S10[User Inbox] end S4 -.->|Normal Range| S1 ``` --- ## 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Authentication Handshake ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as Python smtplib Client participant Server as Gmail SMTP Client->>Server: TCP SYN to port 587 Server-->>Client: 220 smtp.gmail.com ESMTP ready Client->>Server: EHLO greenhouse-pi-01 Server-->>Client: 250 AUTH LOGIN PLAIN XOAUTH2 and STARTTLS Client->>Server: STARTTLS Server-->>Client: 220 Ready to start TLS Note over Client,Server: TLS handshake completes on port 587 Client->>Server: EHLO greenhouse-pi-01 Server-->>Client: 250 capability list re-issued Client->>Server: AUTH LOGIN Server-->>Client: 334 VXNlcm5hbWU6 base64 Username prompt Client->>Server: Z3JlZW5ob3VzZS1pb3RAZ21haWwuY29t Server-->>Client: 334 UGFzc3dvcmQ6 base64 Password prompt Client->>Server: YWJjZCBlZmdoIGlqa2wgbW5vcA== Server-->>Client: 235 2.7.0 Accepted ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between `smtplib.SMTP` and `smtplib.SMTP_SSL` in Python. In which scenario is each used?** **Model Answer:** > `smtplib.SMTP` establishes a **plain-text** TCP connection to the SMTP server. The caller must subsequently invoke `starttls()` to upgrade the channel to TLS. It is the correct class for port **587** (submission with STARTTLS). > > `smtplib.SMTP_SSL` wraps the entire socket in TLS **immediately upon connection** (implicit TLS). It is used for port **465** (legacy SMTPS) where the server expects the first byte to be a TLS ClientHello. Calling `starttls()` inside `SMTP_SSL` raises `SMTPNotSupportedError` because the channel is already encrypted. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List any three exception classes defined by the `smtplib` module and state the condition under which each is raised.** **Model Answer:** 1. **`smtplib.SMTPAuthenticationError`** — Raised when the `login()` call is rejected by the server. The SMTP reply code is **535** ("Authentication credentials invalid"). Common cause: wrong password, or using a normal Gmail password instead of a 16-character App Password. **(1 mark)** 2. **`smtplib.SMTPRecipientsRefused`** — Raised when **all** recipients are rejected. The exception's `.recipients` attribute is a dict of `{addr: (code, msg)}`. Raised by `sendmail()` / `send_message()`. **(1 mark)** 3. **`smtplib.SMTPDataError`** — Raised when the server rejects the `DATA` content (e.g., spam-flagged body, oversized attachment, DKIM failure). Reply code is typically **554**. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** *Answer any ONE full question from this module. Each question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.* --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the SMTP protocol with reference to its command-response model. List the major command verbs and their associated 3-digit reply codes. **(b) [7 marks]** Write a complete Python program using `smtplib` and `EmailMessage` that reads a temperature value from a DHT11 sensor (simulated with a variable) and sends an **HTML-formatted** email alert to `farmer@ktu.ac.in` when the value exceeds 50 °C. The email must use Gmail's SMTP server on port 587 with STARTTLS. --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### Part (a) — SMTP Command-Response Model SMTP (defined in **RFC 5321**) is a **line-oriented, text-based** protocol. Each command is an ASCII verb followed by a space and an argument, terminated by ``. The server replies with a 3-digit code indicating the outcome of the command. | Command Verb | Purpose | Example Argument | Success Code | |---|---|---|---| | `HELO` / `EHLO` | Identify client | `EHLO client-domain` | **250** | | `STARTTLS` | Upgrade to TLS | — | **220** | | `AUTH` | Authenticate | `AUTH LOGIN` | **235** | | `MAIL FROM` | Start mail transaction | `MAIL FROM:` | **250** | | `RCPT TO` | Declare a recipient | `RCPT TO:` | **250** | | `DATA` | Stream message body | — | **354** (intermediate) / **250** (final) | | `RSET` | Abort current transaction | — | **250** | | `VRFY` | Verify address (often disabled) | `VRFY user` | **250 / 252** | | `NOOP` | Keep-alive | — | **250** | | `QUIT` | Close session | — | **221** | The **first digit** of the reply code carries meta-information: * `2yz` → Positive completion * `3yz` → Positive intermediate (e.g., `354` for DATA) * `4yz` → Transient negative (retry possible) * `5yz` → Permanent negative (do not retry) `smtplib` returns these tuples to the Python caller, but **automatically raises an exception** for `4yz` and `5yz` replies inside `send_message()`. **(7 marks — distribute: 2 marks command list, 2 marks reply codes, 2 marks RFC, 1 mark smtplib behaviour)** --- #### Part (b) — Program ```python import random import smtplib from email.message import EmailMessage # ---------- (1) Simulated sensor read ---------- def read_dht11(): # In production: import Adafruit_DHT, sensor = Adafruit_DHT.DHT11, ... return random.uniform(15.0, 60.0) # °C # ---------- (2) Compose HTML email ---------- def build_alert(temp_c: float) -> EmailMessage: msg = EmailMessage() msg['From'] = 'greenhouse-iot@gmail.com' msg['To'] = 'farmer@ktu.ac.in' msg['Subject'] = f'[ALERT] Temperature {temp_c:.1f} °C exceeds threshold' msg.set_content( f"Automated alert from greenhouse-pi-01.\nTemperature: {temp_c:.1f} °C." ) msg.add_alternative(f"""

Greenhouse Temperature Alert

Sensor DHT11 on GPIO 4 reported {temp_c:.1f} °C at 2024-11-18 14:32 IST.

Threshold: 50 °C. Action: exhaust fan engaged.

""", subtype='html') return msg # ---------- (3) SMTP transport ---------- def send(msg: EmailMessage) -> None: with smtplib.SMTP(host='smtp.gmail.com', port=587, timeout=30) as s: s.ehlo('greenhouse-pi-01') s.starttls() s.ehlo('greenhouse-pi-01') s.login('greenhouse-iot@gmail.com', 'abcd efgh ijkl mnop') s.send_message(msg) # ---------- (4) Threshold dispatcher ---------- if __name__ == '__main__': THRESHOLD = 50.0 t = read_dht11() print(f'Sensor read: {t:.1f} °C') if t > THRESHOLD: send(build_alert(t)) print('Alert email dispatched.') else: print('Nominal. No email sent.') ``` **Mark Allocation Guide (7 marks):** | Component | Marks | |---|---| | Correct import of `smtplib` and `EmailMessage` | 1 | | Proper header setting (`From`, `To`, `Subject`) | 1 | | HTML body construction using `add_alternative(subtype='html')` | 2 | | Correct SMTP host/port (`smtp.gmail.com`, `587`) | 1 | | STARTTLS upgrade **before** `login()` | 1 | | Use of `with` context manager / `try-except` | 1 | | **Total** | **7** | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the security vulnerabilities of using SMTP without encryption. Explain how `starttls()` and `SMTP_SSL` mitigate these. **(b) [7 marks]** Write a Python script that sends an email with **a PDF attachment** using `smtplib`. Mention the MIME types used and the role of the `add_attachment()` method. --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### Part (a) — SMTP Security & TLS Plain SMTP transmits every byte — including the `AUTH LOGIN` base64-encoded credentials and the entire message body — in **clear text**. An attacker on the same Wi-Fi network (or anyone tapping a backbone link) can reconstruct: * The sender and recipient addresses (`MAIL FROM`, `RCPT TO`). * The **base64 username** and **base64 password** (trivially decoded — base64 is **encoding**, not encryption). * The complete email body and any attachments. This violates **CIA Triad** *Confidentiality* and *Integrity*. **Mitigations offered by `smtplib`:** 1. **`SMTP.starttls()`** — Implements **opportunistic STARTTLS** (RFC 3207). The client issues `STARTTLS`; both peers perform a TLS handshake on the existing TCP socket; all subsequent traffic — including `AUTH` and `DATA` — is encrypted. The minimum recommended version is **TLS 1.2** with forward-secrecy cipher suites (configured via `ssl.create_default_context()`). 2. **`smtplib.SMTP_SSL()`** — Implements **implicit TLS** (RFC 8314). The TLS handshake occurs *before* any SMTP verb is sent. This is the modern recommended approach (port **465**). It removes the *opportunistic* downgrade attack surface present in STARTTLS. 3. **App Passwords / OAuth 2.0** — Even with TLS, credentials should be **rotated** and **scoped**. Google now mandates 16-character App Passwords; `smtplib` supports OAuth 2.0 via `XOAUTH2` mechanism if a token string is supplied. **(7 marks — 2 each for vulnerabilities, starttls, SMTP_SSL; 1 for credential hygiene)** --- #### Part (b) — PDF Attachment Script ```python import smtplib from email.message import EmailMessage from pathlib import Path # ---------- Build the message ---------- msg = EmailMessage() msg['From'] = 'reports@ktu.ac.in' msg['To'] = 'hod@ktu.ac.in' msg['Subject'] = 'Daily IoT Telemetry Report (PDF)' msg.set_content( "Dear Sir,\n\n" "Please find attached the daily IoT telemetry report.\n\n" "Regards,\nIoT Gateway" ) pdf_path = Path('/home/pi/reports/daily_2024-11-18.pdf') pdf_bytes = pdf_path.read_bytes() msg.add_attachment( pdf_bytes, maintype='application', # top-level MIME type subtype='pdf', # specific subtype filename=pdf_path.name, # appears in attachment header cid='daily_report' # optional Content-ID for inlining ) ``` **Sending the email via implicit TLS (port 465):** ```python with smtplib.SMTP_SSL('smtp.gmail.com', 465, timeout=30) as server: server.login('reports@ktu.ac.in', 'app-password-16-chars') server.send_message(msg) ``` **Role of `add_attachment()`:** It packages the binary bytes as a **Base64-encoded MIME part** with the following auto-generated headers, conforming to **RFC 2045** (MIME) and **RFC 2046** (media types): ``` Content-Type: application/pdf; name="daily_2024-11-18.pdf" Content-Transfer-Encoding: base64 Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="daily_2024-11-18.pdf" Content-ID: ``` **Mark Allocation Guide (7 marks):** | Component | Marks | |---|---| | Correct `maintype='application'` and `subtype='pdf'` | 2 | | Reading bytes with `read_bytes()` (binary mode) | 1 | | Using `add_attachment()` correctly with `filename` | 2 | | Using `SMTP_SSL` on port 465 (or STARTTLS on 587) | 1 | | Correct `From`, `To`, `Subject` headers | 1 | | **Total** | **7** | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in SMTPLib questions — Top 5 traps:** > > 1. **Forgetting `starttls()` before `login()` on port 587** → Server returns `530 5.7.0 Must issue a STARTTLS command first`. Examiner deducts **2 marks**. > 2. **Calling `starttls()` after `SMTP_SSL()`** → `SMTPNotSupportedError`. The two are *mutually exclusive*. Deduct **1 mark**. > 3. **Sending the message body as a Python `str` to `sendmail()`** → TypeError. You must use `send_message(msg)` *or* `sendmail(from, to, msg.as_string())`. Deduct **1 mark**. > 4. **Treating `Bcc` as visible** → `Bcc` is *consumed* by `send_message()` and *stripped* from the message body. A student who claims "Bcc appears in headers" loses **1 mark**. > 5. **Using the user's real Gmail password** — Examiner deducts **1 mark** if you don't mention *App Password* / *OAuth 2.0*. Since May 2022, plain-text passwords are rejected. > 6. **Not using a `with` block / `try-finally`** — A subtle 1-mark penalty for *ungraceful* socket handling in IoT code. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this and stick it on your wall before the exam.** - [x] `smtplib` is a **standard library**, no `pip install` needed. - [x] Always use `EmailMessage` (modern, policy-aware) — avoid raw `MIMEMultipart` unless required. - [x] **Port 587** → `SMTP(...)` + `starttls()` + `login()`. - [x] **Port 465** → `SMTP_SSL(...)` + `login()` (no `starttls`!). - [x] **Port 25** → Server-to-server, almost always blocked by ISPs. - [x] `ehlo()` is called **twice**: once before `starttls()`, once after. - [x] Use **App Passwords** (16 chars) for Gmail — *never* the account password. - [x] Wrap SMTP operations in `with smtplib.SMTP(...) as s:` for automatic socket close. - [x] `send_message(msg)` internally issues `MAIL`, `RCPT`, `DATA`; `Bcc` is stripped from headers. - [x] `add_attachment(data, maintype, subtype, filename)` handles Base64 encoding automatically. - [x] Add HTML alongside plain text using `add_alternative(html, subtype='html')` for client fallback. - [x] Catch `SMTPAuthenticationError`, `SMTPRecipientsRefused`, `SMTPDataError`, `SMTPException`. - [x] For IoT: use `set_debuglevel(1)` during development to see the wire trace. - [x] Default `timeout=30` seconds prevents indefinite hangs on flaky IoT links. - [x] Maximum Gmail message size: **25 MB**; Outlook: **33 MB**; Yahoo: **25 MB**. - [x] RFC references: **RFC 5321** (SMTP), **RFC 3207** (STARTTLS), **RFC 8314** (SMTPS), **RFC 2045–2049** (MIME).

topics.title: Programming Raspberry Pi with Python - Controlling LED with Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST834 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Programming Raspberry Pi with Python - Controlling LED with Raspberry Pi # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Raspberry Pi (RPi):** A series of small, single-board computers (SBCs) developed by the Raspberry Pi Foundation in the United Kingdom. In the context of the Internet of Things (IoT), the Raspberry Pi functions as a low-cost, credit-card sized edge-computing node capable of reading digital/analog inputs from sensors and driving actuators through its general-purpose input/output (GPIO) header. > [!IMPORTANT] > **General-Purpose Input/Output (GPIO):** A generic pin on an integrated circuit (IC) or microcontroller whose behavior—including whether it is configured as an input or output—is controllable by the user at runtime. On the Raspberry Pi, the GPIO header exposes 26 or 40 physical pins (depending on model) operating at a logic level of **3.3 V DC**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Light Emitting Diode (LED) Interfacing:** The process of electrically and logically connecting a semiconductor light-emitting diode to a digital output pin of a host controller (Raspberry Pi) such that a HIGH logic level turns the LED ON and a LOW logic level turns it OFF, mediated by a current-limiting series resistor. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the Raspberry Pi as the **human brain** and the GPIO pins as the **nerve endings in your fingers**. Your brain decides "I want to touch the doorbell," sends an electrical signal through the nerves (GPIO pins) to your finger, and your finger presses the button (the LED lights up). In this analogy: - The **brain's decision** = a Python `if` statement (e.g., `if time == "evening":`). - The **nerve signal** = a `HIGH` (3.3 V) voltage on a GPIO pin. - The **finger/doorbell** = the LED with its protective current-limiting resistor. - The **muscle movement** = current flowing from the pin, through the resistor, through the LED, to ground. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Logic Levels on Raspberry Pi GPIO:** > - **HIGH** = **3.3 V** (logic 1) > - **LOW** = **0 V / GND** (logic 0) > - **Maximum source/sink current per pin:** ~**16 mA** (with a total bank limit of ~50 mA). ## 1.3 Why We Cannot Connect an LED Directly A bare LED without a resistor behaves like a **short circuit** once its forward voltage is exceeded. It will draw unlimited current, destroying both the LED and the Raspberry Pi's GPIO pin. Hence, a **current-limiting resistor** is mandatory in every practical interfacing circuit. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the LED Control System The complete process of "controlling an LED with a Raspberry Pi using Python" can be decomposed into the following structured logic steps: 1. **Hardware Provisioning** - Identify the physical pin layout of the RPi model (e.g., 40-pin header on Pi 3/4/Zero). - Select one GPIO pin (commonly **GPIO 17** / physical pin 11) as the OUTPUT. - Connect the LED's **anode** (+) through a current-limiting resistor to the chosen GPIO pin. - Connect the LED's **cathode** (−) to any **GND** pin on the header. 2. **Electrical Conditioning (Ohm's Law Application)** - The Raspberry Pi GPIO outputs **3.3 V** when HIGH. - A typical red LED has a forward voltage drop $V_f \approx 2.0$ V and a safe continuous forward current $I_f \approx 20$ mA. - A series resistor must absorb the remaining voltage and limit current to a safe value. 3. **Software Layer (Python with RPi.GPIO library)** - Import the GPIO library: `import RPi.GPIO as GPIO`. - Choose a numbering scheme: `GPIO.BCM` (Broadcom SOC channel) or `GPIO.BOARD` (physical pin number). - Set warnings off and define pin mode: `GPIO.setup(17, GPIO.OUT)`. - Drive the pin HIGH/LOW: `GPIO.output(17, GPIO.HIGH)`. - Clean up on exit: `GPIO.cleanup()`. 4. **Execution Flow** - The Python interpreter (running on the RPi's ARM CPU) translates `GPIO.output()` calls into register-level writes to the BCM2835/BCM2837/BCM2711 SoC. - The SoC drives the corresponding physical pin to 3.3 V. - Current flows → LED emits photons. ## 2.2 GPIO Numbering Schemes (Critical for KTU Exams) | Scheme | Constant in Code | Example Value | Description | |---|---|---|---| | **BCM** | `GPIO.BCM` | `17` | Refers to the Broadcom SoC channel number. **Recommended** for hardware portability across RPi revisions. | | **BOARD** | `GPIO.BOARD` | `11` | Refers to the physical pin position on the 40-pin header. Easier for beginners tracing wires. | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit | Governing Relation | |---|---|---|---| | $V_{CC}$ | RPi GPIO HIGH output voltage | **3.3 V** | — | | $V_f$ | LED forward voltage drop | **1.8 V – 3.2 V** (color-dependent) | — | | $I_f$ | LED safe forward current | **10 mA – 20 mA** | — | | $R_S$ | Series current-limiting resistor | **$\Omega$** | $R_S = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_f}$ | | $P_{R_S}$ | Power dissipated by resistor | **W (Watt)** | $P_{R_S} = I_f^{2} \cdot R_S$ | | $V_{GPIO,max}$ | Maximum GPIO voltage tolerance | **3.3 V** | Never apply **5 V** to a GPIO pin. | > [!NOTE] > **Color-wise Typical Forward Voltages (for reference):** > - **Red / Yellow** ≈ **2.0 V** > - **Green** ≈ **2.1 V** > - **Blue / White** ≈ **3.0 – 3.2 V** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility LED interfacing is the **"Hello, World!" of physical computing**, but its industrial applications extend to: - **Status indicators** on IoT gateways (power, network, fault LEDs). - **Optical signaling** in PLC and SCADA systems. - **Visual feedback loops** in HMI (Human-Machine Interface) panels. - **Light-based data transmission** (Li-Fi research prototypes). - **Smart agriculture** greenhouse notification systems. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sizing the Current-Limiting Resistor This is the **single most important calculation** the examiner expects for this topic. We derive the resistance required to safely light a red LED from a 3.3 V Raspberry Pi GPIO pin. **Given (Standard Assumptions for KTU Problems):** - Raspberry Pi GPIO HIGH voltage: $V_{CC} = 3.3$ V - Red LED forward voltage drop: $V_f = 2.0$ V - Desired LED forward current: $I_f = 20$ mA $= 0.020$ A **Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop.** The GPIO pin, the resistor, and the LED form a single series loop. The sum of voltage drops must equal the supply voltage. $$V_{CC} = V_{R_S} + V_f$$ **Step 2: Solve for the voltage drop across the resistor.** $$V_{R_S} = V_{CC} - V_f$$ Substituting numerical values: $$V_{R_S} = 3.3 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V} = 1.3 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3: Apply Ohm's Law to the resistor.** By definition, Ohm's Law states $V = I \cdot R$. Rearranging for $R_S$: $$R_S = \frac{V_{R_S}}{I_f}$$ **Step 4: Substitute and compute.** $$R_S = \frac{1.3 \text{ V}}{0.020 \text{ A}} = 65 \, \Omega$$ **Step 5: Choose the next-higher standard EIA resistor value.** A **65 Ω** resistor does not exist in the standard E12/E24 series. We always round **up** (not down) for safety so that current is *less* than the maximum rating. $$\boxed{R_S^{\text{chosen}} = 220 \, \Omega \text{ (standard E12 value)}}$$ **Step 6: Verify the new operating current with the chosen resistor.** $$I_f^{\text{actual}} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{R_S^{\text{chosen}}} = \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{220} = \frac{1.3}{220}$$ $$I_f^{\text{actual}} \approx 5.91 \text{ mA}$$ This is well within the **10–20 mA** safe range and well below the RPi's **16 mA per-pin** source limit. ✓ **Step 7: Compute the power rating required for the resistor.** $$P_{R_S} = (I_f^{\text{actual}})^2 \cdot R_S^{\text{chosen}} = (0.00591)^2 \cdot 220$$ $$P_{R_S} = 3.49 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 220 \approx 7.69 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} = 7.69 \text{ mW}$$ A standard **$\frac{1}{4}$ W (250 mW)** resistor provides a safety margin of more than 30×. ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Why a 5 V Arduino Cannot Replace a 3.3 V RPi GPIO (Conceptual) Although Arduino digital outputs are 5 V, plugging an Arduino 5 V output into a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin will **destroy** the Broadcom SoC because its I/O ring transistors are rated for **3.3 V max**. A logic-level converter or a simple resistor divider is mandatory for cross-voltage interfacing. KTU frequently tests this awareness. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Raspberry Pi GPIO | Arduino UNO Digital Pin | |---|---|---| | Logic HIGH voltage | **3.3 V** | **5.0 V** | | Max pin current | **~16 mA** | **~40 mA** | | Pin count (typical header) | **40** | **14 digital + 6 analog** | | ADC built-in | None (external MCP3008 needed) | 10-bit, 6 channels | | Library | `RPi.GPIO` / `gpiozero` | `digitalWrite()` | | Safe for 5 V input? | **No – destructive** | Yes | ## 3.4 Pin Map Reference (Most-Used Pins for KTU Practicals) | Physical Pin | BCM GPIO | Common Use | |---|---|---| | Pin 11 | GPIO 17 | LED / Buzzer output | | Pin 13 | GPIO 27 | Second LED | | Pin 15 | GPIO 22 | Button input | | Pin 16 | GPIO 23 | Relay control | | Pin 6 / 9 / 14 / 20 / 25 / 30 / 34 / 39 | GND | Common ground returns | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hardware Circuit Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR RPi["Raspberry Pi GPIO 17
Physical Pin 11
3.3V Logic Output"] R["Current-Limiting
Resistor Rs = 220 ohm"] LED["LED
Forward Voltage 2.0V
Forward Current ~6mA"] GND["GND
Physical Pin 6"] PWR["3.3V Rail
from BCM SoC"] PWi -. supplies .-> RPi RPi -- HIGH 3.3V --> R R -- limits current --> LED LED -- cathode --> GND PWR -. derived from .-> RPi ``` ## 4.2 Python Program Control Flowchart ```mermaid graph TD startA([Program Start]) --> impStep["import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time"] impStep --> modeSel["Set Mode: GPIO.BCM
Set Warnings: False"] modeSel --> pinCfg["GPIO.setup pin 17 as OUTPUT"] pinCfg --> loopStart{Loop Iteration
i in range 5} loopStart -- Yes --> ledOn["GPIO.output 17, HIGH
LED turns ON"] ledOn --> wait1["time.sleep 1 second"] wait1 --> ledOff["GPIO.output 17, LOW
LED turns OFF"] ledOff --> wait2["time.sleep 1 second"] wait2 --> loopStart loopStart -- No --> cleanStep["GPIO.cleanup
Release pin resources"] cleanStep --> stopNode([Program End]) ``` ## 4.3 Reference Python Program (Canonical Implementation) ```python # Program: Blink an LED connected to GPIO 17 (Physical Pin 11) # Hardware: Red LED + 220 ohm resistor in series, cathode to GND import RPi.GPIO as GPIO import time # --- Step 1: Pin Numbering Configuration --- # We use BCM numbering for cross-RPi-model portability GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) GPIO.setwarnings(False) # --- Step 2: Pin Declaration --- LED_PIN = 17 GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT) # --- Step 3: Blink Loop --- try: for i in range(5): GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH) # LED ON time.sleep(1) # Wait 1 second GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW) # LED OFF time.sleep(1) # Wait 1 second except KeyboardInterrupt: pass # Graceful exit on Ctrl+C # --- Step 4: Resource Cleanup --- finally: GPIO.cleanup() print("GPIO pins released. Program terminated safely.") ``` ## 4.4 System-Level Block Architecture (RPi as IoT Node) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Cloud["Cloud / Network Layer"] cloudNode["MQTT Broker /
Web Dashboard"] end subgraph Edge["Raspberry Pi Edge Node"] appLogic["Python Application
business logic"] gpioLib["RPi.GPIO Library
register-level driver"] socCtl["BCM2711 SoC
GPIO Controller"] end subgraph Physical["Physical / Actuator Layer"] ledNode["LED + 220 ohm Resistor"] sensorNode["Optional Sensor
e.g. DHT22, PIR"] end cloudNode -- "MQTT / HTTP" --> appLogic appLogic -- "GPIO.output call" --> gpioLib gpioLib -- "register write" --> socCtl socCtl -- "3.3V on Pin 11" --> ledNode sensorNode -- "data back" --> socCtl socCtl -- "register read" --> gpioLib gpioLib -- "GPIO.input call" --> appLogic ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **What is a GPIO pin? Explain the two numbering conventions used in Raspberry Pi.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A General-Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pin is a software-controllable physical pin on the Raspberry Pi's 40-pin header that can be dynamically configured as either an input (to read sensor states) or an output (to drive actuators). It operates at a **3.3 V** logic level. The two numbering conventions are: 1. **BCM (Broadcom) Numbering:** Uses the Broadcom SoC channel numbers (e.g., GPIO 17). Hardware-portable across RPi models — **2 marks for definition + 1 mark for conventions.** ### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Why is a current-limiting resistor essential when interfacing an LED to a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A current-limiting resistor is essential to restrict the current flowing through the LED to a safe value (typically 10–20 mA). Without it, the LED would draw excessive current from the GPIO pin (rated for only **~16 mA**), causing permanent damage to both the LED and the Broadcom SoC's internal transistor. The resistor also drops the excess voltage (**3.3 V − V_f**) safely as heat. — **1 mark each for current protection, voltage drop, and GPIO safety.** --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] ### Question A **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain how to interface an LED to a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin. Derive the value of the current-limiting resistor required for a red LED (V_f = 2.0 V, I_f = 20 mA) operating from a 3.3 V GPIO. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** Write a complete Python program using the `RPi.GPIO` library to blink the LED five times with a 1-second ON and 1-second OFF interval. Handle `KeyboardInterrupt` gracefully. **\[7 marks\]** ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — 7 marks **Circuit Description (3 marks):** - The anode of the LED is connected to **GPIO 17 (Physical Pin 11)** through a current-limiting resistor **R_S**. - The cathode of the LED is connected to **GND (Physical Pin 6)**. - The 3.3 V logic HIGH from the RPi drives current through R_S and the LED to ground. **Resistor Derivation (4 marks):** $$V_{CC} = 3.3 \text{ V}, \quad V_f = 2.0 \text{ V}, \quad I_f = 20 \text{ mA} = 0.020 \text{ A}$$ By KVL: $V_{R_S} = V_{CC} - V_f = 3.3 - 2.0 = 1.3$ V By Ohm's Law: $$R_S = \frac{V_{R_S}}{I_f} = \frac{1.3}{0.020} = 65 \, \Omega$$ Next higher standard value: **220 Ω** (E12 series). Actual current with 220 Ω: $I_f = 1.3 / 220 \approx 5.91$ mA ✓ (safe). #### Part (b) — 7 marks ```python import RPi.GPIO as GPIO import time LED_PIN = 17 # 1 mark: pin declaration GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) # 1 mark: numbering mode GPIO.setwarnings(False) # 1 mark: warning suppression GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT) # 1 mark: pin as output try: # 1 mark: exception handling for i in range(5): # 1 mark: loop for blinking GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH) time.sleep(1) GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW) time.sleep(1) except KeyboardInterrupt: pass finally: GPIO.cleanup() # 1 mark: cleanup ``` --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023] ### Question B **(a)** Compare the BCM and BOARD pin numbering schemes in Raspberry Pi. With a suitable example, show how each is declared in Python. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** Design a complete IoT-based smart-room LED indicator system using a Raspberry Pi. The system should turn ON a green LED when a PIR motion sensor detects movement and turn ON a red LED otherwise. Provide the circuit description, pin allocation table, and the complete Python program using `gpiozero` library. **\[7 marks\]** ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — 7 marks | Aspect | BCM Numbering | BOARD Numbering | |---|---|---| | Constant | `GPIO.BCM` | `GPIO.BOARD` | | Refers to | Broadcom SoC channel | Physical pin on header | | Example for pin 11 | `17` | `11` | | Portability | High (same across models) | Low (varies by model) | | Example code | `GPIO.setup(17, GPIO.OUT)` | `GPIO.setup(11, GPIO.OUT)` | — **3 marks for table + 2 marks each for example declarations.** #### Part (b) — 7 marks **Pin Allocation (2 marks):** - **GPIO 17** → Green LED (motion detected) - **GPIO 27** → Red LED (idle) - **GPIO 22** → PIR motion sensor INPUT **Circuit (1 mark):** Each LED has its own 220 Ω series resistor; cathodes to GND. PIR VCC to 5 V, GND to GND, OUT to GPIO 22. **Python Program using `gpiozero` (4 marks):** ```python from gpiozero import LED, MotionSensor from signal import pause green_led = LED(17) # 1 mark red_led = LED(27) # 1 mark pir = MotionSensor(22) # 1 mark pir.when_motion = green_led.on pir.when_motion = red_led.off pir.when_no_motion = green_led.off pir.when_no_motion = red_led.on pause() # 1 mark: keep program alive ``` --- ## 5.4 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing BCM and BOARD numbers** — Writing `GPIO.setup(11, ...)` with `GPIO.BCM` will silently fail. Always state **both** the numbering mode and the pin number clearly. **(−2 marks)** > 2. **Forgetting `GPIO.cleanup()`** — If a program exits abnormally, the pin stays in its last state. The `finally:` block with `GPIO.cleanup()` is mandatory for **safe resource release**. **(−1 mark)** > 3. **Omitting the current-limiting resistor in the diagram** — Drawing an LED directly between the GPIO pin and GND is a **fatal design error** and will cost **at least 2–3 marks** in circuit-design questions. > 4. **Applying 5 V to a 3.3 V GPIO** — In cross-platform questions (Arduino ↔ RPi), students often forget that the RPi is NOT 5 V tolerant. **(−2 marks)** > 5. **Unit errors in Ohm's Law** — Mixing mA and A in the resistor formula is a frequent arithmetic slip. Always convert mA → A before substituting. **(−1 mark)** > 6. **Not using `try/except/finally`** — Bare scripts that ignore `KeyboardInterrupt` lose marks for poor defensive programming. **(−1 mark)** --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 4** - [x] **Raspberry Pi** is a credit-card sized SBC that acts as an **edge-computing node** in IoT. - [x] **GPIO** pins operate at **3.3 V** logic; absolute maximum per-pin current is **~16 mA**. - [x] **Two numbering modes:** `GPIO.BCM` (channel number) and `GPIO.BOARD` (physical pin position). - [x] **Current-limiting resistor formula:** $R_S = (V_{CC} - V_f) / I_f$. - [x] For a red LED from 3.3 V: theoretical $R_S = 65 \, \Omega$, **practical choice = 220 Ω** (standard E12). - [x] **LED anode** → resistor → GPIO pin; **LED cathode** → GND. - [x] Python libraries: **`RPi.GPIO`** (classic, verbose) and **`gpiozero`** (modern, high-level). - [x] Always end programs with **`GPIO.cleanup()`** to release pin resources. - [x] **Never** apply **5 V** to a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin — it is destructive. - [x] Common physical pin **11** = **BCM GPIO 17** = the de-facto "first LED" pin in textbooks. - [x] The blink program is the **physical-computing equivalent of "Hello, World!"** — be ready to write it from memory. - [x] KTU expects **both** the circuit diagram and the Python code in any 7-or-14-mark question on this topic.

topics.title: Interfacing an LED and Switch with Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST834 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interfacing an LED and Switch with Raspberry Pi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Raspberry Pi** is a series of small, single-board computers (SBCs) developed by the Raspberry Pi Foundation in the United Kingdom, based on Broadcom System-on-Chip (SoC) architecture. It runs a Linux-based operating system (typically Raspberry Pi OS) and exposes a **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** header, which serves as the physical interface for connecting external electronic components such as LEDs, switches, sensors, and actuators.

**Interfacing** refers to the hardware and software methodology of connecting and establishing bidirectional communication between the Raspberry Pi and external peripheral devices. The process involves three core layers:

1. **Hardware Layer** — Physical wiring of components (LED, switch, resistors) to GPIO pins.
2. **Electrical Layer** — Compliance with the **3.3 V DC** logic level standard of the Raspberry Pi.
3. **Software Layer** — Python programming using libraries such as `RPi.GPIO` or `gpiozero` to control pin states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The official 2024 scheme places significant emphasis on GPIO-based physical computing as the foundation for IoT edge-node development. Students are expected to demonstrate competency in **both circuit design (with proper current-limiting calculations)** and **Python script execution**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Body Model"

To understand the architecture intuitively, imagine the **Raspberry Pi as a human body**:

| Raspberry Pi Component | Human Body Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| CPU / SoC | Brain | Processes information, makes decisions |
| GPIO Pins | Hands & Skin | Reaches out to touch/interact with the external world |
| LED (Output) | Mouth / Indicator Light | Communicates status back to the user |
| Switch (Input) | Ear / Pressure Sensor | Receives signals/events from the external environment |
| Python Script | Nervous System | Carries instructions between the brain and the limbs |
| 3.3 V Rail | Heart (Power Pump) | Supplies regulated energy to all organs |

When you **press a switch**, it is analogous to the human skin sensing a touch — the brain (Raspberry Pi CPU) processes this event through the nervous system (Python code) and decides to activate the mouth (LED), which glows as a response.

---

## 1.3 Core Hardware Components in Focus

> [!NOTE]
> **GPIO Pin Definition (Strict KTU Terminology):** A GPIO pin is a generic pin on an integrated circuit whose behavior — including whether it is an input or output — is controllable by the user at runtime. On the Raspberry Pi 40-pin header, pins are categorized into:
> - **Power Pins:** 5 V (Pins 2, 4), 3.3 V (Pins 1, 17), Ground (Pins 6, 9, 14, 20, 25, 30, 34, 39)
> - **GPIO Data Pins:** 26 programmable pins (GPIO2–GPIO27)
> - **Special Function Pins:** I2C (SCL/SDA), SPI (MOSI/MISO/SCLK/CE), UART (TX/RX)

### 1.3.1 The LED (Light Emitting Diode)

An **LED** is a semiconductor diode that emits light when forward-biased. Key electrical characteristics:

- **Forward Voltage (V<sub>f</sub>):** Typically **1.8 V to 3.2 V** depending on color (Red ≈ 1.8 V, Green ≈ 2.0 V, Blue/White ≈ 3.0–3.2 V).
- **Forward Current (I<sub>f</sub>):** Typically **5 mA to 20 mA** for standard indicator LEDs.
- **Polarity:** Has an **Anode (+)** and **Cathode (−)**. The longer lead indicates the anode.

### 1.3.2 The Push-Button Switch

A **tactile push-button switch** is a momentary mechanical switch that completes a circuit only while pressed. It is a **passive input device** that requires external pull-up or pull-down resistors to define a stable logic state when open.

### 1.3.3 Resistors — The Silent Protectors

Resistors are used for two critical functions in GPIO interfacing:

1. **Current-Limiting Resistor (for LED):** Prevents excessive current from destroying the LED or the Raspberry Pi GPIO pin.
2. **Pull-up / Pull-Down Resistor (for Switch):** Ensures a defined logic HIGH or LOW state when the switch is open (floating).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Safety Constant:** The Raspberry Pi GPIO pins operate at **3.3 V** logic and can safely source/sink a maximum of **16 mA per pin** (with a total board limit of **50 mA across all GPIO pins**). Exceeding this will permanently damage the SoC. **Never connect an LED directly without a current-limiting resistor.**

---

## 1.4 Two Numbering Conventions — The Most Common Student Confusion

| Convention | Description | Example Pin |
|---|---|---|
| **BOARD** | Physical pin position on the 40-pin header | Pin 11 |
| **BCM** (Broadcom) | Internal Broadcom SoC channel number | GPIO17 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Mixing BOARD and BCM numbering in the same Python script will cause incorrect pin behavior. Always declare the mode **once** at the beginning of the script using `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` or `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BOARD)`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — LED Interfacing

### Step-by-Step Theory

1. **Identify the GPIO Pin:** Choose a programmable GPIO pin (e.g., GPIO17 / Pin 11).
2. **Set Pin as OUTPUT:** Configure the pin direction in software using `GPIO.setup(pin, GPIO.OUT)`.
3. **Connect LED with Resistor:** Place a current-limiting resistor in **series** with the LED between the GPIO pin and Ground (or between 3.3 V and the GPIO pin — but the former is the standard configuration).
4. **Apply Logic HIGH:** Writing `GPIO.output(pin, GPIO.HIGH)` drives the pin to **3.3 V**, forward-biasing the LED and completing the circuit through the resistor to ground.
5. **Apply Logic LOW:** Writing `GPIO.output(pin, GPIO.LOW)` drives the pin to **0 V**, removing the potential difference, and the LED turns off.

**The "Why" Behind the Resistor:**
Ohm's Law governs the current through the LED branch. Without a resistor, the LED would draw excessive current (limited only by its own internal resistance, which is very low when forward-biased), causing thermal runaway and permanent damage.

### Derivation of Current-Limiting Resistor Value

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the LED-resistor loop:

$$V_{RPi} = V_R + V_{LED}$$

$$V_R = V_{RPi} - V_{LED}$$

By Ohm's Law:

$$R = \frac{V_R}{I_f} = \frac{V_{RPi} - V_{LED}}{I_f}$$

**Standard Design Parameters (per Raspberry Pi official guidelines):**
- V<sub>RPi</sub> = 3.3 V
- V<sub>LED</sub> = 1.8 V (assume Red LED)
- I<sub>f</sub> = 8 mA (safe operating current)

$$R = \frac{3.3 - 1.8}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.5}{0.008} = 187.5 \, \Omega$$

The nearest standard E12 series resistor value is **220 Ω**, which is the most commonly used value in KTU lab kits.

---

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Switch Interfacing

### Step-by-Step Theory

1. **Identify the GPIO Pin:** Choose a different GPIO pin (e.g., GPIO27 / Pin 13).
2. **Set Pin as INPUT:** Configure the pin direction using `GPIO.setup(pin, GPIO.IN)`.
3. **Connect the Switch with a Pull-Up/Pull-Down Resistor:**
   - **Pull-Up Configuration:** Resistor (typically **10 kΩ**) connects the GPIO pin to 3.3 V. The switch connects the GPIO pin to Ground. When the switch is **open**, the pin reads **HIGH**. When **closed**, the pin reads **LOW** (active-low logic).
   - **Pull-Down Configuration:** Resistor (typically **10 kΩ**) connects the GPIO pin to Ground. The switch connects the GPIO pin to 3.3 V. When **open**, the pin reads **LOW**. When **closed**, the pin reads **HIGH** (active-high logic).
4. **Read Pin State:** Use `GPIO.input(pin)` to read the current state — returns `GPIO.HIGH` (True / 1) or `GPIO.LOW` (False / 0).
5. **Debouncing:** Mechanical switches exhibit "bounce" — rapid contact fluctuations for ~5–50 ms. Software debouncing (time delay) or hardware debouncing (RC filter) is essential.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Pull-Up/Pull-Down:** The Raspberry Pi SoC has built-in software-configurable pull-up and pull-down resistors (typically 50 kΩ–100 kΩ). These can be enabled in Python using:
> `GPIO.setup(pin, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)`
> This eliminates the need for an external 10 kΩ resistor in simple lab circuits.

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Pull Resistors

Without a pull resistor, when the switch is **open**, the GPIO pin is in a **floating state** — it is not connected to any defined voltage reference. The pin would randomly read HIGH or LOW due to electromagnetic noise (a phenomenon called "antenna effect"). A pull resistor anchors the pin to a known, stable logic level.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Current-Limiting Resistor | $R = \frac{V_{RPi} - V_f}{I_f}$ | 220 Ω (Red LED) | Ohms (Ω) | Protects LED and GPIO pin from overcurrent |
| Ohm's Law (Basic) | $V = I \times R$ | — | V, A, Ω | Fundamental circuit analysis |
| Power Dissipation (Resistor) | $P = I^2 \times R$ | $\leq$ 0.25 W (standard) | Watts (W) | Resistor power rating selection |
| Pull-up/Pull-down Resistor | $R_{pull}$ | 10 kΩ (external) / 50–100 kΩ (internal) | Ohms (Ω) | Defines stable logic level for floating inputs |
| GPIO Logic HIGH Voltage | $V_{IH}$ | 3.3 V | Volts (V) | Input high threshold |
| GPIO Logic LOW Voltage | $V_{IL}$ | 0 V | Volts (V) | Input low threshold |
| Max GPIO Current per pin | $I_{max}$ | 16 mA | Milliamps (mA) | Safety limit per pin |
| Max Total GPIO Current | $I_{total}$ | 50 mA | Milliamps (mA) | Safety limit across all pins |
| Switch Debounce Time | $t_{db}$ | 50–200 ms | Milliseconds (ms) | Eliminates mechanical contact bounce |
| LED Forward Voltage (Red) | $V_f$ | 1.8 V | Volts (V) | Standard Red indicator LED |
| LED Forward Voltage (Green) | $V_f$ | 2.0 V | Volts (V) | Standard Green indicator LED |
| LED Forward Current (typical) | $I_f$ | 5–20 mA | Milliamps (mA) | Operating current for brightness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This interface architecture is the **atomic building block of every IoT edge device**. Smart home systems, industrial PLCs, weather stations, and wearable health monitors all rely on this exact pattern — a microcontroller reading a digital input (switch/sensor) and driving a digital output (LED/relay/actuator) under software control.

---

## 2.5 Python Programming Layer — Library Selection

The KTU 2024 syllabus references two primary libraries:

| Library | Import Statement | Complexity | Recommended For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RPi.GPIO** | `import RPi.GPIO as GPIO` | Low-level, more control | Advanced lab exercises, KTU exam questions |
| **gpiozero** | `from gpiozero import LED, Button` | High-level, abstracted | Beginners, rapid prototyping |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU examinations, the **`RPi.GPIO` library** is the de facto standard expected in model answers because it explicitly demonstrates understanding of pin modes, edge detection, and pull-up configuration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Current-Limiting Resistor Design

### Problem Statement
A Red LED (V<sub>f</sub> = 1.8 V, I<sub>f</sub> = 10 mA) is to be interfaced with Raspberry Pi GPIO Pin 11 (3.3 V logic). Calculate the required current-limiting resistor, the power dissipated by it, and verify it is within the GPIO current limit.

### Step 1: Identify Known Parameters
- Raspberry Pi GPIO output voltage: V<sub>RPi</sub> = **3.3 V**
- LED forward voltage (Red): V<sub>LED</sub> = **1.8 V**
- Desired LED forward current: I<sub>f</sub> = **10 mA** = **0.010 A**

### Step 2: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)
The sum of voltage drops around a closed loop must equal zero. The loop consists of the GPIO pin → resistor → LED → ground.

$$V_{RPi} - V_R - V_{LED} = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve for V<sub>R</sub> (Voltage across resistor)
$$V_R = V_{RPi} - V_{LED}$$

$$V_R = 3.3 \, V - 1.8 \, V = 1.5 \, V$$

### Step 4: Apply Ohm's Law to find R
$$R = \frac{V_R}{I_f}$$

$$R = \frac{1.5 \, V}{0.010 \, A} = 150 \, \Omega$$

### Step 5: Select Standard Resistor Value
The calculated 150 Ω is not a standard E12 series value. We select the **next higher** standard value to ensure the current stays **at or below** the safe limit:

$$R_{standard} = 220 \, \Omega$$

### Step 6: Recalculate Actual LED Current with 220 Ω
$$I_{actual} = \frac{V_R}{R_{standard}} = \frac{1.5}{220} = 0.00682 \, A = 6.82 \, mA$$

### Step 7: Verify GPIO Current Safety
$$I_{actual} = 6.82 \, mA < I_{max} = 16 \, mA \quad \checkmark \, \text{SAFE}$$

### Step 8: Calculate Power Dissipation in Resistor
$$P_R = I_{actual}^2 \times R_{standard}$$

$$P_R = (0.00682)^2 \times 220 = 0.0000465 \times 220 = 0.01024 \, W$$

$$P_R \approx 10.24 \, mW$$

### Step 9: Verify Resistor Power Rating
A standard quarter-watt (1/4 W = 250 mW) resistor is more than sufficient:

$$P_R = 10.24 \, mW < 250 \, mW \quad \checkmark \, \text{SAFE}$$

### Final Design Summary

| Parameter | Calculated Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Current-Limiting Resistor | 220 | Ω |
| Actual LED Current | 6.82 | mA |
| Resistor Power Dissipation | 10.24 | mW |
| Resistor Power Rating Required | 250 (1/4 W standard) | mW |
| Safety Margin | 220/150 = 1.47× | — |

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Switch Pull-Up Resistor Analysis

### Problem Statement
A tactile push-button switch is connected to GPIO27 (Pin 13) with an external 10 kΩ pull-up resistor to 3.3 V. The switch connects the GPIO pin to ground when pressed. Determine the voltage at the GPIO pin when the switch is (a) open and (b) closed.

### Case (a): Switch OPEN
When the switch is open, **no current flows** through the 10 kΩ resistor (because the GPIO pin is configured as a high-impedance input). Therefore, there is no voltage drop across the resistor.

$$V_{GPIO} = V_{RPi} - (I \times R) = 3.3 - (0 \times 10000) = 3.3 \, V$$

**Logic State: HIGH** ✓

### Case (b): Switch CLOSED
When the switch is closed, the GPIO pin is shorted to ground. The current flows from 3.3 V → 10 kΩ resistor → ground.

$$V_{GPIO} = 0 \, V$$

**Logic State: LOW** ✓

### Why 10 kΩ? — The Engineering Trade-off

A pull resistor value is chosen based on two competing constraints:

| Constraint | Lower R Value | Higher R Value |
|---|---|---|
| Noise Immunity | **Better** (lower impedance) | **Worse** (higher impedance picks up noise) |
| Power Consumption | **Worse** (more current wasted) | **Better** (minimal current draw) |
| Switching Speed | **Better** (faster transitions) | **Worse** (parasitic capacitance slows transitions) |

For a typical 3.3 V logic circuit, **10 kΩ** represents the optimal trade-off — it provides strong noise immunity while consuming only:

$$I_{waste} = \frac{3.3 \, V}{10000 \, \Omega} = 0.33 \, mA \quad \text{(negligible)}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Complete Interfacing Logic Table

| Component | Pin Configuration | Active State | Inactive State | Resistor Used | Polarity Sensitive? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LED (Output) | `GPIO.OUT` | GPIO.HIGH (3.3 V) → LED ON | GPIO.LOW (0 V) → LED OFF | 220 Ω (current-limit) | **Yes** (Anode → GPIO, Cathode → GND) |
| Switch (Input, Pull-Up) | `GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP` | Pressed → GPIO.LOW | Released → GPIO.HIGH | 10 kΩ (internal or external) | No |
| Switch (Input, Pull-Down) | `GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_DOWN` | Pressed → GPIO.HIGH | Released → GPIO.LOW | 10 kΩ (internal or external) | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Hardware Interfacing — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PS["POWER SUPPLY UNIT"]
        pwr3v3["3.3V REGULATED RAIL"]
        pwrGND["COMMON GROUND"]
    end

    subgraph RPI["RASPBERRY PI GPIO HEADER"]
        gpio17["GPIO17 - PIN 11 OUTPUT"]
        gpio27["GPIO27 - PIN 13 INPUT"]
        internalPull["INTERNAL PULL UP RESISTOR 50K OHM"]
    end

    subgraph LEDCKT["LED INTERFACE CIRCUIT"]
        ledResistor["220 OHM RESISTOR"]
        ledAnode["LED ANODE POSITIVE"]
        ledCathode["LED CATHODE NEGATIVE"]
        lightEmit["VISIBLE LIGHT OUTPUT"]
    end

    subgraph SWCKT["SWITCH INTERFACE CIRCUIT"]
        swResistor["EXTERNAL 10K OHM PULL UP"]
        btnSwitch["TACTILE PUSH BUTTON"]
        btnContact["MECHANICAL CONTACT"]
    end

    subgraph PYENV["PYTHON SOFTWARE LAYER"]
        pyImport["IMPORT RPi.GPIO AS GPIO"]
        pySetup["GPIO.SETUP LED OUT SWITCH IN"]
        pyLogic["IF SWITCH PRESSED THEN LED ON ELSE LED OFF"]
        pyCleanup["GPIO.CLEANUP ON EXIT"]
    end

    gpio17 -->|Drives 3.3V| ledResistor
    ledResistor -->|Limits current| ledAnode
    ledAnode -->|Forward biased| ledCathode
    ledCathode -->|Returns to| pwrGND
    ledCathode -.->|Emits| lightEmit

    pwr3v3 -->|Pull up voltage| swResistor
    swResistor -->|Defines logic level| gpio27
    btnSwitch -->|Shorts to ground when pressed| pwrGND
    gpio27 -.->|Reads state| btnContact

    pyImport --> pySetup
    pySetup --> pyLogic
    pyLogic -.->|Controls| gpio17
    pyLogic -.->|Reads from| gpio27
    pyLogic --> pyCleanup
```

---

## 4.2 Logical Control Flow — LED and Switch Combined Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([Program Start]) --> importLib["Import RPi.GPIO Library"]
    importLib --> setMode["Set Mode GPIO.BCM"]
    setMode --> setWarnings["Disable Warnings GPIO.setwarnings False"]
    setWarnings --> configLED["Setup GPIO17 as OUTPUT"]
    configLED --> configSW["Setup GPIO27 as INPUT with PUD_UP"]
    configSW --> tryBlock["Begin Try Block"]

    tryBlock --> readSW{GPIO27 State}
    readSW -->|GPIO.LOW Pressed| ledON["GPIO17 HIGH - LED Glows"]
    readSW -->|GPIO.HIGH Released| ledOFF["GPIO17 LOW - LED Off"]

    ledON --> delay1["time.sleep 0.2 sec"]
    ledOFF --> delay1
    delay1 --> readSW

    readSW -.->|KeyboardInterrupt| exceptBlock["Except Block Triggered"]
    exceptBlock --> cleanup["GPIO.cleanup - Releases Pins"]
    cleanup --> stop([Program End])
```

---

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit — LED Branch Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["GPIO17 OUTPUT 3.3V"] --> B["RESISTOR 220 OHM"]
    B --> C["LED ANODE"]
    C --> D["LED CATHODE"]
    D --> E["GROUND 0V"]

    F["CURRENT FLOW I = 6.82 mA"] -.-> A
    G["VOLTAGE DROP V = 1.5V across R"] -.-> B
    H["FORWARD VOLTAGE Vf = 1.8V"] -.-> C
```

---

## 4.4 Equivalent Circuit — Switch Branch with Pull-Up

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OPEN_STATE["SWITCH OPEN - RELEASED"]
        v1["3.3V SUPPLY"] --> r1["10K OHM PULL UP"]
        r1 --> node1["GPIO27 INPUT"]
        sw1["SWITCH OPEN"] -.->|No path| gnd1["GROUND"]
        node1 --> read1["V = 3.3V - LOGIC HIGH"]
    end

    subgraph CLOSED_STATE["SWITCH CLOSED - PRESSED"]
        v2["3.3V SUPPLY"] --> r2["10K OHM PULL UP"]
        r2 --> node2["GPIO27 INPUT"]
        sw2["SWITCH CLOSED"] --> gnd2["GROUND"]
        node2 --> read2["V = 0V - LOGIC LOW"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Explain the difference between BOARD numbering and BCM numbering in Raspberry Pi GPIO. Which is preferred for production code and why?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | BOARD Numbering | BCM Numbering |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Physical pin position on the 40-pin header | Broadcom SoC internal channel number |
| Example | Pin 11 | GPIO17 |
| Hardware Dependency | Tied to the specific board revision | Tied to the SoC chip |

**Preferred for Production:** **BCM numbering** is preferred because it is **hardware-revision independent**. The same BCM pin number refers to the same internal SoC channel across all Raspberry Pi models, whereas BOARD pin numbers may shift between revisions (e.g., the original 26-pin header vs. the current 40-pin header). Additionally, BCM numbering allows the use of higher-level libraries like `gpiozero` and `pigpio` that require the BCM convention. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Exam]`

**Why is a current-limiting resistor mandatory when interfacing an LED with a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin? What is the consequence of omitting it?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A current-limiting resistor is mandatory because the Raspberry Pi GPIO pin provides a **3.3 V** logic level, which exceeds the LED's forward voltage (typically 1.8–3.2 V). Without a resistor, the LED would draw excessive current limited only by its own internal resistance.

**Consequences of omission:**
1. **LED Damage** — Thermal runaway due to uncontrolled current destroys the semiconductor junction.
2. **GPIO Pin Damage** — The Raspberry Pi SoC has a per-pin limit of **16 mA**. Exceeding this permanently damages the pin or the entire SoC.
3. **Unstable Operation** — The voltage drop across the LED is unpredictable without current regulation.

The standard resistor value is **220 Ω** for a Red LED at 3.3 V, calculated using $R = \frac{V_{RPi} - V_f}{I_f}$. **(3 Marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram to interface an LED with Raspberry Pi GPIO Pin 11 (BCM GPIO17) using a 220 Ω current-limiting resistor. Write a complete Python program using the `RPi.GPIO` library to blink the LED at 1-second intervals (1 second ON, 1 second OFF). Explain each line of the code. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of pull-up and pull-down resistors with respect to switch interfacing. Why is a floating input dangerous? Write the Python code to read a switch connected to GPIO27 with an internal pull-up resistor and print "PRESSED" or "RELEASED" on the console. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Circuit Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
GPIO17 (Pin 11) ----[ 220 Ω ]---->| (LED Anode to Cathode) ---- GND (Pin 6)
```

**Python Program:**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

LED_PIN = 17

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setwarnings(False)
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)

try:
    while True:
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)
        print("LED ON")
        time.sleep(1)
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)
        print("LED OFF")
        time.sleep(1)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

**Line-by-Line Explanation:**

| Line | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `import RPi.GPIO as GPIO` | Imports the GPIO library with alias `GPIO` |
| `import time` | Imports the time module for `sleep()` delays |
| `LED_PIN = 17` | Defines BCM pin number as a constant for readability |
| `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` | Selects BCM (Broadcom) numbering convention |
| `GPIO.setwarnings(False)` | Suppresses pin-already-in-use warnings |
| `GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)` | Configures GPIO17 as an output pin |
| `while True:` | Creates an infinite loop for continuous blinking |
| `GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)` | Drives the pin to 3.3 V → LED turns ON |
| `time.sleep(1)` | Pauses execution for 1 second |
| `GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)` | Drives the pin to 0 V → LED turns OFF |
| `except KeyboardInterrupt` | Gracefully handles Ctrl+C termination |
| `finally: GPIO.cleanup()` | Resets all GPIO pins to a safe state |

**(7 Marks — 3 for circuit, 2 for code, 2 for explanation)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Pull-Up Resistor:** A resistor connected between the GPIO pin and the supply voltage (3.3 V). It ensures the pin reads **HIGH** when the switch is open. When the switch closes (connects to ground), the pin reads **LOW**.

**Pull-Down Resistor:** A resistor connected between the GPIO pin and ground. It ensures the pin reads **LOW** when the switch is open. When the switch closes (connects to 3.3 V), the pin reads **HIGH**.

**Floating Input Danger:** When no pull resistor is used, the open switch leaves the GPIO pin electrically disconnected — a "floating" state. The pin acts as a small antenna and randomly picks up electromagnetic noise, causing unpredictable toggling between HIGH and LOW states, leading to false triggers and erratic program behavior.

**Python Code:**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

SW_PIN = 27

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setwarnings(False)
GPIO.setup(SW_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)

try:
    while True:
        if GPIO.input(SW_PIN) == GPIO.LOW:
            print("PRESSED")
        else:
            print("RELEASED")
        time.sleep(0.2)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

**Explanation:** The internal pull-up (`GPIO.PUD_UP`) makes the pin read HIGH when the switch is open. Pressing the switch connects the pin to ground, making it read LOW (active-low logic). The 200 ms delay provides basic software debouncing to filter mechanical contact bounce.

**(7 Marks — 2 for theory, 3 for code, 2 for explanation)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Design a complete LED and switch interfacing circuit with Raspberry Pi where pressing the switch toggles the LED state (ON → OFF → ON). Provide the circuit description, Python code, and explain debouncing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the exact current-limiting resistor value for a Blue LED (V<sub>f</sub> = 3.0 V, desired I<sub>f</sub> = 12 mA) interfaced with a 3.3 V Raspberry Pi GPIO pin. Show all derivation steps. What is the power dissipated by the resistor, and is a 1/8 W resistor sufficient? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Circuit Description:**

- **LED:** Connected between GPIO17 (Pin 11) and GND through a 220 Ω current-limiting resistor.
- **Switch:** Connected between GPIO27 (Pin 13) and GND, with an internal pull-up resistor enabled in software.
- **Logic:** The program reads the switch state. On each detected press (with debounce), the LED state is toggled using a software flag variable.

**Python Code:**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

LED_PIN = 17
SW_PIN = 27
DEBOUNCE_MS = 300

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setwarnings(False)
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)
GPIO.setup(SW_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)

led_state = False
last_press_time = 0

try:
    while True:
        if GPIO.input(SW_PIN) == GPIO.LOW:
            current_time = time.time() * 1000
            if (current_time - last_press_time) > DEBOUNCE_MS:
                led_state = not led_state
                GPIO.output(LED_PIN, led_state)
                last_press_time = current_time
                print("LED State Toggled to:", "ON" if led_state else "OFF")
        time.sleep(0.05)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

**Debouncing Explanation:** Mechanical switches exhibit "contact bounce" — when pressed, the metal contacts physically vibrate and open/close rapidly for 5–50 ms before settling. Without debouncing, the program would interpret a single press as multiple presses, causing erratic LED toggling. The `DEBOUNCE_MS = 300` variable ensures that only one state change is registered per 300 ms window, effectively filtering out bounce artifacts.

**(7 Marks — 2 for circuit, 3 for code, 2 for debounce explanation)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Step 1: Known Parameters**
- V<sub>RPi</sub> = 3.3 V
- V<sub>LED</sub> (Blue) = 3.0 V
- I<sub>f</sub> (desired) = 12 mA = 0.012 A

**Step 2: Apply KVL**

$$V_R = V_{RPi} - V_{LED} = 3.3 - 3.0 = 0.3 \, V$$

**Step 3: Calculate R**

$$R = \frac{V_R}{I_f} = \frac{0.3}{0.012} = 25 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4: Select Standard Resistor**
Nearest standard E12 value (next higher) = **27 Ω**

**Step 5: Recalculate Actual Current**

$$I_{actual} = \frac{0.3}{27} = 0.01111 \, A = 11.11 \, mA$$

**Step 6: Verify GPIO Safety**

$$I_{actual} = 11.11 \, mA < 16 \, mA \quad \checkmark \, \text{SAFE}$$

**Step 7: Power Dissipation**

$$P_R = I_{actual}^2 \times R = (0.01111)^2 \times 27 = 0.0001234 \times 27 = 0.00333 \, W = 3.33 \, mW$$

**Step 8: Check Resistor Rating**
1/8 W = 125 mW. Since 3.33 mW < 125 mW:

$$3.33 \, mW \ll 125 \, mW \quad \checkmark \, \text{1/8 W RESISTOR IS MORE THAN SUFFICIENT}$$

**(7 Marks — 2 for setup, 2 for R calculation, 2 for power, 1 for safety verification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `GPIO.cleanup()`** in the `finally` block — results in "pin busy" errors on re-execution. **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Not disabling warnings** with `GPIO.setwarnings(False)` — noisy output is penalized. **(-1 mark)**
> 3. **Mixing BOARD and BCM numbering** in the same script — immediate logic failure. **(-2 marks)**
> 4. **Omitting the current-limiting resistor** in the circuit diagram — shows fundamental misunderstanding. **(-3 marks)**
> 5. **Not including `time.sleep()` for debouncing** in switch-reading code — demonstrates incomplete practical knowledge. **(-2 marks)**
> 6. **Incorrect V<sub>f</sub> value** for LED color in numerical problems — use Red=1.8V, Green=2.0V, Blue=3.0V. **(-1 mark)**
> 7. **Using 5V pin** instead of 3.3V GPIO pin in derivations — 5V will damage the Pi. **(-2 marks)**

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Concepts to Memorize for KTU Exam:**

- **Raspberry Pi GPIO operates at 3.3 V logic — NEVER 5 V on data pins.**
- **Maximum current per GPIO pin = 16 mA; total board limit = 50 mA.**
- **Current-Limiting Resistor Formula:** $R = \frac{V_{RPi} - V_f}{I_f}$
- **Standard resistor for Red LED at 3.3 V = 220 Ω** (most common KTU lab value).
- **Standard pull-up/pull-down resistor = 10 kΩ** (external) or 50–100 kΩ (internal, software-enabled).
- **BCM numbering is preferred** over BOARD numbering for production code.
- **Pull-Up Configuration:** Switch reads LOW when pressed (active-low).
- **Pull-Down Configuration:** Switch reads HIGH when pressed (active-high).
- **LED Polarity:** Longer lead = Anode (+), shorter lead = Cathode (−). Flat side on LED body = Cathode side.
- **GPIO Mode Declaration:** Always call `GPIO.setmode()` **once** before any `GPIO.setup()`.
- **Cleanup is mandatory:** `GPIO.cleanup()` resets pins to input mode (safe state).
- **Debounce time:** 50–300 ms is typical for software debouncing of tactile switches.
- **LED Forward Voltages (memorize):** Red = 1.8 V, Yellow = 2.0 V, Green = 2.1 V, Blue/White = 3.0–3.2 V.
- **Library preference for KTU exams:** `RPi.GPIO` (low-level) is expected in model answers.
- **Always wrap GPIO code in `try...except KeyboardInterrupt...finally`** for graceful termination.
- **Floating inputs cause erratic behavior** — always use pull resistors (internal or external).
- **GPIO.setup() parameters:** `GPIO.IN`/`GPIO.OUT`, optional `pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP`/`GPIO.PUD_DOWN`.
- **GPIO.input() returns** `GPIO.HIGH` (True) or `GPIO.LOW` (False) — use `==` comparison.
- **PWM (bonus concept):** For LED brightness control, use `GPIO.PWM(pin, frequency)` with `start(duty_cycle)` and `ChangeDutyCycle()`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other IoT Devices - PcDino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST834 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Other IoT Devices - PcDino

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is PcDino?

**PcDino** refers to a family of low-cost, compact, microcontroller-based development boards and modules primarily manufactured by the Shenzhen-based electronics company PcDino (also stylized as PCDuino or PcDuino in various product lines). In the KTU 2024 syllabus context under "Other IoT Devices," PcDino represents an **ARM-based single-board computer (SBC) / IoT prototyping platform** that bridges the gap between simple microcontroller boards (like Arduino) and full-fledged single-board computers (like Raspberry Pi).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *PcDino is an open-source, Linux-compatible, ARM Cortex-A8/A9-based single-board IoT prototyping platform designed to integrate seamlessly with Arduino shields, GPIO pins, and IoT communication protocols (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee) for rapid edge-device development.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swiss Army Knife" of IoT Prototyping

Imagine you are building a smart home. You have:
- A **calculator** (Arduino) — can do simple tasks, but cannot run an Operating System.
- A **mini-laptop** (Raspberry Pi) — powerful, runs Linux, but expensive and overkill for tiny sensor nodes.
- A **multi-tool pocket knife** (PcDino) — combines the simplicity of Arduino's plug-and-play hardware with the brainpower of a Linux-running ARM processor. It is the **"just-right" device** for cost-sensitive, network-connected IoT nodes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike Arduino (microcontroller only, no OS), PcDino runs a full **Linux/Android OS** on an ARM processor, but unlike Raspberry Pi, it natively supports **Arduino-compatible headers**, making it a hybrid platform.

### 1.3 Standard Physical & Technical Metrics of PcDino Boards

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Processor (CPU)** | Allwinner A1X (ARM Cortex-A8 @ **1 GHz**) |
| **RAM** | **1 GB DDR3** |
| **Storage** | microSD (up to **32 GB**) + onboard NAND (optional **2 GB / 4 GB**) |
| **OS Support** | Ubuntu 12.04 / Debian / Android 4.x (ICS) |
| **GPIO Voltage** | **3.3 V** (logic level, Arduino shields via level shifters) |
| **Wi-Fi** | Optional onboard **802.11 b/g/n** module |
| **Power** | **5 V DC**, **2 A** (via barrel jack or micro-USB) |
| **Dimensions** | Approx. **125 mm × 52 mm** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why the 3.3 V matters in KTU exams:** Always remember that PcDino's GPIO logic level is **3.3 V**, NOT the 5 V used by older Arduino Uno boards. A common exam question asks about level shifting — remember this fact!
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Breakdown of PcDino

PcDino's architecture is a **System-on-Chip (SoC) design**, meaning the CPU, GPU, memory controller, and I/O peripherals are integrated into a single silicon die. Below is the layered logical architecture:

### Layer 1: Hardware Core (SoC Layer)
- **Allwinner A10/A20 SoC** contains:
  - **ARM Cortex-A8/A9 CPU Core** — executes OS instructions, runs Python, Node.js, C/C++ applications.
  - **Mali-400 GPU** — handles graphical rendering (HDMI output at 1080p).
  - **DDR Memory Controller** — interfaces with **DDR3 RAM**.
  - **ARM Cortex-M3 Coprocessor** — this is the *secret weapon*. It mimics an Arduino-compatible microcontroller for real-time GPIO operations.

### Layer 2: Peripheral Interface Layer
- **GPIO Bank**: 18-pin Arduino-compatible header (Digital + Analog + PWM + I2C + SPI + UART).
- **Connectivity**: USB 2.0 (Host + OTG), HDMI, Ethernet (10/100 Mbps), audio jack.
- **Storage Interface**: SATA (on some variants), microSD, NAND flash.

### Layer 3: Software/OS Layer
- **Bootloader** → **Linux Kernel** → **Filesystem (Ubuntu/Debian)** → **User Applications** (Python, C, Node-RED, MQTT clients).

## 2.2 The Hybrid CPU Concept (Why It Matters)

PcDino's most novel feature is its **dual-CPU architecture**:

1. **High-level CPU (ARM Cortex-A8)** — runs Linux, handles networking, web servers, cloud connectivity.
2. **Low-level MCU (ARM Cortex-M3)** — handles real-time sensor reading, PWM generation, ADC sampling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Punchline:** When a question asks *"How does PcDino differ from Raspberry Pi and Arduino combined?"*, the answer is: **PcDino integrates both a Linux-capable ARM host processor AND an Arduino-compatible microcontroller on the same board, eliminating the need for two separate boards.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Formula / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f_{CPU}$ | CPU Clock Frequency | $1.0$ | GHz |
| $V_{GPIO}$ | GPIO Logic Voltage | $3.3$ | V |
| $V_{IN(ADC)}$ | ADC Input Range | $0 \text{ to } 3.3$ | V |
| $N_{ADC}$ | ADC Resolution | $12$ | bits |
| $I_{MAX}$ | Max Current per GPIO | $40$ | mA |
| $I_{TOTAL}$ | Max Total Pin Current | $200$ | mA |
| $N_{steps}$ | ADC Quantization Steps | $2^{N_{ADC}} = 2^{12} = 4096$ | levels |
| $V_{LSB}$ | ADC Least Significant Bit | $\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{N_{ADC}}} = \dfrac{3.3}{4096}$ | V |
| $R_{WiFi}$ | Wi-Fi Range (indoor) | $\approx 30$ | m |
| $P_{TX}$ | Wi-Fi Transmit Power | $\approx 16$ | dBm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use the vertical pipe `|` inside table rows.** For absolute value notation, always use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` to avoid breaking the markdown table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PcDino boards are deployed in:
- **Smart Agriculture** — soil moisture + temperature sensing + cloud upload via Wi-Fi.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — machine vibration monitoring using the on-board ADC.
- **Edge AI Prototyping** — running lightweight TensorFlow Lite models at the edge.
- **Digital Signage** — HDMI output + cloud-driven content updates.
- **Educational Labs** — teaching Linux, embedded C, and IoT in a single board.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Digital Output for a Given Analog Input

PcDino's analog pins use a **12-bit Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC**. The relationship between the analog input voltage and the digital output code is a linear quantization function.

### Given:
- Reference Voltage: $V_{REF} = 3.3$ V
- ADC Resolution: $N_{ADC} = 12$ bits
- Analog Input Voltage: $V_{IN}$ (unknown, to be measured)

### Step 1 — Determine the Number of Quantization Levels

A 12-bit ADC can resolve $2^{N_{ADC}}$ discrete digital codes.

$$
N_{steps} = 2^{N_{ADC}}
$$

$$
N_{steps} = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

**Textual logic:** Each binary code from 0 to 4095 represents one unique voltage window.

### Step 2 — Determine the Voltage per Least Significant Bit (LSB)

The step-size (resolution) of the ADC is the reference voltage divided by the total number of steps.

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{N_{steps}}
$$

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{4096} \approx 8.06 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{LSB} \approx 0.806 \text{ mV}
$$

**Textual logic:** This means the smallest voltage change the ADC can detect is about 0.806 mV.

### Step 3 — Compute the Digital Output Code

The digital output $D$ for a given input $V_{IN}$ is given by:

$$
D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{IN}}{V_{LSB}}\right) = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{IN} \cdot N_{steps}}{V_{REF}}\right)
$$

**Textual logic:** We multiply the input by 4096 and divide by 3.3, then round to the nearest integer.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

Suppose an analog sensor outputs $V_{IN} = 1.65$ V (a mid-rail value).

$$
D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{1.65 \times 4096}{3.3}\right)
$$

$$
D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{6758.4}{3.3}\right)
$$

$$
D = \text{round}(2048)
$$

$$
D = 2048
$$

**Verification:** $D$ is exactly half of 4096, which makes sense because 1.65 V is exactly half of 3.3 V. ✓

### Step 5 — Inverse Conversion (Reconstructing Voltage from Digital Code)

If the ADC returns a digital code $D$, the original voltage is reconstructed as:

$$
V_{IN} = D \times V_{LSB} = D \times \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{N_{ADC}}}
$$

**Textual logic:** This is the inverse mapping — used by the software to convert raw ADC readings back into engineering units (e.g., temperature in °C).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: PcDino vs Other IoT Boards

| Feature | Arduino Uno | Raspberry Pi 4 | **PcDino** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU Type | 8-bit AVR | ARM Cortex-A72 (64-bit) | **ARM Cortex-A8 (32-bit)** |
| CPU Clock | 16 MHz | 1.5 GHz (quad-core) | **1 GHz (single-core)** |
| OS | None (bare-metal) | Linux (Raspbian) | **Linux / Android** |
| Arduino Shield Support | Native | None | **Native (via headers)** |
| Analog Inputs | 6 (10-bit) | None | **6 (12-bit)** |
| Real-time GPIO | Yes (deterministic) | No (Linux latency) | **Yes (via on-board MCU)** |
| Power Consumption | ~0.5 W | ~7.5 W | **~3.5 W** |
| Cost (approx.) | ₹400 | ₹4500 | **₹2500** |
| Best Use Case | Sensor reading | Cloud server / media | **Hybrid IoT prototyping** |

## 3.3 Power Budget Calculation

For an IoT node built on PcDino, total power consumption is the sum of the SoC, peripherals, and connected sensors.

$$
P_{total} = P_{SoC} + P_{WiFi} + P_{Sensors} + P_{Misc}
$$

Assuming typical values:
- $P_{SoC} = 2.5$ W (ARM core under load)
- $P_{WiFi} = 0.6$ W (during transmission)
- $P_{Sensors} = 0.3$ W (5 sensors × 60 mW each)
- $P_{Misc} = 0.1$ W (LEDs, voltage regulators)

$$
P_{total} = 2.5 + 0.6 + 0.3 + 0.1
$$

$$
P_{total} = 3.5 \text{ W}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** At 5 V supply, the current draw is:

$$
I_{total} = \frac{P_{total}}{V_{supply}} = \frac{3.5}{5} = 0.7 \text{ A}
$$

This validates the datasheet recommendation of a **2 A power adapter** (leaving headroom for inrush currents).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PcDino System Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **internal functional architecture** of the PcDino board — showing how the host CPU, microcontroller coprocessor, peripherals, and external interfaces interact.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["ARM Cortex A8 CPU 1GHz"] --> nodeB["DDR3 RAM 1GB"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Linux Kernel Ubuntu"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Mali 400 GPU HDMI Output"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["SATA and USB Host Controller"]
    nodeF["ARM Cortex M3 MCU"] --> nodeG["GPIO Bank 18 Pin"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["ADC 12 bit 6 Channel"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["PWM and Timer Module"]
    nodeA <--> nodeF
    nodeA --> nodeJ["WiFi 802.11 b/g/n"]
    nodeA --> nodeK["Ethernet 10/100 Mbps"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["IoT Cloud MQTT Broker"]
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeG --> nodeM["External Sensor Array"]
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN["Temperature Humidity Motion"]
    nodeL --> nodeO["Dashboard and Analytics"]
```

## 4.2 IoT Data Flow Sequence (Sensor-to-Cloud)

This sequence diagram shows how a temperature reading travels from a sensor through PcDino up to a cloud dashboard.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as Temp Sensor
    participant MCU as Cortex M3 MCU
    participant CPU as Cortex A8 Linux
    participant WiFi as WiFi Module
    participant Cloud as IoT Cloud
    participant User as Web Dashboard

    Sensor->>MCU: Analog voltage on ADC pin
    MCU->>MCU: Convert to 12 bit digital code
    MCU->>CPU: Send data over UART or shared RAM
    CPU->>CPU: Apply calibration formula in Python
    CPU->>WiFi: Encapsulate as MQTT JSON payload
    WiFi->>Cloud: Publish to topic home/livingroom/temp
    Cloud->>User: Push notification and update graph
    User->>Cloud: Request historical data
    Cloud-->>User: Return time series JSON
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: When to Use PcDino

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart["IoT Project Requirement"] --> nodeQ1{"Need Linux OS?"}
    nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeQ2{"Need Arduino shield support?"}
    nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeQ3["Use Arduino or ESP32"]
    nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeQ4["Use PcDino"]
    nodeQ2 -- No --> nodeQ5{"Need high compute 64 bit?"}
    nodeQ5 -- Yes --> nodeQ6["Use Raspberry Pi 4"]
    nodeQ5 -- No --> nodeQ4
    nodeQ4 --> nodeEnd["PcDino Selected for Hybrid IoT Node"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define PcDino and list its key features. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

PcDino is an open-source, ARM Cortex-A8-based single-board IoT prototyping platform that combines the processing power of a Linux computer with the hardware simplicity of an Arduino. **Key features (1.5 marks):**

1. **1 GHz ARM Cortex-A8 CPU** with **1 GB DDR3 RAM** running Linux/Ubuntu.
2. **Arduino-compatible GPIO header** (18 pins) for direct shield interfacing.
3. **12-bit ADC** with 6 analog input channels (range 0 to 3.3 V).
4. **On-board Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n)** and **10/100 Ethernet** for cloud connectivity.
5. **Hybrid dual-CPU design** — Linux host + real-time ARM Cortex-M3 coprocessor for deterministic GPIO control. **(1.5 marks for the dual-CPU explanation)**

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between PcDino and Raspberry Pi in terms of real-time I/O handling. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Raspberry Pi | **PcDino** |
|---|---|---|
| **Real-time I/O** | None — Linux scheduler causes **jitter of 1–10 ms** on GPIO toggles. | **On-board ARM Cortex-M3 MCU** provides **microsecond-level deterministic** GPIO control. **(1.5 marks)** |
