## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Data Analytics for IoT. List the 5 Vs of Big Data as applied to IoT.*

**Model Answer:**
Data Analytics for IoT is the process of examining raw sensor data streams to extract patterns, generate insights, and enable intelligent decision-making. The 5 Vs are:

1. **Volume** – Massive scale (zettabytes).
2. **Velocity** – Real-time arrival (milliseconds).
3. **Variety** – Multiple formats (time-series, image, video).
4. **Veracity** – Trustworthiness of noisy sensor data.
5. **Value** – Useful business or operational outcome derived.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Edge Analytics and Cloud Analytics in IoT.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Edge Analytics | Cloud Analytics |
|---|---|---|
| Location | On device / gateway | Remote data center |
| Latency | < 1 ms | > 100 ms |
| Bandwidth | Filters data, saves BW | Consumes full BW |
| Compute | Limited (MCU) | Massive (GPU) |
| Use case | Safety-critical control | Deep learning training |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the four types of IoT analytics — descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive — with one IoT example each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an IoT data analytics system. Explain the role of each layer. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The four analytics types are:

- **Descriptive** answers "What happened?" — *Example:* A dashboard showing hourly energy usage in a smart home.
- **Diagnostic** answers "Why did it happen?" — *Example:* Identifying that a motor overheated due to a clogged filter.
- **Predictive** answers "What will happen?" — *Example:* An LSTM model predicting bearing failure in 7 days.
- **Prescriptive** answers "What should we do?" — *Example:* Auto-ordering a replacement bearing and scheduling downtime.

**(b)** The five-layer architecture:

1. **Perception Layer** – Sensors/actuators collect physical data.
2. **Network Layer** – Gateways forward data via Wi-Fi/5G/LoRa.
3. **Edge/Fog Layer** – Performs local filtering and inference.
4. **Cloud Layer** – Stores data, runs heavy ML training, generates insights.
5. **Application Layer** – Dashboards, alerts, and control loops that actuate decisions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of Machine Learning in IoT data analytics. Compare supervised and unsupervised learning with an IoT example for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A vibration sensor in a factory machine produces the following 6 readings (in mm/s): **3.1, 3.3, 3.2, 3.4, 3.3, 5.8**. Compute the mean, standard deviation, and z-score of the last reading. Is the last reading an anomaly at 3σ? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Machine Learning enables IoT systems to learn from sensor data without explicit programming.

- **Supervised Learning** uses labeled data. *Example:* Training a classifier on labeled ECG beats (normal vs arrhythmic) to detect heart disease.
- **Unsupervised Learning** finds hidden patterns in unlabeled data. *Example:* Using k-Means clustering on unlabeled sensor data to discover unknown operating modes of a machine.

**(b)** **Step 1** — Mean:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{3.1 + 3.3 + 3.2 + 3.4 + 3.3 + 5.8}{6} = \frac{22.1}{6} \approx 3.683\,mm/s$$

**Step 2** — Squared deviations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(3.1 - 3.683)^2 &= 0.339 \\
(3.3 - 3.683)^2 &= 0.147 \\
(3.2 - 3.683)^2 &= 0.233 \\
(3.4 - 3.683)^2 &= 0.080 \\
(3.3 - 3.683)^2 &= 0.147 \\
(5.8 - 3.683)^2 &= 4.472
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Variance:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{0.339 + 0.147 + 0.233 + 0.080 + 0.147 + 4.472}{6} = \frac{5.418}{6} \approx 0.903$$

**Step 4** — Standard deviation:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.903} \approx 0.950\,mm/s$$

**Step 5** — z-score of the last reading (5.8):

$$z = \frac{5.8 - 3.683}{0.950} \approx 2.23$$

Since $\vert z \vert \approx 2.23 < 3$, **strictly not an anomaly at the 3σ level**, but it is borderline. A 2σ threshold ($\vert z \vert > 2$) would flag it as a warning — indicating possible early degradation of the machine.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "Big Data Analytics" with "IoT Analytics"** — Big Data is the *toolset*; IoT analytics is the *application* over streaming sensor data. Examiners deduct marks for using generic Hadoop examples without IoT context.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention latency budgets** — When justifying edge vs cloud, *always* state the latency requirement numerically.
> 3. **Pipes in tables** — Writing `|z| > 3` inside a markdown table breaks rendering. Use `\vert z \vert > 3`.
> 4. **Skipping units** in numerical problems — Every sensor reading must carry its unit (°C, mm/s, etc.) or marks are cut.
> 5. **Mixing up 3-sigma and 2-sigma** rules — State which one you are using and the corresponding probability (0.27% vs 4.5%).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Data Analytics for IoT** = transforming raw sensor streams into actionable insights.
- **Four Types**: Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive (memorize the *question* each answers).
- **5 Vs of Big Data**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
- **Three Analytics Locations**: Edge, Fog, Cloud — pick based on **latency budget**.
- **Streaming Analytics** for real-time alerts; **Batch Analytics** for historical reports.
- **z-score anomaly rule**: $\vert z \vert > 3$ ⇒ ~0.27% false positive rate.
- **Moving Average** reduces noise by $\sqrt{k}$ but introduces latency $\frac{k-1}{2}$.
- **Common ML models**: Random Forest (classification), LSTM (time series), k-Means (clustering).
- **Always state units** in any numerical answer.
- **Use `\vert` not `|`** for absolute value in LaTeX within markdown.
- **KTU favorite example**: Predictive maintenance in IIoT — know it cold.
- **Privacy + Bandwidth** are the strongest reasons to push analytics to the **edge**.
